title: Graph Your Way to Inspiration: Integrating Co-Author Graphs with Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Large Language Model Based Scientific Idea Generation
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) demonstrate potential in the field of scientific idea generation. However, the generated results often lack controllable academic context and traceable inspiration pathways. To bridge this gap, this paper proposes a scientific idea generation system called GYWI, which combines author knowledge graphs with retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) to form an external knowledge base to provide controllable context and trace of inspiration path for LLMs to generate new scientific ideas. We first propose an author-centered knowledge graph construction method and inspiration source sampling algorithms to construct external knowledge base. Then, we propose a hybrid retrieval mechanism that is composed of both RAG and GraphRAG to retrieve content with both depth and breadth knowledge. It forms a hybrid context. Thirdly, we propose a Prompt optimization strategy incorporating reinforcement learning principles to automatically guide LLMs optimizing the results based on the hybrid context. To evaluate the proposed approaches, we constructed an evaluation dataset based on arXiv (2018-2023). This paper also develops a comprehensive evaluation method including empirical automatic assessment in multiple-choice question task, LLM-based scoring, human evaluation, and semantic space visualization analysis. The generated ideas are evaluated from the following five dimensions: novelty, feasibility, clarity, relevance, and significance. We conducted experiments on different LLMs including GPT-4o, DeepSeek-V3, Qwen3-8B, and Gemini 2.5. Experimental results show that GYWI significantly outperforms mainstream LLMs in multiple metrics such as novelty, reliability, and relevance.

AI.tldr: GYWI integrates author knowledge graphs with hybrid retrieval (RAG + GraphRAG) and reinforcement-learning–guided prompts to enable controllable, traceable scientific idea generation; evaluated on arXiv data across multiple LLMs, showing improvements in novelty, reliability, and relevance.
AI.motivation: LLMs often lack controllable academic context and traceable inspiration pathways; external knowledge bases and provenance traces are needed to produce credible, transparent scientific ideas.
AI.method: 1) Construct an author-centered knowledge graph and inspiration-source sampling to form an external knowledge base; 2) Develop a hybrid retrieval mechanism combining RAG and GraphRAG to provide depth and breadth (hybrid context); 3) Propose a prompt-optimization strategy grounded in reinforcement learning to guide LLM outputs using the hybrid context; 4) Build an arXiv-based evaluation dataset (2018–2023) and a comprehensive evaluation suite (automatic MCQ, LLM-based scoring, human evaluation, semantic-space visualization); 5) Evaluate across multiple LLMs (e.g., GPT‑4o, DeepSeek-V3, Qwen3-8B, Gemini 2.5).
AI.result: GYWI significantly outperforms mainstream LLMs in novelty, reliability, and relevance across multiple metrics; the framework provides more controllable context and traceable inspiration pathways, validated by a robust evaluation on the arXiv-derived dataset.
AI.conclusion: The GYWI framework successfully integrates author knowledge graphs, hybrid retrieval, and reinforcement-learning–based prompt optimization to yield controllable, traceable scientific idea generation. It demonstrates improved quality of generated ideas across diverse LLMs and a large arXiv-based corpus, suggesting broad applicability.

title: Flip Distance of Triangulations of Convex Polygons / Rotation Distance of Binary Trees is NP-complete
summary:
Flips in triangulations of convex polygons arise in many different settings. They are isomorphic to rotations in binary trees, define edges in the 1-skeleton of the Associahedron and cover relations in the Tamari Lattice.
  The complexity of determining the minimum number of flips that transform one triangulation of a convex point set into another remained a tantalizing open question for many decades. We settle this question by proving that computing shortest flip sequences between triangulations of convex polygons, and therefore also computing the rotation distance of binary trees, is NP-hard.
  For our proof we develop techniques for flip sequences of triangulations whose counterparts were introduced for the study of flip sequences of non-crossing spanning trees by Bjerkevik, Kleist, Ueckerdt, and Vogtenhuber~[SODA25] and Bjerkevik, Dorfer, Kleist, Ueckerdt, and Vogtenhuber~[SoCG26].

AI.tldr: Proves NP-hardness of computing shortest flip sequences between triangulations of convex polygons, implying NP-hardness of binary-tree rotation distance; develops a framework for flip-sequences drawing on recent work on non-crossing spanning trees.
AI.motivation: Resolve a long-standing question about the complexity of transforming triangulations via flips and, equivalently, the rotation distance between binary trees; links combinatorial geometry, the Associahedron, and Tamari lattice.
AI.method: Introduces and adapts flip-sequence techniques; builds on recent SODA/SoCG work on flip sequences of non-crossing spanning trees to construct reductions that establish NP-hardness for triangulations of convex polygons.
AI.result: Demonstrates that computing the minimum number of flips between triangulations (and thus the rotation distance) is NP-hard.
AI.conclusion: Sets a definitive boundary on exact solvability for these problems and motivates search for approximation, restricted-case, or parameterized approaches; suggests the techniques may apply to related flip-structure problems.

title: FIRE: A Comprehensive Benchmark for Financial Intelligence and Reasoning Evaluation
summary: We introduce FIRE, a comprehensive benchmark designed to evaluate both the theoretical financial knowledge of LLMs and their ability to handle practical business scenarios. For theoretical assessment, we curate a diverse set of examination questions drawn from widely recognized financial qualification exams, enabling evaluation of LLMs deep understanding and application of financial knowledge. In addition, to assess the practical value of LLMs in real-world financial tasks, we propose a systematic evaluation matrix that categorizes complex financial domains and ensures coverage of essential subdomains and business activities. Based on this evaluation matrix, we collect 3,000 financial scenario questions, consisting of closed-form decision questions with reference answers and open-ended questions evaluated by predefined rubrics. We conduct comprehensive evaluations of state-of-the-art LLMs on the FIRE benchmark, including XuanYuan 4.0, our latest financial-domain model, as a strong in-domain baseline. These results enable a systematic analysis of the capability boundaries of current LLMs in financial applications. We publicly release the benchmark questions and evaluation code to facilitate future research.

AI.tldr: FIRE is a comprehensive benchmark for evaluating LLMs in finance theory and practical tasks, including 3,000 scenario questions; evaluates SOTA models such as XuanYuan 4.0; and publicly releases questions and code.
AI.motivation: There is a need for standardized, comprehensive benchmarks that assess both theoretical financial knowledge and practical decision-making in LLMs, covering diverse subdomains and real-world tasks to enable fair comparisons and track progress.
AI.method: Design a two-part evaluation: (1) theoretical assessment using questions drawn from widely recognized financial qualification exams, and (2) practical assessment via a systematic evaluation matrix covering financial subdomains, yielding 3,000 scenario questions (closed-form with reference answers and open-ended rubrics). Evaluate state-of-the-art LLMs (including XuanYuan 4.0 as an in-domain baseline). Publicly release benchmark questions and evaluation code.
AI.result: Provides a framework and baseline analyses of current LLMs in finance, outlining capability boundaries and practical usefulness; includes strong in-domain baseline (XuanYuan 4.0) and a plan to publicly release data to facilitate future research.
AI.conclusion: FIRE constitutes a comprehensive, publicly available benchmark for rigorous evaluation of LLMs in financial applications, enabling reproducibility and progress in both theory and practice.

title: FLIGHT: Fibonacci Lattice-based Inference for Geometric Heading in real-Time
summary: Estimating camera motion from monocular video is a fundamental problem in computer vision, central to tasks such as SLAM, visual odometry, and structure-from-motion. Existing methods that recover the camera's heading under known rotation, whether from an IMU or an optimization algorithm, tend to perform well in low-noise, low-outlier conditions, but often decrease in accuracy or become computationally expensive as noise and outlier levels increase. To address these limitations, we propose a novel generalization of the Hough transform on the unit sphere (S(2)) to estimate the camera's heading. First, the method extracts correspondences between two frames and generates a great circle of directions compatible with each pair of correspondences. Then, by discretizing the unit sphere using a Fibonacci lattice as bin centers, each great circle casts votes for a range of directions, ensuring that features unaffected by noise or dynamic objects vote consistently for the correct motion direction. Experimental results on three datasets demonstrate that the proposed method is on the Pareto frontier of accuracy versus efficiency. Additionally, experiments on SLAM show that the proposed method reduces RMSE by correcting the heading during camera pose initialization.

AI.tldr: A robust heading estimation method for monocular camera motion using a sphere-based Hough transform; combines great-circle votes per feature correspondence with Fibonacci sphere discretization to robustly estimate heading under noise and outliers; achieves Pareto-optimal accuracy/efficiency and improves SLAM initialization RMSE.
AI.motivation: Existing heading estimation methods degrade with noise/outliers; need robust, efficient monocular heading estimation to improve SLAM/VO/SfM pipelines.
AI.method: For each frame pair, derive correspondences, form a great circle of directions compatible with the pair; discretize S^2 via Fibonacci lattice; each great circle votes over a range of directions; robust consensus direction emerges.
AI.result: Experiments on three datasets show Pareto frontier in accuracy vs efficiency; SLAM experiments show RMSE reduction when heading corrected during pose initialization.
AI.conclusion: Sphere-based Hough transform yields robust, efficient heading estimation under challenging conditions and is beneficial for SLAM initialization and related monocular motion estimation tasks.

title: Multi-Level Causal Embeddings
summary: Abstractions of causal models allow for the coarsening of models such that relations of cause and effect are preserved. Whereas abstractions focus on the relation between two models, in this paper we study a framework for causal embeddings which enable multiple detailed models to be mapped into sub-systems of a coarser causal model. We define causal embeddings as a generalization of abstraction, and present a generalized notion of consistency. By defining a multi-resolution marginal problem, we showcase the relevance of causal embeddings for both the statistical marginal problem and the causal marginal problem; furthermore, we illustrate its practical use in merging datasets coming from models with different representations.

AI.tldr: Causal embeddings extend abstraction by allowing multiple detailed causal models to map into sub-systems of a coarser causal model, introducing a multi-resolution marginal framework that connects statistical and causal marginal problems and enables merging datasets from heterogeneous representations.
AI.motivation: To enable consistent, multi-resolution reasoning across several detailed causal models by embedding them into a unified, coarser representation, addressing limitations of standard abstractions for multi-model data integration.
AI.method: Formalize causal embeddings as a generalization of abstraction, define a generalized consistency notion, introduce a multi-resolution marginal problem, and relate it to statistical and causal marginal problems; illustrate practical applicability in merging datasets from differently represented models.
AI.result: A theoretical framework for causal embeddings is established, including generalized consistency and a multi-resolution marginal problem, with demonstrations of relevance to marginal problems and practical data-merging scenarios across models with diverse representations.
AI.conclusion: Causal embeddings generalize abstractions, enabling coherent integration of multiple detailed models into coarser causal structures and supporting multi-resolution data fusion and analysis.

title: Agent Behavioral Contracts: Formal Specification and Runtime Enforcement for Reliable Autonomous AI Agents
comment: 71 pages, 7 figures, 14 tables. Patent pending. Also available on Zenodo: DOI 10.5281/zenodo.18775393
summary: Traditional software relies on contracts -- APIs, type systems, assertions -- to specify and enforce correct behavior. AI agents, by contrast, operate on prompts and natural language instructions with no formal behavioral specification. This gap is the root cause of drift, governance failures, and frequent project failures in agentic AI deployments. We introduce Agent Behavioral Contracts (ABC), a formal framework that brings Design-by-Contract principles to autonomous AI agents. An ABC contract C = (P, I, G, R) specifies Preconditions, Invariants, Governance policies, and Recovery mechanisms as first-class, runtime-enforceable components. We define (p, delta, k)-satisfaction -- a probabilistic notion of contract compliance that accounts for LLM non-determinism and recovery -- and prove a Drift Bounds Theorem showing that contracts with recovery rate gamma > alpha (the natural drift rate) bound behavioral drift to D* = alpha/gamma in expectation, with Gaussian concentration in the stochastic setting. We establish sufficient conditions for safe contract composition in multi-agent chains and derive probabilistic degradation bounds. We implement ABC in AgentAssert, a runtime enforcement library, and evaluate on AgentContract-Bench, a benchmark of 200 scenarios across 7 models from 6 vendors. Results across 1,980 sessions show that contracted agents detect 5.2-6.8 soft violations per session that uncontracted baselines miss entirely (p < 0.0001, Cohen's d = 6.7-33.8), achieve 88-100% hard constraint compliance, and bound behavioral drift to D* < 0.27 across extended sessions, with 100% recovery for frontier models and 17-100% across all models, at overhead < 10 ms per action.

AI.tldr: ABC provides a Design-by-Contract inspired framework for autonomous AI agents, introducing runtime-enforceable Preconditions, Invariants, Governance, and Recovery with probabilistic satisfaction and drift bounds, implemented in AgentAssert and validated on a broad benchmark; results show improved detection of soft violations, strong hard-constraint compliance, bounded drift, and low overhead.
AI.motivation: Traditional software contracts (APIs, types, assertions) do not apply directly to AI agents operating on prompts, leading to drift, governance failures, and project failures. A formal contract-based approach could provide runtime enforceablility and governance for agentic AI.
AI.method: Define ABC contract C = (P, I, G, R) with (p, delta, k)-satisfaction to accommodate LLM nondeterminism and recovery; prove Drift Bounds Theorem to relate recovery rate gamma to natural drift alpha; establish safe composition conditions for multi-agent chains; implement runtime enforcement in AgentAssert; evaluate on AgentContract-Bench (200 scenarios, 7 models, 6 vendors; 1,980 sessions).
AI.result: Contracts detect 5.2-6.8 soft violations per session that uncontracted baselines miss (p<0.0001, Cohen's d=6.7-33.8); achieve 88-100% hard constraint compliance; bound behavioral drift to D*<0.27 across extended sessions; 100% recovery for frontier models and 17-100% across all models; overhead <10 ms per action.
AI.conclusion: ABC couples formal contract theory with runtime enforcement to mitigate drift and governance risks in agentic AI, enabling safer composition and more reliable behavior; results show strong violation detection, high hard-constraint compliance, and low overhead, supporting practical viability and suggesting directions for broader adoption and further theoretical refinement.

title: SODA-CitrON: Static Object Data Association by Clustering Multi-Modal Sensor Detections Online
comment: 8 pages, 5 figures; Submitted to the 2026 International Conference on Information Fusion (FUSION 2026). Under review
summary: The online fusion and tracking of static objects from heterogeneous sensor detections is a fundamental problem in robotics, autonomous systems, and environmental mapping. Although classical data association approaches such as JPDA are well suited for dynamic targets, they are less effective for static objects observed intermittently and with heterogeneous uncertainties, where motion models provide minimal discriminative with respect to clutter. In this paper, we propose a novel method for static object data association by clustering multi-modal sensor detections online (SODA-CitrON), while simultaneously estimating positions and maintaining persistent tracks for an unknown number of objects. The proposed unsupervised machine learning approach operates in a fully online manner and handles temporally uncorrelated and multi-sensor measurements. Additionally, it has a worst-case loglinear complexity in the number of sensor detections while providing full output explainability. We evaluate the proposed approach in different Monte Carlo simulation scenarios and compare it against state-of-the-art methods, including Bayesian filtering, DBSTREAM clustering, and JPDA. The results demonstrate that SODA-CitrON consistently outperforms the compared methods in terms of F1 score, position RMSE, MOTP, and MOTA in the static object mapping scenarios studied.

AI.tldr: Introduces SODA-CitrON, an online unsupervised clustering method for static object data association across heterogeneous sensors that maintains tracks for an unknown number of objects with loglinear worst-case complexity and full explainability; it outperforms baselines in static object mapping tasks.
AI.motivation: Static objects observed intermittently with heterogeneous uncertainties; motion-based data association (e.g., JPDA) is less effective due to weak discriminative cues; need online, scalable, explainable multi-sensor fusion and tracking for static objects.
AI.method: Online clustering-based data association (SODA-CitrON) that clusters multi-modal detections, estimates object positions, and maintains persistent tracks for an unknown number of objects; unsupervised, fully online; handles temporally uncorrelated multi-sensor measurements; provides explainable outputs; worst-case loglinear complexity in detections.
AI.result: Monte Carlo simulations comparing to Bayesian filtering, DBSTREAM, and JPDA; SODA-CitrON achieves higher F1, lower position RMSE, and better MOTP/MOTA in static object mapping scenarios.
AI.conclusion: SODA-CitrON provides a scalable, explainable online solution for static object mapping with unknown object count, outperforming several state-of-the-art methods.

title: Enabling clinical use of foundation models in histopathology
summary: Foundation models in histopathology are expected to facilitate the development of high-performing and generalisable deep learning systems. However, current models capture not only biologically relevant features, but also pre-analytic and scanner-specific variation that bias the predictions of task-specific models trained from the foundation model features. Here we show that introducing novel robustness losses during training of downstream task-specific models reduces sensitivity to technical variability. A purpose-designed comprehensive experimentation setup with 27,042 WSIs from 6155 patients is used to train thousands of models from the features of eight popular foundation models for computational pathology. In addition to a substantial improvement in robustness, we observe that prediction accuracy improves by focusing on biologically relevant features. Our approach successfully mitigates robustness issues of foundation models for computational pathology without retraining the foundation models themselves, enabling development of robust computational pathology models applicable to real-world data in routine clinical practice.

AI.tldr: Introducing novel robustness losses during training of downstream task-specific models reduces sensitivity to technical variability (pre-analytic/scanner variation) in foundation-model features, yielding improved robustness and accuracy without retraining foundation models, demonstrated on a large WSIs dataset across eight foundation models.
AI.motivation: Address the bias from non-biological, technical variation encoded in foundation-model features that impairs generalization in computational pathology.
AI.method: Apply purpose-designed robustness losses during downstream model training using features from eight foundation models; evaluate on a large, multi-patient WSIs dataset (27,042 slides from 6,155 patients) across thousands of models.
AI.result: Substantial robustness improvements and higher prediction accuracy achieved by focusing on biologically relevant features; robustness achieved without retraining foundation models.
AI.conclusion: Robustness issues of foundation models in computational pathology can be mitigated through downstream training-time robustness losses, enabling reliable clinical deployment without modifying foundation models.

title: To Deceive is to Teach? Forging Perceptual Robustness via Adversarial Reinforcement Learning
summary: Despite their impressive capabilities, Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) exhibit perceptual fragility when confronted with visually complex scenes. This weakness stems from a reliance on finite training datasets, which are prohibitively expensive to scale and impose a ceiling on model robustness. We introduce \textbf{AOT-SFT}, a large-scale adversarial dataset for bootstrapping MLLM robustness. Building on this, we propose \textbf{AOT (Adversarial Opponent Training)}, a self-play framework that forges MLLM robustness by creating its own training data. Our method orchestrates a co-evolution between an image-editing Attacker and a Defender MLLM, where the Attacker generates a diverse and dynamic curriculum of image manipulations, forcing the Defender to adapt and improve. Extensive experiments demonstrate that AOT enhances the Defender's perceptual robustness and reduces hallucinations, establishing a scalable paradigm for training more reliable MLLMs.

AI.tldr: AOT-SFT introduces a large-scale adversarial dataset and AOT self-play to co-evolve an Attacker and Defender MLLM, yielding improved perceptual robustness and fewer hallucinations in multimodal models.
AI.motivation: MLLMs suffer perceptual fragility due to limited, biased training data; scalable approaches to increase robustness are needed to prevent misperception and hallucinations in complex scenes.
AI.method: Develop AOT-SFT, a large adversarial dataset; implement Adversarial Opponent Training (AOT) where an Attacker applies diverse image manipulations to create a dynamic curriculum; the Defender MLLM learns through self-play and adaptation to these attacks, fostering a co-evolution.
AI.result: Empirical studies show that AOT improves the Defender's perceptual robustness and reduces hallucinations; demonstrates a scalable, data-efficient paradigm for enhancing reliability of MLLMs.
AI.conclusion: AOT provides a scalable framework for bootstrapping MLLM robustness via adversarial self-play, with potential applicability across multimodal tasks and opportunities for further refinement of the attack curriculum and evaluation metrics.

title: Vibe Researching as Wolf Coming: Can AI Agents with Skills Replace or Augment Social Scientists?
summary: AI agents -- systems that execute multi-step reasoning workflows with persistent state, tool access, and specialist skills -- represent a qualitative shift from prior automation technologies in social science. Unlike chatbots that respond to isolated queries, AI agents can now read files, run code, query databases, search the web, and invoke domain-specific skills to execute entire research pipelines autonomously. This paper introduces the concept of vibe researching -- the AI-era parallel to ``vibe coding'' (Karpathy, 2025) -- and uses scholar-skill, a 21-skill plugin for Claude Code covering the full research pipeline from idea to submission, as an illustrative case. I develop a cognitive task framework that classifies research activities along two dimensions -- codifiability and tacit knowledge requirement -- to identify a delegation boundary that is cognitive, not sequential: it cuts through every stage of the research pipeline, not between stages. I argue that AI agents excel at speed, coverage, and methodological scaffolding but struggle with theoretical originality and tacit field knowledge. The paper concludes with an analysis of three implications for the profession -- augmentation with fragile conditions, stratification risk, and a pedagogical crisis -- and proposes five principles for responsible vibe researching.

AI.tldr: AI agents enable end-to-end research pipelines and introduce vibe researching, using a 21-skill plugin to illustrate the concept. A cognitive task framework (codifiability vs. tacit knowledge) defines a cognitive delegation boundary across the research pipeline. AI agents offer speed, coverage, and scaffolding but struggle with originality and tacit domain knowledge. Three professional implications (fragile augmentation, stratification, pedagogical crisis) are identified, with five proposed principles for responsible vibe researching.
AI.motivation: To understand how AI agents transform research workflows and to establish a framework for when delegation should occur within research tasks, separating cognitive boundaries from sequential stage boundaries.
AI.method: Conceptual framework development: classifying research activities along two dimensions (codifiability and tacit knowledge) to locate a cognitive delegation boundary in the entire pipeline. Includes illustration via a 21-skill plugin (scholar-skill) for Claude Code and a discussion of vibe researching and vibe coding.
AI.result: AIs excel at speed, breadth, and methodological scaffolding, but struggle with theoretical originality and tacit field knowledge. The delegation boundary is cognitive in nature, not strictly sequential. Three implications are discussed: augmentation under fragile conditions, stratification risk, and a pedagogical crisis.
AI.conclusion: Five principles for responsible vibe researching are proposed.

title: Detection and Recognition: A Pairwise Interaction Framework for Mobile Service Robots
summary: Autonomous mobile service robots, like lawnmowers or cleaning robots, operating in human-populated environments need to reason about local human-human interactions to support safe and socially aware navigation while fulfilling their tasks. For such robots, interaction understanding is not primarily a fine-grained recognition problem, but a perception problem under limited sensing quality and computational resources. Many existing approaches focus on holistic group activity recognition, which often requires complex and large models which may not be necessary for mobile service robots. Others use pairwise interaction methods which commonly rely on skeletal representations but their use in outdoor environments remains challenging. In this work, we argue that pairwise human interaction constitute a minimal yet sufficient perceptual unit for robot-centric social understanding. We study the problem of identifying interacting person pairs and classifying coarse-grained interaction behaviors sufficient for downstream group-level reasoning and service robot decision-making. To this end, we adopt a two-stage framework in which candidate interacting pairs are first identified based on lightweight geometric and motion cues, and interaction types are subsequently classified using a relation network. We evaluate the proposed approach on the JRDB dataset, where it achieves sufficient accuracy with reduced computational cost and model size compared to appearance-based methods. Additional experiments on the Collective Activity Dataset and zero shot test on a lawnmower-collected dataset further illustrate the generality of the proposed framework. These results suggest that pairwise geometric and motion cues provide a practical basis for interaction perception on mobile service robot providing a promising method for integration into mobile robot navigation stacks in future work. Code will be released soon

AI.tldr: Pairwise human interactions are proposed as a minimal perceptual unit for robot-centric social understanding; a two-stage pipeline identifies candidate interacting pairs via lightweight geometric/motion cues and classifies their interaction type with a relation network, achieving efficient, generalizable performance on JRDB and other datasets and enabling integration into mobile service robot navigation.
AI.motivation: Robots operating around people require safe, socially aware navigation under limited sensing quality and computational resources. Holistic group activity recognition is often too heavy for mobile robots, and pairwise methods relying on skeletal cues can be challenging outdoors. The authors argue that pairwise interactions suffice for downstream reasoning and decision-making.
AI.method: A two-stage framework: (1) lightweight identification of candidate interacting pairs using geometric and motion cues; (2) a relation network to classify coarse-grained interaction types. Evaluation on JRDB with reduced cost/model size vs appearance-based methods, plus experiments on Collective Activity Dataset and a lawnmower-collected dataset for zero-shot generalization.
AI.result: The approach achieves sufficient accuracy with reduced computational cost and model size compared to appearance-based methods. Demonstrates generality across JRDB, Collective Activity Dataset, and lawnmower data (zero-shot).
AI.conclusion: Pairwise geometric and motion cues provide a practical basis for robot social understanding and can be integrated into mobile robot navigation stacks; future work includes broader integration and opening-code release.

title: Optimizing Neural Network Architecture for Medical Image Segmentation Using Monte Carlo Tree Search
summary: This paper proposes a novel medical image segmentation framework, MNAS-Unet, which combines Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) and Neural Architecture Search (NAS). MNAS-Unet dynamically explores promising network architectures through MCTS, significantly enhancing the efficiency and accuracy of architecture search. It also optimizes the DownSC and UpSC unit structures, enabling fast and precise model adjustments. Experimental results demonstrate that MNAS-Unet outperforms NAS-Unet and other state-of-the-art models in segmentation accuracy on several medical image datasets, including PROMISE12, Ultrasound Nerve, and CHAOS. Furthermore, compared with NAS-Unet, MNAS-Unet reduces the architecture search budget by 54% (early stopping at 139 epochs versus 300 epochs under the same search setting), while achieving a lightweight model with only 0.6M parameters and lower GPU memory consumption, which further improves its practical applicability. These results suggest that MNAS-Unet can improve search efficiency while maintaining competitive segmentation accuracy under practical resource constraints.

AI.tldr: MNAS-Unet uses Monte Carlo Tree Search-guided neural architecture search to design efficient medical image segmentation networks, achieving competitive accuracy with only 0.6M parameters and a 54% reduction in search budget compared to NAS-Unet.
AI.motivation: To reduce the computational cost and memory footprint of NAS in medical image segmentation while maintaining or improving segmentation accuracy, enabling practical deployment on resource-limited systems.
AI.method: Integrates Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) with Neural Architecture Search (NAS) to explore architectural space; jointly optimizes the DownSC and UpSC units for fast, accurate adjustments; conducts early stopping (139 vs 300 epochs) to further reduce search time.
AI.result: MNAS-Unet outperforms NAS-Unet and other state-of-the-art models in segmentation accuracy on PROMISE12, Ultrasound Nerve, and CHAOS datasets; reduces NAS search budget by ~54% and yields a lightweight model with 0.6M parameters and lower GPU memory usage.
AI.conclusion: MNAS-Unet demonstrates improved search efficiency while maintaining competitive segmentation accuracy under practical resource constraints, offering a viable option for efficient medical image segmentation.

title: Patient-Centered, Graph-Augmented Artificial Intelligence-Enabled Passive Surveillance for Early Stroke Risk Detection in High-Risk Individuals
summary: Stroke affected millions annually, yet poor symptom recognition often delayed care-seeking. To address risk recognition gap, we developed a passive surveillance system for early stroke risk detection using patient-reported symptoms among individuals with diabetes. Constructing a symptom taxonomy grounded in patients own language and a dual machine learning pipeline (heterogeneous GNN and EN/LASSO), we identified symptom patterns associated with subsequent stroke. We translated findings into a hybrid risk screening system integrating symptom relevance and temporal proximity, evaluated across 3-90 day windows through EHR-based simulations. Under conservative thresholds, intentionally designed to minimize false alerts, the screening system achieved high specificity (1.00) and prevalence-adjusted positive predictive value (1.00), with good sensitivity (0.72), an expected trade-off prioritizing precision, that was highest in 90-day window. Patient-reported language alone supported high-precision, low-burden early stroke risk detection, that could offer a valuable time window for clinical evaluation and intervention for high-risk individuals.

AI.tldr: Passive, patient-reported symptom surveillance for early stroke risk in diabetes using a hybrid ML pipeline and symptom taxonomy to achieve high precision and 3–90 day predictive windows.
AI.motivation: Stroke care is delayed by poor symptom recognition; leveraging patient language and passive data can detect early risk in high-risk groups (diabetes) to enable timely clinical evaluation.
AI.method: Developed a symptom taxonomy grounded in patient language. Built a dual ML pipeline combining a heterogeneous graph neural network (GNN) and elastic net/LASSO to identify patterns linked to subsequent stroke. Created a hybrid risk screening system that fuses symptom relevance with temporal proximity, evaluated in EHR-based simulations across 3-, 30-, and 90-day windows.
AI.result: Under conservative alert thresholds prioritizing precision, the system achieved specificity = 1.00 and prevalence-adjusted PPV = 1.00, with sensitivity ≈ 0.72, and performance peaking at the 90-day window, indicating high-precision, low-burden risk detection.
AI.conclusion: Patient-reported language can support high-precision, low-burden early stroke risk detection, offering a meaningful time window for clinical assessment and intervention for high-risk individuals.

title: Towards Autonomous Memory Agents
summary: Recent memory agents improve LLMs by extracting experiences and conversation history into an external storage. This enables low-overhead context assembly and online memory update without expensive LLM training. However, existing solutions remain passive and reactive; memory growth is bounded by information that happens to be available, while memory agents seldom seek external inputs in uncertainties. We propose autonomous memory agents that actively acquire, validate, and curate knowledge at a minimum cost. U-Mem materializes this idea via (i) a cost-aware knowledge-extraction cascade that escalates from cheap self/teacher signals to tool-verified research and, only when needed, expert feedback, and (ii) semantic-aware Thompson sampling to balance exploration and exploitation over memories and mitigate cold-start bias. On both verifiable and non-verifiable benchmarks, U-Mem consistently beats prior memory baselines and can surpass RL-based optimization, improving HotpotQA (Qwen2.5-7B) by 14.6 points and AIME25 (Gemini-2.5-flash) by 7.33 points.

AI.tldr: U-Mem introduces autonomous memory agents with a cost-aware extraction cascade and semantic-aware Thompson sampling to actively acquire and curate memory, outperforming baselines on verifiable and non-verifiable benchmarks with notable gains on HotpotQA (+14.6) and AIME25 (+7.33).
AI.motivation: Address the limitations of passive memory that only captures information available and avoids expensive retraining, by enabling proactive, cost-efficient knowledge acquisition and curation with better handling of initial uncertainty (cold-start).
AI.method: Two main components: (i) a cost-aware knowledge-extraction cascade that escalates from inexpensive self/teacher signals to tool-verified research, and only queries expert feedback when necessary; (ii) semantic-aware Thompson sampling to balance exploration and exploitation over stored memories, mitigating cold-start bias.
AI.result: On both verifiable and non-verifiable benchmarks, U-Mem surpasses prior memory baselines and can exceed RL-based optimization, achieving substantial improvements on HotpotQA and AIME25 as cited.
AI.conclusion: Autonomous, cost-aware memory acquisition and retrieval via a cascade of signals and Thompson sampling yields stronger memory-driven QA performance, suggesting practical gains for LLM-based agents with external memory.

title: Hierarchical Trajectory Planning of Floating-Base Multi-Link Robot for Maneuvering in Confined Environments
comment: Accepted to IEEE T-ASE; DOI pending
summary: Floating-base multi-link robots can change their shape during flight, making them well-suited for applications in confined environments such as autonomous inspection and search and rescue. However, trajectory planning for such systems remains an open challenge because the problem lies in a high-dimensional, constraint-rich space where collision avoidance must be addressed together with kinematic limits and dynamic feasibility. This work introduces a hierarchical trajectory planning framework that integrates global guidance with configuration-aware local optimization. First, we exploit the dual nature of these robots - the root link as a rigid body for guidance and the articulated joints for flexibility - to generate global anchor states that decompose the planning problem into tractable segments. Second, we design a local trajectory planner that optimizes each segment in parallel with differentiable objectives and constraints, systematically enforcing kinematic feasibility and maintaining dynamic feasibility by avoiding control singularities. Third, we implement a complete system that directly processes point-cloud data, eliminating the need for handcrafted obstacle models. Extensive simulations and real-world experiments confirm that this framework enables an articulated aerial robot to exploit its morphology for maneuvering that rigid robots cannot achieve. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first planning framework for floating-base multi-link robots that has been demonstrated on a real robot to generate continuous, collision-free, and dynamically feasible trajectories directly from raw point-cloud inputs, without relying on handcrafted obstacle models.

AI.tldr: A hierarchical planning framework for floating-base multi-link robots using global anchor states and parallel local optimization, processing raw point-cloud data without handcrafted obstacle models to produce continuous, collision-free, dynamically feasible trajectories; validated in simulation and real hardware.
AI.motivation: Planar high-dimensional, constraint-rich trajectory planning for floating-base multi-link robots; address collision avoidance, kinematic limits, and dynamic feasibility in confined environments; leverage morphology (root rigid body + articulated joints) and avoid reliance on handcrafted obstacles.
AI.method: 1) Generate global anchor states by treating the root link as a rigid body for guidance, decomposing the problem into tractable segments. 2) Develop a local trajectory planner that optimizes each segment in parallel using differentiable objectives/constraints, ensuring kinematic feasibility and avoiding control singularities to maintain dynamic feasibility. 3) Build a complete system that directly processes point-cloud data, eliminating handcrafted obstacle models.
AI.result: Extensive simulations and real-world experiments show the articulated aerial robot can exploit its morphology for maneuvers unattainable by rigid robots, demonstrating a complete framework for planning continuous, collision-free, dynamically feasible trajectories from raw point-cloud inputs.
AI.conclusion: This work delivers the first planning framework for floating-base multi-link robots demonstrated on a real robot that generates continuous, collision-free, and dynamically feasible trajectories directly from raw point-cloud inputs without handcrafted obstacle models.

title: AeroDGS: Physically Consistent Dynamic Gaussian Splatting for Single-Sequence Aerial 4D Reconstruction
summary: Recent advances in 4D scene reconstruction have significantly improved dynamic modeling across various domains. However, existing approaches remain limited under aerial conditions with single-view capture, wide spatial range, and dynamic objects of limited spatial footprint and large motion disparity. These challenges cause severe depth ambiguity and unstable motion estimation, making monocular aerial reconstruction inherently ill-posed. To this end, we present AeroDGS, a physics-guided 4D Gaussian splatting framework for monocular UAV videos. AeroDGS introduces a Monocular Geometry Lifting module that reconstructs reliable static and dynamic geometry from a single aerial sequence, providing a robust basis for dynamic estimation. To further resolve monocular ambiguity, we propose a Physics-Guided Optimization module that incorporates differentiable ground-support, upright-stability, and trajectory-smoothness priors, transforming ambiguous image cues into physically consistent motion. The framework jointly refines static backgrounds and dynamic entities with stable geometry and coherent temporal evolution. We additionally build a real-world UAV dataset that spans various altitudes and motion conditions to evaluate dynamic aerial reconstruction. Experiments on synthetic and real UAV scenes demonstrate that AeroDGS outperforms state-of-the-art methods, achieving superior reconstruction fidelity in dynamic aerial environments.

AI.tldr: AeroDGS enables robust monocular 4D dynamic reconstruction for aerial scenes via physics-guided 4D Gaussian splatting, combining monocular geometry lifting with differentiable priors to recover coherent static and dynamic content from UAV videos, outperforming SOTA on synthetic and real data.
AI.motivation: Monocular aerial reconstruction is ill-posed due to depth ambiguity, wide spatial range, and large motion disparity; single-view UAV videos struggle to accurately recover dynamic geometry, necessitating physics-informed, geometry-aware methods.
AI.method: Introduce Monocular Geometry Lifting to recover reliable static and dynamic geometry from a single aerial sequence; add Physics-Guided Optimization with differentiable ground-support, upright-stability, and trajectory-smoothness priors; employ 4D Gaussian splatting to model scene dynamics and jointly refine static background and dynamic entities.
AI.result: Demonstrates superior reconstruction fidelity over state-of-the-art on both synthetic and real UAV scenes; the approach is validated on a newly collected real-world UAV dataset spanning multiple altitudes and motion conditions.
AI.conclusion: AeroDGS provides a principled, physics-guided framework for monocular 4D aerial reconstruction, leveraging geometry lifting and trajectory priors to achieve coherent, stable dynamic reconstructions; the real UAV dataset supports comprehensive evaluation and demonstrates practical applicability.

title: Improving Spatial Allocation for Energy System Coupling with Graph Neural Networks
summary: In energy system analysis, coupling models with mismatched spatial resolutions is a significant challenge. A common solution is assigning weights to high-resolution geographic units for aggregation, but traditional models are limited by using only a single geospatial attribute. This paper presents an innovative method employing a self-supervised Heterogeneous Graph Neural Network to address this issue. This method models high-resolution geographic units as graph nodes, integrating various geographical features to generate physically meaningful weights for each grid point. These weights enhance the conventional Voronoi-based allocation method, allowing it to go beyond simply geographic proximity by incorporating essential geographic information.In addition, the self-supervised learning paradigm overcomes the lack of accurate ground-truth data. Experimental results demonstrate that applying weights generated by this method to cluster-based Voronoi Diagrams significantly enhances scalability, accuracy, and physical plausibility, while increasing precision compared to traditional methods.

AI.tldr: Self-supervised heterogeneous GNN to learn physically meaningful weights for high-resolution geographic units, improving Voronoi-based aggregation in energy systems.
AI.motivation: Mismatched spatial resolutions and reliance on a single geospatial attribute limit traditional aggregation; richer integration of multiple geographic features is needed for scalable, physically plausible mappings between fine and coarse resolutions.
AI.method: Model high-resolution geographic units as nodes in a heterogeneous graph, integrating multiple geographic features to learn weights for grid points via self-supervised training; augment the Voronoi-based allocation with these learned weights to reflect more than mere geographic proximity.
AI.result: Learned weights improve scalability, accuracy, and physical plausibility of cluster-based Voronoi diagrams, yielding higher precision than traditional proximity-based or single-attribute methods.
AI.conclusion: Self-supervised heterogeneous GNNs enable multi-attribute, physically grounded aggregation across spatial scales, enhancing energy system analyses and potentially other domains with similar spatial-aggregation challenges.

title: Exploring Human Behavior During Abstract Rule Inference and Problem Solving with the Cognitive Abstraction and Reasoning Corpus
summary: Humans exhibit remarkable flexibility in abstract reasoning, and can rapidly learn and apply rules from sparse examples. To investigate the cognitive strategies underlying this ability, we introduce the Cognitive Abstraction and Reasoning Corpus (CogARC), a diverse human-adapted subset of the Abstraction and Reasoning Corpus (ARC) which was originally developed to benchmark abstract reasoning in artificial intelligence. Across two experiments, CogARC was administered to a total of 260 human participants who freely generated solutions to 75 abstract visual reasoning problems. Success required inferring input-output rules from a small number of examples to transform the test input into one correct test output. Participants' behavior was recorded at high temporal resolution, including example viewing, edit sequences, and multi-attempt submissions. Participants were generally successful (mean accuracy ~90% for experiment 1 (n=40), ~80% for experiment 2 (n=220) across problems), but performance varied widely across problems and participants. Harder problems elicited longer deliberation times and greater divergence in solution strategies. Over the course of the task, participants initiated responses more quickly but showed a slight decline in accuracy, suggesting increased familiarity with the task structure rather than improved rule-learning ability. Importantly, even incorrect solutions were often highly convergent, even when the problem-solving trajectories differed in length and smoothness. Some trajectories progressed directly and efficiently toward a stable outcome, whereas others involved extended exploration or partial restarts before converging. Together, these findings highlight CogARC as a rich behavioral environment for studying human abstract reasoning, providing insight into how people generalize, misgeneralize, and adapt their strategies under uncertainty.

AI.tldr: CogARC is a human-adapted subset of ARC designed to study how people infer input-output rules for abstract visual reasoning. Across two experiments with 260 participants solving 75 problems, accuracy was high but variable. The study reveals how rule-learning strategies and problem difficulty shape deliberation, convergence of solutions, and adaptation under uncertainty, providing a rich dataset for analyzing generalization and misgeneralization in human reasoning.
AI.motivation: To understand the cognitive strategies humans use for abstract reasoning, particularly rule inference from sparse examples, and to create a human-adapted benchmark (CogARC) aligned with ARC for comparing human and artificial reasoning under uncertainty.
AI.method: Develop CogARC (a human-adapted ARC subset) and administer it in two experiments (Exp 1: n=40; Exp 2: n=220) with a total of 260 participants solving 75 abstract visual problems. Participants freely generate solutions; task data include example viewing, edit sequences, and multi-attempt submissions. Measure accuracy, response times, and strategy trajectories; analyze how performance correlates with problem difficulty and familiarity with task structure.
AI.result: Overall accuracy was high (≈90% in Experiment 1; ≈80% in Experiment 2) but exhibited wide variation across problems and participants. Harder problems elicited longer deliberations and greater divergence in strategies. Over time, participants initiated responses faster yet showed a slight decline in accuracy, suggesting improved familiarity with task structure rather than enhanced rule-learning. Convergent paths to correct or near-correct solutions occurred even when individual trajectories differed. Some solutions followed direct, efficient routes, while others involved extended exploration or partial restarts before convergence.
AI.conclusion: CogARC provides a rich behavioral platform for studying human abstract reasoning, revealing how people generalize, misgeneralize, and adapt strategies under uncertainty. The dataset enables analysis of rule inference dynamics, convergence of problem-solving trajectories, and the interplay between familiarity with task structure and actual rule-learning.

title: EgoAVFlow: Robot Policy Learning with Active Vision from Human Egocentric Videos via 3D Flow
summary: Egocentric human videos provide a scalable source of manipulation demonstrations; however, deploying them on robots requires active viewpoint control to maintain task-critical visibility, which human viewpoint imitation often fails to provide due to human-specific priors. We propose EgoAVFlow, which learns manipulation and active vision from egocentric videos through a shared 3D flow representation that supports geometric visibility reasoning and transfers without robot demonstrations. EgoAVFlow uses diffusion models to predict robot actions, future 3D flow, and camera trajectories, and refines viewpoints at test time with reward-maximizing denoising under a visibility-aware reward computed from predicted motion and scene geometry. Real-world experiments under actively changing viewpoints show that EgoAVFlow consistently outperforms prior human-demo-based baselines, demonstrating effective visibility maintenance and robust manipulation without robot demonstrations.

AI.tldr: EgoAVFlow enables robot manipulation with active viewpoint control from egocentric videos using a shared 3D flow representation and diffusion-based action and view planning, achieving robust visibility without robot demonstrations.
AI.motivation: Egocentric videos provide scalable manipulation demonstrations but passive human viewpoints may fail to maintain task-critical visibility; active vision is needed for robust robotic manipulation, especially when viewpoints change.
AI.method: Learn manipulation and active vision from egocentric data via a shared 3D flow representation; use diffusion models to predict robot actions, future 3D flow, and camera trajectories; test-time viewpoint refinement via reward-maximizing denoising on visibility-aware reward computed from predicted motion and scene geometry.
AI.result: Real-world experiments with actively changing viewpoints show EgoAVFlow outperforms prior human-demo baselines, achieving effective visibility maintenance and robust manipulation without robot demonstrations.
AI.conclusion: The approach enables robust, demonstration-free robot manipulation under changing viewpoints by unifying perception and action planning through 3D flow and diffusion-based priors; potential limitations include reliance on accurate 3D flow estimation and scene geometry.

title: Enhancing Renal Tumor Malignancy Prediction: Deep Learning with Automatic 3D CT Organ Focused Attention
comment: 5 pages, 2 figures, Accepted at IEEE ISBI 2026
summary: Accurate prediction of malignancy in renal tumors is crucial for informing clinical decisions and optimizing treatment strategies. However, existing imaging modalities lack the necessary accuracy to reliably predict malignancy before surgical intervention. While deep learning has shown promise in malignancy prediction using 3D CT images, traditional approaches often rely on manual segmentation to isolate the tumor region and reduce noise, which enhances predictive performance. Manual segmentation, however, is labor-intensive, costly, and dependent on expert knowledge. In this study, a deep learning framework was developed utilizing an Organ Focused Attention (OFA) loss function to modify the attention of image patches so that organ patches attend only to other organ patches. Hence, no segmentation of 3D renal CT images is required at deployment time for malignancy prediction. The proposed framework achieved an AUC of 0.685 and an F1-score of 0.872 on a private dataset from the UF Integrated Data Repository (IDR), and an AUC of 0.760 and an F1-score of 0.852 on the publicly available KiTS21 dataset. These results surpass the performance of conventional models that rely on segmentation-based cropping for noise reduction, demonstrating the frameworks ability to enhance predictive accuracy without explicit segmentation input. The findings suggest that this approach offers a more efficient and reliable method for malignancy prediction, thereby enhancing clinical decision-making in renal cancer diagnosis.

AI.tldr: Segmentation-free deep learning for renal tumor malignancy using Organ Focused Attention (OFA); achieves competitive AUC/F1 on private UF IDR and public KiTS21, outperforming segmentation-based cropping methods.
AI.motivation: Manual segmentation for noise reduction is labor-intensive, costly, and restricts deployment. A segmentation-free approach could streamline workflow while maintaining or improving predictive accuracy.
AI.method: Introduce Organ Focused Attention (OFA) loss to steer patch-level attention so organ patches attend only to other organ patches, removing the need for 3D renal CT segmentation at deployment. Evaluate on private UF IDR data and the KiTS21 dataset, reporting AUC and F1 scores.
AI.result: On UF IDR: AUC 0.685, F1 0.872. On KiTS21: AUC 0.760, F1 0.852. The approach surpasses segmentation-based cropping models in predictive performance.
AI.conclusion: OFA enables efficient, segmentation-free malignancy prediction from 3D renal CT with potential to improve clinical decision-making. External validation and broader testing are needed to assess generalization and robustness across diverse populations and imaging protocols.

title: Zatom-1: A Multimodal Flow Foundation Model for 3D Molecules and Materials
summary: General-purpose 3D chemical modeling encompasses molecules and materials, requiring both generative and predictive capabilities. However, most existing AI approaches are optimized for a single domain (molecules or materials) and a single task (generation or prediction), which limits representation sharing and transfer. We introduce Zatom-1, the first foundation model that unifies generative and predictive learning of 3D molecules and materials. Zatom-1 is a Transformer trained with a multimodal flow matching objective that jointly models discrete atom types and continuous 3D geometries. This approach supports scalable pretraining with predictable gains as model capacity increases, while enabling fast and stable sampling. We use joint generative pretraining as a universal initialization for downstream multi-task prediction of properties, energies, and forces. Empirically, Zatom-1 matches or outperforms specialized baselines on both generative and predictive benchmarks, while reducing the generative inference time by more than an order of magnitude. Our experiments demonstrate positive predictive transfer between chemical domains from joint generative pretraining: modeling materials during pretraining improves molecular property prediction accuracy.

AI.tldr: Zatom-1 is a unified 3D chemistry foundation model that jointly learns generative and predictive tasks for molecules and materials via a multimodal flow matching Transformer, enabling fast sampling and cross-domain transfer.
AI.motivation: Most AI models in chemistry are domain- or task-specific (molecules vs materials; generation vs prediction), hindering representation sharing and transfer. A universal 3D chemistry model could enable scalable pretraining and cross-domain benefits.
AI.method: A Transformer trained with a multimodal flow matching objective that jointly models discrete atom types and continuous 3D geometries. Pretraining on both molecules and materials to enable joint generative pretraining that serves as universal initialization for downstream prediction of properties, energies, and forces; scalable with model capacity and fast, stable sampling.
AI.result: Matches or surpasses specialized baselines on both generative and predictive benchmarks; generative inference time reduced by >10×; joint pretraining yields positive transfer across chemical domains (materials pretraining improves molecular property prediction).
AI.conclusion: Joint generative pretraining enables a universal 3D chemistry foundation model with scalable gains, efficient sampling, and cross-domain transfer for both generation and prediction.

title: Epistemic Filtering and Collective Hallucination: A Jury Theorem for Confidence-Calibrated Agents
summary: We investigate the collective accuracy of heterogeneous agents who learn to estimate their own reliability over time and selectively abstain from voting. While classical epistemic voting results, such as the \textit{Condorcet Jury Theorem} (CJT), assume fixed participation, real-world aggregation often benefits from allowing agents to say ``I don't know.'' We propose a probabilistic framework where agents engage in a \textit{calibration} phase, updating beliefs about their own fixed competence, before facing a final confidence gate that determines whether to vote or abstain. We derive a non-asymptotic lower bound on the group's success probability and prove that this \textit{selective participation} generalizes the asymptotic guarantees of the CJT to a sequential, confidence-gated setting. Empirically, we validate these bounds via Monte Carlo simulations. While our results are general, we discuss their potential application to AI safety, outlining how this framework can mitigate \textit{hallucinations} in collective LLM decision-making.

AI.tldr: A probabilistic framework for selective participation in collective decision making, where agents calibrate their own competence and abstain if uncertain; non-asymptotic lower bound on group success; CJT generalized to sequential, confidence-gated setting; validated by Monte Carlo; potential AI-safety applications to reduce hallucinations in LLM ensembles.
AI.motivation: Extend classical Condorcet Jury Theorem to realistic settings where participants may abstain based on self-assessed reliability, addressing fixed-participation limitations and improving collective accuracy.
AI.method: Agents undergo a calibration phase to update beliefs about fixed competence, followed by a final confidence gate to decide voting or abstaining. The paper derives a non-asymptotic lower bound on the group's probability of correct decision and shows CJT guarantees extend to a sequential, confidence-gated framework. Validation is via Monte Carlo simulations.
AI.result: Derived a non-asymptotic lower bound for group success under selective participation; established formal generalization of CJT to sequential, gated participation; empirical support through Monte Carlo experiments.
AI.conclusion: Selective participation enhances collective accuracy and offers a principled approach to mitigate errors in ensembles (e.g., LLMs). The framework holds promise for AI safety applications by reducing hallucinations through calibrated abstention.

title: When to Act, Ask, or Learn: Uncertainty-Aware Policy Steering
summary: Policy steering is an emerging way to adapt robot behaviors at deployment-time: a learned verifier analyzes low-level action samples proposed by a pre-trained policy (e.g., diffusion policy) and selects only those aligned with the task. While Vision-Language Models (VLMs) are promising general-purpose verifiers due to their reasoning capabilities, existing frameworks often assume these models are well-calibrated. In practice, the overconfident judgment from VLM can degrade the steering performance under both high-level semantic uncertainty in task specifications and low-level action uncertainty or incapability of the pre-trained policy. We propose uncertainty-aware policy steering (UPS), a framework that jointly reasons about semantic task uncertainty and low-level action feasibility, and selects an uncertainty resolution strategy: execute a high-confidence action, clarify task ambiguity via natural language queries, or ask for action interventions to correct the low-level policy when it is deemed incapable at the task. We leverage conformal prediction to calibrate the composition of the VLM and the pre-trained base policy, providing statistical assurances that the verifier selects the correct strategy. After collecting interventions during deployment, we employ residual learning to improve the capability of the pre-trained policy, enabling the system to learn continually but with minimal expensive human feedback. We demonstrate our framework through experiments in simulation and on hardware, showing that UPS can disentangle confident, ambiguous, and incapable scenarios and minimizes expensive user interventions compared to uncalibrated baselines and prior human- or robot-gated continual learning approaches. Videos can be found at https://jessie-yuan.github.io/ups/

AI.tldr: Uncertainty-aware policy steering (UPS) calibrates a Vision-Language Model verifier with a pre-trained policy using conformal prediction, enabling selective actions, natural-language clarification, or intervention requests to handle semantic uncertainty and low-level action feasibility, followed by residual learning to improve the policy with minimal human feedback.
AI.motivation: VLMs offer broad reasoning for verification but can be overconfident, harming steering when task semantics are uncertain or the policy cannot realize actions at the low level. There is a need to explicitly manage semantic and action-level uncertainty at deployment.
AI.method: Propose UPS, which jointly reasons about task semantic uncertainty and action feasibility. It selects one of three strategies (execute high-confidence action, ask for natural-language clarification, or request action interventions). Calibrate the verifier using conformal prediction to provide statistical guarantees on strategy selection. Collect interventions to perform residual learning, improving the base policy over time for continual, low-cost updates. Validate in simulation and on hardware.
AI.result: UPS can disentangle confident, ambiguous, and incapable scenarios, reducing expensive user interventions compared with uncalibrated baselines and prior continual-learning approaches. Demonstrates effectiveness via simulation and hardware experiments, with videos linked.
AI.conclusion: Uncertainty-aware policy steering with conformal-calibrated verification enables reliable task deployment under both semantic and low-level uncertainty, and it supports continual policy improvement with minimal human effort.

title: Vision Transformers Need More Than Registers
summary: Vision Transformers (ViTs), when pre-trained on large-scale data, provide general-purpose representations for diverse downstream tasks. However, artifacts in ViTs are widely observed across different supervision paradigms and downstream tasks. Through systematic analysis of artifacts in ViTs, we find that their fundamental mechanisms have yet to be sufficiently elucidated. In this paper, through systematic analysis, we conclude that these artifacts originate from a lazy aggregation behavior: ViT uses semantically irrelevant background patches as shortcuts to represent global semantics, driven by global attention and Coarse-grained semantic supervision. Our solution selectively integrates patch features into the CLS token, reducing the influence of background-dominated shortcuts and consistently improving performance across 12 benchmarks under label-, text-, and self-supervision. We hope this work offers a new perspective on ViT behavior.

AI.tldr: ViTs exhibit artifacts due to lazy aggregation where background patches act as shortcuts for global semantics; a selective patch integration into the CLS token reduces background-dominated shortcuts and improves performance across 12 benchmarks.
AI.motivation: To understand and mitigate pervasive artifacts in Vision Transformers that arise from their aggregation mechanisms, aiming to elucidate fundamental ViT behavior and improve generalization across supervision paradigms.
AI.method: Systematic analysis of ViT artifacts identifying lazy aggregation driven by global attention and coarse-grained supervision; propose a method that selectively integrates patch features into the CLS token to suppress background shortcuts; evaluate across 12 benchmarks under label-, text-, and self-supervision.
AI.result: The proposed method reduces reliance on background-dominated shortcuts and consistently improves performance across 12 benchmarks regardless of supervision regime.
AI.conclusion: Artifacts in ViTs stem from lazy aggregation; selectively integrating patch features into CLS provides a more faithful representation and yields robust improvements across diverse tasks, offering a new perspective on ViT behavior.

title: Causal Direction from Convergence Time: Faster Training in the True Causal Direction
summary: We introduce Causal Computational Asymmetry (CCA), a principle for causal direction identification based on optimization dynamics in which one neural network is trained to predict $Y$ from $X$ and another to predict $X$ from $Y$, and the direction that converges faster is inferred to be causal. Under the additive noise model $Y = f(X) + \varepsilon$ with $\varepsilon \perp X$ and $f$ nonlinear and injective, we establish a formal asymmetry: in the reverse direction, residuals remain statistically dependent on the input regardless of approximation quality, inducing a strictly higher irreducible loss floor and non-separable gradient noise in the optimization dynamics, so that the reverse model requires strictly more gradient steps in expectation to reach any fixed loss threshold; consequently, the forward (causal) direction converges in fewer expected optimization steps. CCA operates in optimization-time space, distinguishing it from methods such as RESIT, IGCI, and SkewScore that rely on statistical independence or distributional asymmetries, and proper z-scoring of both variables is required for valid comparison of convergence rates. On synthetic benchmarks, CCA achieves 26/30 correct causal identifications across six neural architectures, including 30/30 on sine and exponential data-generating processes. We further embed CCA into a broader framework termed Causal Compression Learning (CCL), which integrates graph structure learning, causal information compression, and policy optimization, with all theoretical guarantees formally proved and empirically validated on synthetic datasets.

AI.tldr: CCA uses optimization-time asymmetry to infer causal direction by comparing convergence speeds of forward and reverse prediction models; under additive noise with nonlinear injective f, forward direction converges faster due to a higher irreducible loss floor and noisier gradients in the reverse direction. Empirically effective on synthetic data across architectures; extends to Causal Compression Learning (CCL) with theoretical guarantees.
AI.motivation: Provide a causal-direction criterion that leverages optimization dynamics rather than purely statistical dependencies or distributional asymmetries, addressing limitations of existing methods.
AI.method: Train two neural nets to predict Y from X and X from Y; compare their convergence rates to a fixed loss threshold after proper z-scoring; show reverse direction incurs higher irreducible loss and non-separable gradient noise, requiring more gradient steps on average. Compare to RESIT, IGCI, SkewScore. Validate on synthetic benchmarks across six architectures; provide DGPs such as sine/exponential.
AI.result: CCA achieves 26/30 correct causal identifications across six architectures; 30/30 on sine and exponential DGPs. Demonstrates robustness to architecture and data-generating process. Integrates into Causal Compression Learning (CCL) with formal guarantees and empirical validation on synthetic datasets.
AI.conclusion: Optimization-time asymmetry provides a viable causal-direction signal distinct from statistical independence; CCA is scalable and architecture-robust, and the CCL framework further unifies causal inference with graph learning and information compression under theoretical guarantees.

title: ArchAgent: Agentic AI-driven Computer Architecture Discovery
summary:
Agile hardware design flows are a critically needed force multiplier to meet the exploding demand for compute. Recently, agentic generative AI systems have demonstrated significant advances in algorithm design, improving code efficiency, and enabling discovery across scientific domains.
  Bridging these worlds, we present ArchAgent, an automated computer architecture discovery system built on AlphaEvolve. We show ArchAgent's ability to automatically design/implement state-of-the-art (SoTA) cache replacement policies (architecting new mechanisms/logic, not only changing parameters), broadly within the confines of an established cache replacement policy design competition.
  In two days without human intervention, ArchAgent generated a policy achieving a 5.3% IPC speedup improvement over the prior SoTA on public multi-core Google Workload Traces. On the heavily-explored single-core SPEC06 workloads, it generated a policy in just 18 days showing a 0.9% IPC speedup improvement over the existing SoTA (a similar "winning margin" as reported by the existing SoTA). ArchAgent achieved these gains 3-5x faster than prior human-developed SoTA policies.
  Agentic flows also enable "post-silicon hyperspecialization" where agents tune runtime-configurable parameters exposed in hardware policies to further align the policies with a specific workload (mix). Exploiting this, we demonstrate a 2.4% IPC speedup improvement over prior SoTA on SPEC06 workloads.
  Finally, we outline broader implications for computer architecture research in the era of agentic AI. For example, we demonstrate the phenomenon of "simulator escapes", where the agentic AI flow discovered and exploited a loophole in a popular microarchitectural simulator - a consequence of the fact that these research tools were designed for a (now past) world where they were exclusively operated by humans acting in good-faith.

AI.tldr: ArchAgent uses AlphaEvolve to automatically design/implement state-of-the-art cache replacement policies, achieving notable IPC improvements against the prior SoTA on both Google Workload Traces (5.3% on multi-core) and SPEC06 (0.9%), and doing so 3–5× faster than human-driven methods; also demonstrates post-silicon tuning (2.4% IPC gain) and highlights risks with simulator exploits (“simulator escapes”)—discusses broader implications for agentic AI in architecture research.
AI.motivation: The rapid growth in compute demand necessitates agile hardware design flows. Agentic generative AI Systems can automate algorithm/architecture discovery, enabling efficient exploration of design spaces and potentially uncovering novel mechanisms beyond human-derived designs.
AI.method: ArchAgent, built on AlphaEvolve, automatically designs and implements cache replacement policies (not mere parameter tuning) within established policy design competitions. Evaluation on public multi-core Google Workload Traces and SPE C06 single-core workloads; comparison against existing state-of-the-art policies. Demonstrates post-silicon hyperspecialization by tuning runtime-configurable hardware-policy parameters. Discusses occurrence of simulator escapes.
AI.result: Achieved 5.3% IPC speedup over prior SoTA on Google Workload Traces in 2 days without human intervention; 0.9% IPC speedup on SPEC06 in 18 days; overall 3–5× faster than prior human-developed SoTA approaches; post-silicon tuning yielded an additional 2.4% IPC speedup on SPEC06.
AI.conclusion: Agentic AI workflows can accelerate architecture space exploration and policy optimization, with substantial performance gains and faster iteration, but also raise concerns about simulator robustness and the need for careful evaluation of agent-generated policies in real hardware contexts; broader implications for AI-assisted computer architecture research.

title: SignVLA: A Gloss-Free Vision-Language-Action Framework for Real-Time Sign Language-Guided Robotic Manipulation
summary:
We present, to our knowledge, the first sign language-driven Vision-Language-Action (VLA) framework for intuitive and inclusive human-robot interaction. Unlike conventional approaches that rely on gloss annotations as intermediate supervision, the proposed system adopts a gloss-free paradigm and directly maps visual sign gestures to semantic instructions. This design reduces annotation cost and avoids the information loss introduced by gloss representations, enabling more natural and scalable multimodal interaction.
  In this work, we focus on a real-time alphabet-level finger-spelling interface that provides a robust and low-latency communication channel for robotic control. Compared with large-scale continuous sign language recognition, alphabet-level interaction offers improved reliability, interpretability, and deployment feasibility in safety-critical embodied environments. The proposed pipeline transforms continuous gesture streams into coherent language commands through geometric normalization, temporal smoothing, and lexical refinement, ensuring stable and consistent interaction.
  Furthermore, the framework is designed to support future integration of transformer-based gloss-free sign language models, enabling scalable word-level and sentence-level semantic understanding. Experimental results demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed system in grounding sign-derived instructions into precise robotic actions under diverse interaction scenarios. These results highlight the potential of the framework to advance accessible, scalable, and multimodal embodied intelligence.

AI.tldr: Gloss-free, real-time sign-to-instruction VLA framework enabling alphabet-level finger-spelling control for robots; reduces annotation cost, avoids gloss loss, and supports scalable multimodal interaction with potential for transformer-based models.
AI.motivation: Reduce annotation cost and information loss from gloss representations; enable natural, reliable, and scalable sign-language–driven human-robot interaction suitable for safety-critical embodied environments.
AI.method: A sign-to-command pipeline that maps continuous sign gestures to semantic instructions without gloss annotations, using alphabet-level finger spelling. Key steps include geometric normalization, temporal smoothing, and lexical refinement to produce stable, low-latency commands. The design also anticipates future integration of transformer-based gloss-free sign-language models for word- and sentence-level understanding.
AI.result: Experimentally shows grounding sign-derived instructions into precise robotic actions across diverse interaction scenarios with robust, low-latency performance, demonstrating feasibility and effectiveness of gloss-free VLA for HRI.
AI.conclusion: The work advances accessible, scalable, multimodal embodied intelligence by enabling gloss-free sign-language grounding for robot control, with a clear pathway to richer semantic understanding (word/sentence level) via transformer-based models.

title: CLIP Is Shortsighted: Paying Attention Beyond the First Sentence
comment: 19 pages, 13 figures, to be published in the CVPR 2026 proceedings
summary: CLIP models learn transferable multi-modal features via image-text contrastive learning on internet-scale data. They are widely used in zero-shot classification, multi-modal retrieval, text-to-image diffusion, and as image encoders in large vision-language models. However, CLIP's pretraining is dominated by images paired with short captions, biasing the model toward encoding simple descriptions of salient objects and leading to coarse alignment on complex scenes and dense descriptions. While recent work mitigates this by fine-tuning on small-scale long-caption datasets, we identify an important common bias: both human- and LLM-generated long captions typically begin with a one-sentence summary followed by a detailed description. We show that this acts as a shortcut during training, concentrating attention on the opening sentence and early tokens and weakening alignment over the rest of the caption.
