To resolve this, we introduce DeBias-CLIP, which removes the summary sentence during training and applies sentence sub-sampling and text token padding to distribute supervision across all token positions. DeBias-CLIP achieves state-of-the-art long-text retrieval, improves short-text retrieval, and is less sensitive to sentence order permutations. It is a drop-in replacement for Long-CLIP with no additional trainable parameters.

AI.tldr: Debias CLIP by removing the opening summary sentence during training and rebalancing supervision across caption tokens; achieves state-of-the-art long-text retrieval and robust short-text performance with no extra parameters.
AI.motivation: Long captions often start with a one-sentence summary; this shortcut biases CLIP training toward the opening sentence, weakening alignment over the full caption and in dense scenes. Long-caption methods that don’t address this still inherit the shortcut bias.
AI.method: Drop-in replacement for Long-CLIP: remove the summary sentence during training, apply sentence sub-sampling and text token padding to distribute supervision across all token positions; no new trainable parameters.
AI.result: State-of-the-art long-text retrieval, improved short-text retrieval, and reduced sensitivity to sentence order permutations.
AI.conclusion: DeBias-CLIP effectively mitigates a common caption bias in CLIP pretraining, providing a robust, parameter-free improvement suitable as a plug-in for long-text retrieval tasks.

title: Deep Sequence Modeling with Quantum Dynamics: Language as a Wave Function
summary: We introduce a sequence modeling framework in which the latent state is a complex-valued wave function evolving on a finite-dimensional Hilbert space under a learned, time-dependent Hamiltonian. Unlike standard recurrent architectures that rely on gating mechanisms to suppress competing hypotheses, our framework utilizes quantum interference: the Hamiltonian steers the phases of complex amplitudes so that conflicting interpretations cancel while compatible ones reinforce. The dynamics are strictly unitary, ensuring that the state norm is preserved exactly at every time step via a Cayley (Crank--Nicolson) discretization. Token probabilities are extracted using the Born rule, a quadratic measurement operator that couples magnitudes and relative phases. Our primary theoretical contribution is a separation theorem characterizing the representational advantage of this readout: we define a family of disambiguation tasks that a complex unitary model of dimension $N$ solves exactly, but which requires a state dimension of $Ω(N^2)$ for any real-valued orthogonal model equipped with a standard affine-softmax readout. This quadratic gap arises because the Born rule implicitly lifts the $N$-dimensional state into the space of rank-one Hermitian matrices, accessing pairwise phase correlations that are inaccessible to linear projections. Finally, we derive a continuity equation for the latent probability mass, yielding conserved pairwise currents that serve as a built-in diagnostic for tracing information flow between dimensions.

AI.tldr: A complex-valued, unitary sequence model evolves via a learned Hamiltonian, using quantum interference for disambiguation. The paper proves a quadratic representational gap compared to real-valued models with standard readouts, via a separation theorem, and introduces a continuity equation for latent currents as an internal diagnostic.
AI.motivation: Address limitations of real-valued RNNs by leveraging complex amplitudes and unitary dynamics to naturally encode interference patterns and preserve norm, enabling a theoretically stronger representational capacity for sequence modeling.
AI.method: Model the latent state as a complex-valued wave function on a finite-dimensional Hilbert space with time-dependent Hamiltonian dynamics implemented via a Cayley (Crank–Nicolson) discretization to ensure exact norm preservation. Readout uses the Born rule. Prove a separation theorem showing a quadratic gap in representational efficiency between complex unitary models and real-valued affine-softmax readouts. Derive a continuity equation for latent probability mass yielding conserved pairwise currents as diagnostics.
AI.result: Theoretical result establishing a quadratic separation: a family of disambiguation tasks solvable exactly by complex unitary models of dimension N but requiring Ω(N^2) state dimension for any real-valued orthogonal model with a standard affine-softmax readout. The analysis attributes this gap to the Born-rule lifting to rank-one Hermitian matrices, capturing pairwise phase correlations inaccessible to linear projections. Also provides a continuity equation with conserved pairwise currents to trace information flow.
AI.conclusion: The framework offers a principled quantum-inspired approach to sequence modeling with provable representational advantages over real-valued counterparts and a built-in diagnostic (latent-current conservation). It highlights the importance of phase-based information in task disambiguation and positions complex unitary dynamics as a compelling direction for future research in neural sequence modeling.

title: How Do Latent Reasoning Methods Perform Under Weak and Strong Supervision?
summary: Latent reasoning has been recently proposed as a reasoning paradigm and performs multi-step reasoning through generating steps in the latent space instead of the textual space. This paradigm enables reasoning beyond discrete language tokens by performing multi-step computation in continuous latent spaces. Although there have been numerous studies focusing on improving the performance of latent reasoning, its internal mechanisms remain not fully investigated. In this work, we conduct a comprehensive analysis of latent reasoning methods to better understand the role and behavior of latent representation in the process. We identify two key issues across latent reasoning methods with different levels of supervision. First, we observe pervasive shortcut behavior, where they achieve high accuracy without relying on latent reasoning. Second, we examine the hypothesis that latent reasoning supports BFS-like exploration in latent space, and find that while latent representations can encode multiple possibilities, the reasoning process does not faithfully implement structured search, but instead exhibits implicit pruning and compression. Finally, our findings reveal a trade-off associated with supervision strength: stronger supervision mitigates shortcut behavior but restricts the ability of latent representations to maintain diverse hypotheses, whereas weaker supervision allows richer latent representations at the cost of increased shortcut behavior.

AI.tldr: Comprehensive empirical analysis of latent reasoning reveals pervasive shortcut behavior and that latent steps do not faithfully implement explicit BFS-like search; a supervision-strength trade-off exists between maintaining diverse latent hypotheses and mitigating shortcuts.
AI.motivation: To understand how latent representations enable multi-step reasoning, and why latent reasoning gains may arise even without true, structured latent search.
AI.method: Systematic analysis of latent reasoning methods under different supervision regimes; probing latent-space dynamics, exploration behavior, and the relationship between supervision strength and reasoning efficacy.
AI.result: Two primary findings: (1) shortcut behavior is pervasive across methods, allowing high accuracy without genuine latent reasoning; (2) latent representations can encode multiple possibilities, but the reasoning process does not implement structured search and instead shows implicit pruning/compression. A trade-off is observed: stronger supervision reduces shortcuts but limits diversity of latent hypotheses, while weaker supervision preserves diversity at the cost of more shortcuts.
AI.conclusion: Supervision strength shapes latent reasoning capabilities; latent reasoning does not inherently implement explicit structured search. Future work should develop mechanisms to enforce or encourage structured latent search and improve metrics to capture latent-space dynamics and hypothesis diversity.

title: Metamorphic Testing of Vision-Language Action-Enabled Robots
summary: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models are multimodal robotic task controllers that, given an instruction and visual inputs, produce a sequence of low-level control actions (or motor commands) enabling a robot to execute the requested task in the physical environment. These systems face the test oracle problem from multiple perspectives. On the one hand, a test oracle must be defined for each instruction prompt, which is a complex and non-generalizable approach. On the other hand, current state-of-the-art oracles typically capture symbolic representations of the world (e.g., robot and object states), enabling the correctness evaluation of a task, but fail to assess other critical aspects, such as the quality with which VLA-enabled robots perform a task. In this paper, we explore whether Metamorphic Testing (MT) can alleviate the test oracle problem in this context. To do so, we propose two metamorphic relation patterns and five metamorphic relations to assess whether changes to the test inputs impact the original trajectory of the VLA-enabled robots. An empirical study involving five VLA models, two simulated robots, and four robotic tasks shows that MT can effectively alleviate the test oracle problem by automatically detecting diverse types of failures, including, but not limited to, uncompleted tasks. More importantly, the proposed MRs are generalizable, making the proposed approach applicable across different VLA models, robots, and tasks, even in the absence of test oracles.

AI.tldr: Metamorphic testing can mitigate the test oracle problem in Vision-Language-Action (VLA) robotic systems by validating trajectory consistency under input transformations using metamorphic relations; an empirical study shows MT detects diverse failures and generalizes across models, robots, and tasks.
AI.motivation: VLA models rely on opaque test oracles that may rely on symbolic world states and instruction prompts, failing to capture execution quality. A robust, generalizable testing approach is needed to assess not just task correctness but also behavior under input perturbations.
AI.method: Introduce two metamorphic relation patterns and five metamorphic relations (MRs) to assess how input changes affect original trajectories. Evaluate on five VLA models, two simulated robots, four robotic tasks to test MT's effectiveness and generalizability.
AI.result: MT successfully detects diverse failures including incomplete tasks; the proposed MRs generalize across different VLA models, robots, and tasks, functioning even without explicit test oracles.
AI.conclusion: Metamorphic testing provides a practical, scalable approach to mitigate the test oracle problem in VLA systems, enabling cross-model, cross-robot, and cross-task validation of task execution quality.

title: SimpleOCR: Rendering Visualized Questions to Teach MLLMs to Read
summary: Despite the rapid advancements in Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs), a critical question regarding their visual grounding mechanism remains unanswered: do these models genuinely ``read'' text embedded in images, or do they merely rely on parametric shortcuts in the text prompt? In this work, we diagnose this issue by introducing the Visualized-Question (VQ) setting, where text queries are rendered directly onto images to structurally mandate visual engagement. Our diagnostic experiments on Qwen2.5-VL reveal a startling capability-utilization gap: despite possessing strong OCR capabilities, models suffer a performance degradation of up to 12.7% in the VQ setting, exposing a deep-seated ``modality laziness.'' To bridge this gap, we propose SimpleOCR, a plug-and-play training strategy that imposes a structural constraint on the learning process. By transforming training samples into the VQ format with randomized styles, SimpleOCR effectively invalidates text-based shortcuts, compelling the model to activate and optimize its visual text extraction pathways. Empirically, SimpleOCR yields robust gains without architectural modifications. On four representative OOD benchmarks, it surpasses the base model by 5.4% and GRPO based on original images by 2.7%, while exhibiting extreme data efficiency, achieving superior performance with 30x fewer samples (8.5K) than recent RL-based methods. Furthermore, its plug-and-play nature allows seamless integration with advanced RL strategies like NoisyRollout to yield complementary improvements. Code is available at https://github.com/aiming-lab/SimpleOCR.

AI.tldr: Diagnoses visual grounding in MLLMs using a Visualized-Question (VQ) setting that renders text on images, reveals a modality laziness gap, and introduces SimpleOCR—a plug-and-play training strategy that enforces visual text extraction, yielding strong OOD gains and data efficiency with compatibility to RL methods.
AI.motivation: Answer whether MLLMs truly read text embedded in images or rely on text-prompt shortcuts; address this via diagnostic visualization and a training strategy that compels visual text extraction.
AI.method: Introduce Visualized-Question (VQ) setting by rendering text queries on images; analyze Qwen2.5-VL to reveal modality laziness; propose SimpleOCR to transform training samples into VQ format with randomized styles (plug-and-play, no architecture changes); demonstrate compatibility with NoisyRollout and RL-based strategies.
AI.result: Observed a performance drop of up to 12.7% in VQ despite strong OCR; SimpleOCR yields robust gains: +5.4% on four OOD benchmarks, +2.7% on GRPO with original images; achieves data efficiency (8.5K samples, ~30x fewer than RL baselines); plug-and-play with NoisyRollout provides complementary gains.
AI.conclusion: SimpleOCR effectively forces visual text extraction pathways, mitigating text-based shortcuts without changing model architecture; a practical, data-efficient approach to improve visual grounding in MLLMs; code released.

title: Orthogonal Weight Modification Enhances Learning Scalability and Convergence Efficiency without Gradient Backpropagation
summary: Recognizing the substantial computational cost of backpropagation (BP), non-BP methods have emerged as attractive alternatives for efficient learning on emerging neuromorphic systems. However, existing non-BP approaches still face critical challenges in efficiency and scalability. Inspired by neural representations and dynamic mechanisms in the brain, we propose a perturbation-based approach called LOw-rank Cluster Orthogonal (LOCO) weight modification. We find that low-rank is an inherent property of perturbation-based algorithms. Under this condition, the orthogonality constraint limits the variance of the node perturbation (NP) gradient estimates and enhances the convergence efficiency. Through extensive evaluations on multiple datasets, LOCO demonstrates the capability to locally train the deepest spiking neural networks to date (more than 10 layers), while exhibiting strong continual learning ability, improved convergence efficiency, and better task performance compared to other brain-inspired non-BP algorithms. Notably, LOCO requires only O(1) parallel time complexity for weight updates, which is significantly lower than that of BP methods. This offers a promising direction for achieving high-performance, real-time, and lifelong learning on neuromorphic systems.

AI.tldr: LOCO is a perturbation-based, low-rank, orthogonal weight-update method for non-backpropagation learning that enables deep spiking networks with efficient, parallel updates.
AI.motivation: Backpropagation is computationally costly for neuromorphic hardware; non-BP approaches must be improved to be scalable, efficient, and capable of lifelong learning.
AI.method: LOCO uses low-rank perturbations with an orthogonality constraint to limit the variance of node perturbation gradient estimates; perturbation-based weight updates (NP) with cluster-based orthogonal modifications.
AI.result: LOCO reportedly trains deepest spiking networks (>10 layers) locally, shows improved convergence efficiency and continual learning, and achieves better task performance than other brain-inspired non-BP methods, with O(1) parallel time complexity for weight updates.
AI.conclusion: LOCO offers a promising path toward high-performance, real-time, lifelong learning on neuromorphic systems, addressing efficiency and scalability challenges of non-BP approaches.

title: A Framework for Assessing AI Agent Decisions and Outcomes in AutoML Pipelines
summary: Agent-based AutoML systems rely on large language models to make complex, multi-stage decisions across data processing, model selection, and evaluation. However, existing evaluation practices remain outcome-centric, focusing primarily on final task performance. Through a review of prior work, we find that none of the surveyed agentic AutoML systems report structured, decision-level evaluation metrics intended for post-hoc assessment of intermediate decision quality. To address this limitation, we propose an Evaluation Agent (EA) that performs decision-centric assessment of AutoML agents without interfering with their execution. The EA is designed as an observer that evaluates intermediate decisions along four dimensions: decision validity, reasoning consistency, model quality risks beyond accuracy, and counterfactual decision impact. Across four proof-of-concept experiments, we demonstrate that the EA can (i) detect faulty decisions with an F1 score of 0.919, (ii) identify reasoning inconsistencies independent of final outcomes, and (iii) attribute downstream performance changes to agent decisions, revealing impacts ranging from -4.9\% to +8.3\% in final metrics. These results illustrate how decision-centric evaluation exposes failure modes that are invisible to outcome-only metrics. Our work reframes the evaluation of agentic AutoML systems from an outcome-based perspective to one that audits agent decisions, offering a foundation for reliable, interpretable, and governable autonomous ML systems.

AI.tldr: Proposes an Evaluation Agent (EA) for decision-centric, post-hoc auditing of agentic AutoML systems; demonstrates capability to assess intermediate decisions and their impact on final performance across four experiments.
AI.motivation: Address the gap in evaluation practices which focus on outcome performance; need structured, decision-level metrics to diagnose intermediate decision quality and improve reliability, interpretability, and governance of autonomous ML.
AI.method: Introduce EA as an observer evaluating intermediate decisions along four dimensions: decision validity, reasoning consistency, model quality risks beyond accuracy, and counterfactual decision impact; conduct four proof-of-concept experiments to validate.
AI.result: EA detects faulty decisions with F1=0.919; uncovers reasoning inconsistencies independent of outcomes; attributes downstream performance changes to agent decisions, with effects ranging -4.9% to +8.3% in final metrics.
AI.conclusion: Decision-centric evaluation reveals failure modes invisible to outcome-only metrics and provides a foundation for reliable, interpretable, and governable autonomous ML systems.

title: Designing Robots for Families: In-Situ Prototyping for Contextual Reminders on Family Routines
comment: Proceedings of the 21st ACM/IEEE International Conference on Human Robot Interaction (HRI 2026)
summary: Robots are increasingly entering the daily lives of families, yet their successful integration into domestic life remains a challenge. We explore family routines as a critical entry point for understanding how robots might find a sustainable role in everyday family settings. Together with each of the ten families, we co-designed robot interactions and behaviors, and a plan for the robot to support their chosen routines, accounting for contextual factors such as timing, participants, locations, and the activities in the environment. We then designed, prototyped, and deployed a mobile social robot as a four-day, in-home user study. Families welcomed the robot's reminders, with parents especially appreciating the offloading of some reminding tasks. At the same time, interviews revealed tensions around timing, authority, and family dynamics, highlighting the complexity of integrating robots into households beyond the immediate task of reminders. Based on these insights, we offer design implications for robot-facilitated contextual reminders and discuss broader considerations for designing robots for family settings.

AI.tldr: Co-design and in-home deployment study investigating how a mobile social robot supports family routines via contextual reminders; reveals design tensions around timing, authority, and family dynamics.
AI.motivation: To understand how to integrate domestic robots into everyday family life by focusing on routines and context-aware reminders, rather than generic tasks.
AI.method: Ten families co-design interactions and plans; prototyping and deploying a mobile social robot for four days in homes; qualitative data from interviews.
AI.result: Reminders were welcomed; parents appreciated offloading; tensions on timing and authority; complexity of family dynamics; design implications for contextual reminders and family settings.
AI.conclusion: Context-aware reminders have potential but must account for social context; provide guidelines for designing robots for households and their routines; consider broader family dynamics beyond task reminders.

title: Exploring Multimodal LMMs for Online Episodic Memory Question Answering on the Edge
summary: We investigate the feasibility of using Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) for real-time online episodic memory question answering. While cloud offloading is common, it raises privacy and latency concerns for wearable assistants, hence we investigate implementation on the edge. We integrated streaming constraints into our question answering pipeline, which is structured into two asynchronous threads: a Descriptor Thread that continuously converts video into a lightweight textual memory, and a Question Answering (QA) Thread that reasons over the textual memory to answer queries. Experiments on the QAEgo4D-Closed benchmark analyze the performance of Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) within strict resource boundaries, showing promising results also when compared to clound-based solutions. Specifically, an end-to-end configuration running on a consumer-grade 8GB GPU achieves 51.76% accuracy with a Time-To-First-Token (TTFT) of 0.41s. Scaling to a local enterprise-grade server yields 54.40% accuracy with a TTFT of 0.88s. In comparison, a cloud-based solution obtains an accuracy of 56.00%. These competitive results highlight the potential of edge-based solutions for privacy-preserving episodic memory retrieval.

AI.tldr: Edge-based Multimodal LLMs (MLLMs) for real-time episodic memory QA on user devices with streaming constraints, featuring a two-thread architecture (Descriptor to memory, QA to reason). Results show edge parity with cloud under privacy constraints: 8GB GPU yields 51.76% accuracy (TTFT 0.41s); enterprise server yields 54.40% accuracy (TTFT 0.88s); cloud baseline around 56% accuracy.
AI.motivation: Privacy and latency concerns of cloud offloading for wearable assistants; need for privacy-preserving, real-time episodic memory querying on edge devices.
AI.method: Two asynchronous threads: Descriptor Thread converts video into lightweight textual memory; QA Thread reasons over textual memory to answer queries. Evaluation on QAEgo4D-Closed benchmark under strict resource constraints, comparing edge setups (consumer-grade GPU and enterprise-grade server) to cloud-based solution.
AI.result: End-to-end edge configurations achieve 51.76% (TTFT 0.41s) on 8GB GPU and 54.40% (TTFT 0.88s) on enterprise server; cloud solution achieves 56.00% accuracy. Demonstrates competitive edge-based episodic memory retrieval under privacy constraints.
AI.conclusion: Edge-based episodic memory QA with streaming constraints is a viable, privacy-preserving alternative to cloud offloading, delivering competitive performance with meaningful latency reductions on edge hardware.

title: Code World Models for Parameter Control in Evolutionary Algorithms
summary: Can an LLM learn how an optimizer behaves -- and use that knowledge to control it? We extend Code World Models (CWMs), LLM-synthesized Python programs that predict environment dynamics, from deterministic games to stochastic combinatorial optimization. Given suboptimal trajectories of $(1{+}1)$-$\text{RLS}_k$, the LLM synthesizes a simulator of the optimizer's dynamics; greedy planning over this simulator then selects the mutation strength $k$ at each step. On \lo{} and \onemax{}, CWM-greedy performs within 6\% of the theoretically optimal policy -- without ever seeing optimal-policy trajectories. On \jump{$_k$}, where a deceptive valley causes all adaptive baselines to fail (0\% success rate), CWM-greedy achieves 100\% success rate -- without any collection policy using oracle knowledge of the gap parameter. On the NK-Landscape, where no closed-form model exists, CWM-greedy outperforms all baselines across fifteen independently generated instances ($36.94$ vs.\ $36.32$; $p<0.001$) when the prompt includes empirical transition statistics. The CWM also outperforms DQN in sample efficiency (200 offline trajectories vs.\ 500 online episodes), success rate (100\% vs.\ 58\%), and generalization ($k{=}3$: 78\% vs.\ 0\%). Robustness experiments confirm stable synthesis across 5 independent runs.

AI.tldr: LLM-synthesized Code World Models learn optimizer dynamics from suboptimal trajectories and enable near-optimal or optimal mutation-control via planning; strong results on deterministic and stochastic benchmarks (including deceptive valleys and NK-landscapes); outperforms DQN in sample efficiency and generalization.
AI.motivation: Leverage LLMs to induce a controllable, differentiable simulator of optimizer behavior that can be planned over, enabling data-efficient control in stochastic combinatorial optimization without requiring optimal-policy data.
AI.method: Extend Code World Models (CWMs) to stochastic optimization. Given suboptimal trajectories of (1+1)-RLS_k, an LLM synthesizes a simulator of the optimizer's dynamics. Greedy planning over this simulator selects the mutation strength k at each step. Prompts include empirical transition statistics. Evaluations span lo and onemax (near-optimal performance), Jump_k (100% success where baselines fail), and NK-Landscape (no closed-form model; 15 instances; comparison to baselines and DQN; offline vs online data).
AI.result: CWM-greedy attains within 6% of the optimal policy on lo/onemax; achieves 100% success on Jump_k with no oracle gap knowledge; on NK-Landscape it outperforms baselines (36.94 vs 36.32, p<0.001) when transition statistics are included; outperforms DQN in sample efficiency (200 offline trajectories vs 500 online episodes), success rate (100% vs 58%), and generalization (k=3: 78% vs 0%). Robust across 5 independent runs.
AI.conclusion: LLM-synthesized dynamics models enable effective, sample-efficient, planning-based control of optimizer behavior in stochastic combinatorial optimization. The approach generalizes to settings without closed-form models and shows strong robustness; suggests potential for broader model-based optimization with LLMs.

title: CWM: Contrastive World Models for Action Feasibility Learning in Embodied Agent Pipelines
summary: A reliable action feasibility scorer is a critical bottleneck in embodied agent pipelines: before any planning or reasoning occurs, the agent must identify which candidate actions are physically executable in the current state. Existing approaches use supervised fine-tuning (SFT) to train action scorers, but SFT treats each candidate independently and does not explicitly teach the model to discriminate between actions that are physically correct and those that are subtly wrong. We propose the Contrastive World Model (CWM), which fine-tunes a large language model (LLM) as an action scorer using an InfoNCE contrastive objective with hard-mined negative examples. The key idea is to push valid actions away from invalid ones in scoring space, with special emphasis on hard negatives: semantically similar but physically incompatible candidates. We evaluate CWM on the ScienceWorld benchmark through two studies. First, an intrinsic affordance evaluation on 605 hard-negative test pairs shows that CWM outperforms SFT by +6.76 percentage points on Precision@1 for minimal-edit negatives -- cases where a single word changes the physical outcome -- and achieves a higher AUC-ROC (0.929 vs. 0.906). Second, a live filter characterisation study measures how well CWM ranks gold-path actions against all valid environment actions during task execution. Under out-of-distribution stress conditions, CWM maintains a significantly better safety margin (-2.39) than SFT (-3.96), indicating that the gold action is ranked closer to the top. These results support the hypothesis that contrastive training induces representations that capture physical feasibility more faithfully than SFT alone.

AI.tldr: Introduces Contrastive World Model (CWM) for action feasibility scoring using InfoNCE with hard negatives; outperforms SFT on intrinsic and live-filter evaluations in ScienceWorld.
AI.motivation: SFT-based scorers treat candidate actions independently and struggle to discriminate subtly incorrect actions; a contrastive objective is needed to separate feasible from infeasible actions, especially near-miss cases.
AI.method: Fine-tune an LLM as an action scorer using an InfoNCE contrastive loss with hard-mined negatives; evaluate on ScienceWorld with intrinsic (605 hard-negative pairs) and live-filter (task execution, OOD stress) studies.
AI.result: Intrinsic: Precision@1 improves by +6.76 percentage points for minimal-edit negatives; AUC-ROC rises to 0.929 from 0.906. Live-filter: under OOD stress, safety margin improves to -2.39 (vs -3.96 for SFT), indicating top-ranked gold action.
AI.conclusion: Contrastive training yields representations that better encode physical feasibility than SFT, improving action feasibility scoring in embodied AI pipelines.

title: Rethinking the Practicality of Vision-language-action Model: A Comprehensive Benchmark and An Improved Baseline
summary: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models have emerged as a generalist robotic agent. However, existing VLAs are hindered by excessive parameter scales, prohibitive pre-training requirements, and limited applicability to diverse embodiments. To improve the practicality of VLAs, we propose a comprehensive benchmark and an improved baseline. First, we propose CEBench, a new benchmark spanning diverse embodiments in both simulation and the real world with consideration of domain randomization. We collect 14.4k simulated trajectories and 1.6k real-world expert-curated trajectories to support training on CEBench. Second, using CEBench as our testbed, we study three critical aspects of VLAs' practicality and offer several key findings. Informed by these findings, we introduce LLaVA-VLA, a lightweight yet powerful VLA designed for practical deployment on consumer-grade GPUs. Architecturally, it integrates a compact VLM backbone with multi-view perception, proprioceptive tokenization, and action chunking. To eliminate reliance on costly pre-training, LLaVA-VLA adopts a two-stage training paradigm including post-training and fine-tuning. Furthermore, LLaVA-VLA extends the action space to unify navigation and manipulation. Experiments across embodiments demonstrate the capabilities of generalization and versatility of LLaVA-VLA , while real-world mobile manipulation experiments establish it as the first end-to-end VLA model for mobile manipulation. We will open-source all datasets, codes, and checkpoints upon acceptance to foster reproducibility and future research.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces CEBench, a diverse-embodiment benchmark for Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models, and LLaVA-VLA, a lightweight two-stage-trained VLA with extended action space, validated across simulated and real-world tasks including mobile manipulation, and accompanied by open-source releases.
AI.motivation: VLAs currently suffer from large parameter counts, costly pre-training, and limited generalization across diverse embodiments, hindering practical deployment; there is a need for an embodied benchmark and a compact, trainable baseline for consumer hardware.
AI.method: Develop CEBench with 14.4k simulated trajectories and 1.6k real-world expert-curated trajectories, incorporating domain randomization; study three practical aspects of VLAs on this benchmark; introduce LLaVA-VLA with a compact VLM backbone, multi-view perception, proprioceptive tokenization, and action chunking; employ a two-stage training paradigm (post-training and fine-tuning); extend the action space to unify navigation and manipulation; validate generalization across embodiments and perform real-world mobile manipulation experiments; plan to open-source datasets, code, and checkpoints.
AI.result: CEBench provides a practical testbed; LLaVA-VLA demonstrates generalization and versatility across embodiments and achieves end-to-end mobile manipulation in real-world settings; open-source release intended to foster reproducibility and future research.
AI.conclusion: A practical, scalable VLA solution—LLaVA-VLA—achieves broad applicability with reduced pre-training requirements, supported by a diverse benchmark; the work lays groundwork for reproducible research through open datasets and code.

title: MammoWise: Multi-Model Local RAG Pipeline for Mammography Report Generation
comment: arXiv preprint (submitted 25 Feb 2026). Local multi-model pipeline for mammography report generation + classification using prompting, multimodal RAG (ChromaDB), and QLoRA fine-tuning; evaluates MedGemma, LLaVA-Med, Qwen2.5-VL on VinDr-Mammo and DMID; reports BERTScore/ROUGE-L and classification metrics
summary: Screening mammography is high volume, time sensitive, and documentation heavy. Radiologists must translate subtle visual findings into consistent BI-RADS assessments, breast density categories, and structured narrative reports. While recent Vision Language Models (VLMs) enable image-to-text reporting, many rely on closed cloud systems or tightly coupled architectures that limit privacy, reproducibility, and adaptability. We present MammoWise, a local multi-model pipeline that transforms open source VLMs into mammogram report generators and multi-task classifiers. MammoWise supports any Ollama-hosted VLM and mammography dataset, and enables zero-shot, few-shot, and Chain-of-Thought prompting, with optional multimodal Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG) using a vector database for case-specific context. We evaluate MedGemma, LLaVA-Med, and Qwen2.5-VL on VinDr-Mammo and DMID datasets, assessing report quality (BERTScore, ROUGE-L), BI-RADS classification, breast density, and key findings. Report generation is consistently strong and improves with few-shot prompting and RAG. Classification is feasible but sensitive to model and dataset choice. Parameter-efficient fine-tuning (QLoRA) of MedGemma improves reliability, achieving BI-RADS accuracy of 0.7545, density accuracy of 0.8840, and calcification accuracy of 0.9341 while preserving report quality. MammoWise provides a practical and extensible framework for deploying local VLMs for mammography reporting within a unified and reproducible workflow.

AI.tldr: MammoWise enables local, open-source VLMs to generate mammography reports and perform multi-task classification in a privacy-preserving, reproducible pipeline, with few-shot prompting and RAG; validated on VinDr-Mammo and DMID with BI-RADS, density, and calcification accuracy metrics; QLoRA improves reliability.
AI.motivation: Mammography reporting is high-volume, time-sensitive, and documentation-heavy. Cloud-based or tightly coupled VLMs raise privacy, reproducibility, and adaptability concerns.
AI.method: A local pipeline (MammoWise) that leverages Ollama-hosted VLMs to produce reports and classifications. Supports zero-shot, few-shot, Chain-of-Thought prompting, and optional multimodal RAG using a vector database. Evaluation on MedGemma, LLaVA-Med, Qwen2.5-VL using VinDr-Mammo and DMID datasets; metrics include BERTScore, ROUGE-L, BI-RADS, density, calcification. Also applies QLoRA parameter-efficient fine-tuning to MedGemma.
AI.result: Report generation quality is strong and improves with few-shot prompting and RAG. Classification is feasible but model/dataset dependent. QLoRA improves reliability and achieves BI-RADS 0.7545, density 0.8840, calcification 0.9341.
AI.conclusion: MammoWise offers a practical, extensible framework for deploying local VLMs for mammography reporting in a unified, reproducible workflow.

title: Sustainable LLM Inference using Context-Aware Model Switching
summary: Large language models have become central to many AI applications, but their growing energy consumption raises serious sustainability concerns. A key limitation in current AI deployments is the reliance on a one-size-fits-all inference strategy where most systems route every request to the same large model, regardless of task complexity, leading to substantial and unnecessary energy waste. To address this issue, we propose a context-aware model switching approach that dynamically selects an appropriate language model based on query complexity. The proposed system uses a Context-Aware Model Switching for Energy-Efficient LLM Inference that combines caching for repeated queries, rulebased complexity scoring for fast and explainable decisions, machine learning classification to capture semantic intent, and a user-adaptive component that learns from interaction patterns over time. The proposed architecture was evaluated using real conversation workloads and three open-source language models (Gemma3 1B, Gemma3 4B and Qwen3 4B) with different computational costs, measuring energy consumption (via NVML GPU power telemetry), response latency, routing accuracy, and output quality (BERTScore F1) to reflect real-world usage conditions. Experimental results show that the model switching approach can reduce energy consumption by up to 67.5% compared to always using the largest model while maintaining a response quality of 93.6%. In addition, the response time for simple queries also improved significantly by approximately 68%. These results show that model switching inference offers a practical and scalable path toward more energy-efficient and sustainable AI systems, demonstrating that significant efficiency gains can be achieved without major sacrifices in response quality.

AI.tldr: Context-aware model switching reduces energy use in LLM inference by routing queries to appropriately sized models based on complexity, using caching, rule-based scoring, ML-based intent classification, and user adaptation. Evaluated on Gemma3 1B/4B and Qwen3 4B; energy down up to 67.5% with 93.6% output quality; simple queries 68% faster.
AI.motivation: Address the environmental and efficiency concerns of large-scale LLM deployments by avoiding a uniform use of the largest model for all queries; enable energy-efficient, scalable inference without sacrificing quality.
AI.method: A heterogeneous routing architecture combining: (1) caching for repeated queries, (2) rule-based complexity scoring for fast, explainable routing decisions, (3) machine learning classification to capture semantic intent, and (4) a user-adaptive component that learns from interaction over time. Evaluation uses real conversation workloads and three open-source models (Gemma3 1B, Gemma3 4B, Qwen3 4B); measurements include NVML GPU power telemetry, latency, routing accuracy, and output quality (BERTScore F1).
AI.result: Energy consumption reduced up to 67.5% compared with always using the largest model; maintains output quality at 93.6% (BERTScore F1) and improves simple-query latency by ~68%. Demonstrates practicality and scalability of model-switching inference for sustainable AI.
AI.conclusion: Model-switching inference is a practical, scalable approach to achieving energy-efficient LLM deployments without sacrificing substantial output quality.

title: ConstraintBench: Benchmarking LLM Constraint Reasoning on Direct Optimization
comment: Preprint. 10 pages, 1 figure, 6 tables. Benchmark and evaluation code will be publicly released
summary: Large language models are increasingly applied to operational decision-making where the underlying structure is constrained optimization. Existing benchmarks evaluate whether LLMs can formulate optimization problems as solver code, but leave open a complementary question. Can LLMs directly produce correct solutions to fully specified constrained optimization problems without access to a solver? We introduce ConstraintBench, a benchmark for evaluating LLMs on direct constrained optimization across 10 operations research domains, with all ground-truth solutions verified by the Gurobi solver. Each task presents a natural-language scenario with entities, constraints, and an optimization objective; the model must return a structured solution that a deterministic verifier checks against every constraint and the solver-proven optimum. We evaluate six frontier models on 200 tasks and find that feasibility, not optimality, is the primary bottleneck. The best model achieves only 65.0% feasibility, yet feasible solutions average 89 to 96% of the Gurobi-optimal objective. No model exceeds 30.5% on joint feasibility and optimality within 0.1% of the solver reference. Per-domain analysis shows large variation in difficulty, with average feasibility spanning from 85.0% in the facility location domain to 0.8% in the crew assignment domain. Further, systematic failure modes include duration constraint misunderstanding, entity hallucination, and a feasibility-optimality decoupling in facility location and vehicle routing where models achieve high feasibility but 0% optimality. ConstraintBench and all evaluation infrastructure will be publicly released.

AI.tldr: ConstraintBench is a benchmark evaluating whether LLMs can directly solve fully specified constrained optimization problems without a solver, across 10 OR domains, with ground-truth solutions verified by Gurobi. Six frontier models are tested on 200 tasks; feasibility is the main bottleneck. The best model is 65.0% feasible; feasible solutions reach 89-96% of the Gurobi optimum, while joint feasibility+optimality remains below 30.5% in most cases. Domain difficulty varies widely (facility location ~85% feasibility vs crew assignment ~0.8%). Common failure modes include misinterpreting duration constraints, entity hallucination, and a decoupling of feasibility and optimality in some domains. Release of ConstraintBench and infrastructure is planned.
AI.motivation: Existing benchmarks largely assess whether LLMs can produce solver code from optimization problems. The complementary question—whether LLMs can directly produce correct, solver-verified solutions to fully specified constrained optimization problems—remains open. A multi-domain, solver-verified evaluation is needed to measure direct problem-solving capabilities of LLMs in operational decision-making.
AI.method: 10 operations research domains; tasks consist of natural-language scenarios with entities, constraints, and an objective. The model must output a structured solution that a deterministic verifier checks against all constraints and the solver-proven optimum. Evaluated six frontier models on 200 tasks; ground-truth optima provided by Gurobi.
AI.result: Feasibility is the primary bottleneck (best model ~65.0% feasible). Feasible solutions average 89-96% of the Gurobi-optimal objective. Joint feasibility and optimality within 0.1% of solver reference remains <=30.5% in the reported results. Domain variation is large: facility location ~85.0% average feasibility; crew assignment ~0.8%. Systematic failures include duration-constraint misunderstanding, entity hallucination, and a feasibility–optimality decoupling in facility location and vehicle routing.
AI.conclusion: ConstraintBench will be publicly released along with evaluation infrastructure. The study reveals substantial gaps in direct constrained optimization by LLMs, with feasibility as the dominant hurdle and significant domain-dependent variability. Further work is needed to improve constraint understanding and consistency between feasibility and optimality.

title: Does the testing environment matter? Carsickness across on-road, test-track, and driving simulator conditions
summary: Carsickness has gained significant attention with the rise of automated vehicles, prompting extensive research across on-road, test-track, and driving simulator environments to understand its occurrence and develop mitigation strategies. However, the lack of carsickness standardization complicates comparisons across studies and environments. Previous works demonstrate measurement validity between two setups at most (e.g., on-road vs. driving simulator), leaving gaps in multi-environment comparisons. This study investigates the recreation of an on-road motion sickness exposure - previously replicated on a test track - using a motion-based driving simulator. Twenty-eight participants performed an eyes-off-road non-driving task while reporting motion sickness using the Misery Scale during the experiment and the Motion Sickness Assessment Questionnaire afterward. Psychological factors known to influence motion sickness were also assessed. The results present subjective and objective measurements for motion sickness across the considered environments. In this paper, acceleration measurements, objective metrics and subjective motion sickness ratings across environments are compared, highlighting key differences in sickness occurrence for simulator-based research validity. Significantly lower motion sickness scores are reported in the simulator compared to on-road and test-track conditions, due to its limited working envelope to reproduce low-frequency (<0.5 Hz) motions, which are the most provocative for motion sickness.

AI.tldr: Motion-sickness across on-road, test-track, and motion-based driving simulator environments shows the simulator underestimates sickness due to limited low-frequency motion reproduction (<0.5 Hz); emphasizing the need for standardization and cross-environment comparability.
AI.motivation: Lack of standardized motion sickness metrics across environments hinders cross-study comparisons and generalizability of simulator-based findings.
AI.method: 28 participants performed an eyes-off-road non-driving task, reported motion sickness with the Misery Scale during the session and completed the Motion Sickness Assessment Questionnaire afterward; acceleration data and other objective metrics collected; psychological factors known to influence motion sickness also assessed; experiments conducted across three environments: on-road, test-track, and a motion-based driving simulator.
AI.result: Significantly lower motion sickness scores observed in the simulator relative to on-road and test-track conditions; the limited working envelope of the simulator reproduces only low-frequency motions (<0.5 Hz) that are less provocative, accounting for the lower sickness; subjective and objective measures were compared across environments, highlighting authenticity concerns for simulator-based research validity.
AI.conclusion: Findings suggest caution when generalizing simulator-based motion sickness results to real-world driving; standardization or correction for motion-envelope differences is needed to improve cross-environment validity and enable robust cross-study comparisons.

title: Beyond Dominant Patches: Spatial Credit Redistribution For Grounded Vision-Language Models
summary: Vision-language models (VLMs) frequently hallucinate objects absent from the input image. We trace this failure to spatial credit collapse: activation credit concentrating on sparse visual patches in early transformer layers, which suppresses contextual evidence and increases reliance on language priors. We introduce Spatial Credit Redistribution (SCR), a training-free inference-time intervention that redistributes hidden-state activation from high-attention source patches to their context, guided by low-entropy inputs. We evaluate six model families (Chameleon, LLaVA, and Qwen, including both Qwen-VL and Qwen2-VL) at scales of 7B, 13B, and 30B, on POPE and CHAIR benchmarks. SCR reduces hallucination by ~4.7-6.0 percentage points on POPE-Adversarial, cuts CHAIR-s by 3.7-5.2 percentage points (42-51 percent relative), and CHAIR-i by 2.7-4.4 percentage points (44-58 percent relative), and preserves CIDEr within 0.8 percentage points. Gains are largest for low-entropy inputs, consistent with the theoretical framework. SCR incurs only 43-56 ms overhead (small models: +43-46 ms; large models: +54-56 ms), roughly 3-6 times lower than OPERA and VCD and 1.3-1.7 times lower than OVCD (+72 ms), while Pareto-dominating all three on both hallucination rate and CIDEr, making it practical for real-time settings. A controlled ablation confirms that attention-guided source selection is essential: replacing it with uniform random selection reduces hallucination rate gains from ~4.7-6.0 percentage points to only ~2.6-3.4 percentage points, pointing to credit-collapse as the key driver.

AI.tldr: SCR is a training-free, inference-time intervention that redistributes hidden-state activation from high-attention source patches to their surrounding context in vision-language models, mitigating spatial credit collapse and reducing hallucinations with modest latency.
AI.motivation: Vision-language models frequently hallucinate objects due to spatial credit collapse, where activation credit concentrates on sparse patches and suppresses contextual evidence. A real-time, training-free mitigation is needed to improve reliability across model families and scales.
AI.method: Spatial Credit Redistribution (SCR) redirects activation from highly attended source patches to their contextual neighbors, guided by low-entropy inputs. The method is evaluated as a training-free inference-time intervention across six model families (Chameleon, LLaVA, Qwen; including Qwen-VL and Qwen2-VL) at 7B, 13B, and 30B, on POPE and CHAIR benchmarks.
AI.result: SCR reduces hallucination rates by ~4.7–6.0 percentage points on POPE-Adversarial, CHAIR-s by 3.7–5.2 points (42–51% relative), and CHAIR-i by 2.7–4.4 points (44–58% relative); CIDEr is preserved within 0.8 percentage points. Gains are larger for low-entropy inputs. Overhead is 43–56 ms, roughly 3–6x lower than OPERA and VCD and 1.3–1.7x lower than OVCD (+72 ms); SCR Pareto-dominates the three baselines on hallucination and CIDEr. An ablation shows attention-guided source selection is essential; replacing it with uniform random selection reduces gains to ~2.6–3.4 points, indicating credit-collapse as the key driver.
AI.conclusion: SCR provides a practical, real-time mitigation of hallucinations in large vision-language models, achieving substantial reductions while preserving caption quality and incurring modest latency.

title: Entropy-Controlled Flow Matching
summary: Modern vision generators transport a base distribution to data through time-indexed measures, implemented as deterministic flows (ODEs) or stochastic diffusions (SDEs). Despite strong empirical performance, standard flow-matching objectives do not directly control the information geometry of the trajectory, allowing low-entropy bottlenecks that can transiently deplete semantic modes. We propose Entropy-Controlled Flow Matching (ECFM): a constrained variational principle over continuity-equation paths enforcing a global entropy-rate budget d/dt H(mu_t) >= -lambda. ECFM is a convex optimization in Wasserstein space with a KKT/Pontryagin system, and admits a stochastic-control representation equivalent to a Schrodinger bridge with an explicit entropy multiplier. In the pure transport regime, ECFM recovers entropic OT geodesics and Gamma-converges to classical OT as lambda -> 0. We further obtain certificate-style mode-coverage and density-floor guarantees with Lipschitz stability, and construct near-optimal collapse counterexamples for unconstrained flow matching.

AI.tldr: Entropy-Controlled Flow Matching (ECFM) adds a global entropy-rate constraint to time-indexed transport in generative models, enforcing a minimum entropy production to prevent low-entropy bottlenecks and improve mode coverage. It yields a convex Wasserstein-space optimization with a Schrodinger-bridge representation, links to entropic OT in the limit, and provides theoretical guarantees (mode-coverage, density floors, stability).
AI.motivation: To mitigate information-geometric degeneracies and transient mode depletion (low-entropy bottlenecks) observed in deterministic flow or diffusion-based generative trajectories, by enforcing a global entropy budget over time.
AI.method: Formulate a constrained variational principle on continuity-equation paths with a fixed entropy-rate budget d/dt H(mu_t) >= -lambda. Solve as a convex optimization in Wasserstein space with KKT/Pontryagin conditions; derive a Schrodinger-bridge representation with an explicit entropy multiplier; in the pure transport regime, show Gamma-convergence to classical OT as lambda->0.
AI.result: ECFM recovers entropic OT geodesics in the transport limit; it connects to Schrodinger bridge via explicit entropy multiplier; proves entropy-budgeted trajectories ensure mode-coverage and density-floor guarantees with Lipschitz stability; constructs near-optimal collapse counterexamples for unconstrained flow matching; demonstrates Gamma-convergence to OT.
AI.conclusion: Imposes a principled entropy-control on flow-matching trajectories, unifying flow-based and entropy-regularized OT perspectives, yielding theoretical guarantees for mode coverage and stability while recovering classical OT in the zero-budget limit.

title: VeRO: An Evaluation Harness for Agents to Optimize Agents
summary: An important emerging application of coding agents is agent optimization: the iterative improvement of a target agent through edit-execute-evaluate cycles. Despite its relevance, the community lacks a systematic understanding of coding agent performance on this task. Agent optimization differs fundamentally from conventional software engineering: the target agent interleaves deterministic code with stochastic LLM completions, requiring structured capture of both intermediate reasoning and downstream execution outcomes. To address these challenges, we introduce VERO (Versioning, Rewards, and Observations), which provides (1) a reproducible evaluation harness with versioned agent snapshots, budget-controlled evaluation, and structured execution traces, and (2) a benchmark suite of target agents and tasks with reference evaluation procedures. Using VERO, we conduct an empirical study comparing optimizer configurations across tasks and analyzing which modifications reliably improve target agent performance. We release VERO to support research on agent optimization as a core capability for coding agents.

AI.tldr: Proposes VERO, a reproducible evaluation framework and benchmark suite for agent optimization, enabling versioned agent snapshots, budget-controlled evaluation, structured traces, and systematic comparison of optimizer configurations in coding agents.
AI.motivation: To address the lack of systematic understanding of coding agent performance in iterative edit–execute–evaluate cycles, where targets combine deterministic code with stochastic LLM outputs and require capturing intermediate reasoning and execution outcomes.
AI.method: Introduce VERO (Versioning, Rewards, and Observations) comprising: (1) a reproducible evaluation harness with versioned agent snapshots, budget-controlled evaluation, and structured execution traces; (2) a benchmark suite of target agents and tasks with reference evaluation procedures; and an empirical study comparing optimizer configurations across tasks.
AI.result: Empirical study across tasks identifying which optimizer modifications reliably improve target agent performance; demonstration of VERO's utility as a benchmark and evaluation platform; open-sourcing of VERO for future research.
AI.conclusion: VERO provides a reproducible, structured framework for evaluating and benchmarking agent optimization as a core capability for coding agents, enabling systematic comparisons and iteration.

title: SCOPE: Skeleton Graph-Based Computation-Efficient Framework for Autonomous UAV Exploration
comment: This paper has been accepted for publication in the IEEE ROBOTICS AND AUTOMATION LETTERS (RA-L). Please cite the paper using appropriate formats
summary: Autonomous exploration in unknown environments is key for mobile robots, helping them perceive, map, and make decisions in complex areas. However, current methods often rely on frequent global optimization, suffering from high computational latency and trajectory oscillation, especially on resource-constrained edge devices. To address these limitations, we propose SCOPE, a novel framework that incrementally constructs a real-time skeletal graph and introduces Implicit Unknown Region Analysis for efficient spatial reasoning. The planning layer adopts a hierarchical on-demand strategy: the Proximal Planner generates smooth, high-frequency local trajectories, while the Region-Sequence Planner is activated only when necessary to optimize global visitation order. Comparative evaluations in simulation demonstrate that SCOPE achieves competitive exploration performance comparable to state-of-the-art global planners, while reducing computational cost by an average of 86.9%. Real-world experiments further validate the system's robustness and low latency in practical scenarios.

AI.tldr: SCOPE enables real-time, low-latency exploration by incrementally building a skeletal graph and using on-demand planning to balance local smoothness and global visitation efficiency, reducing computation substantially without sacrificing performance.
AI.motivation: To overcome high computational latency and trajectory oscillations of global planners in unknown environments, especially on resource-constrained edge devices.
AI.method: Incremental skeletal graph construction; Implicit Unknown Region Analysis for spatial reasoning; hierarchical on-demand planning with Proximal Planner for local trajectories and Region-Sequence Planner for global visitation.
AI.result: In simulation, SCOPE matches state-of-the-art global planners in exploration performance while lowering computation by ~86.9%; real-world experiments show robustness and low latency.
AI.conclusion: SCOPE offers a scalable, real-time exploration framework that trades some global optimization for on-demand planning, achieving competitive performance with substantially reduced computational cost.

title: Pix2Key: Controllable Open-Vocabulary Retrieval with Semantic Decomposition and Self-Supervised Visual Dictionary Learning
summary: Composed Image Retrieval (CIR) uses a reference image plus a natural-language edit to retrieve images that apply the requested change while preserving other relevant visual content. Classic fusion pipelines typically rely on supervised triplets and can lose fine-grained cues, while recent zero-shot approaches often caption the reference image and merge the caption with the edit, which may miss implicit user intent and return repetitive results. We present Pix2Key, which represents both queries and candidates as open-vocabulary visual dictionaries, enabling intent-aware constraint matching and diversity-aware reranking in a unified embedding space. A self-supervised pretraining component, V-Dict-AE, further improves the dictionary representation using only images, strengthening fine-grained attribute understanding without CIR-specific supervision. On the DFMM-Compose benchmark, Pix2Key improves Recall@10 up to 3.2 points, and adding V-Dict-AE yields an additional 2.3-point gain while improving intent consistency and maintaining high list diversity.

AI.tldr: Pix2Key uses open-vocabulary visual dictionaries to encode queries and candidates for composed image retrieval, with a self-supervised V-Dict-AE pretraining. It improves recall and diversity on DFMM-Compose by aligning intent and enabling constraint-based matching.
AI.motivation: Classic CIR pipelines rely on supervised triplets and can miss fine-grained cues; zero-shot caption-based approaches can misinterpret implicit user intent and yield repetitive results. A unified, intent-aware, diverse retrieval framework is needed.
AI.method: Represent both queries and candidates as open-vocabulary visual dictionaries and perform constraint matching in a shared embedding space; employ diversity-aware reranking. Introduce V-Dict-AE, a self-supervised pretraining component that strengthens dictionary representations using only images, improving fine-grained attribute understanding without CIR-specific supervision.
AI.result: On DFMM-Compose, Pix2Key yields Recall@10 gains up to 3.2 points; adding V-Dict-AE adds another ~2.3-point gain and improves intent consistency while maintaining high list diversity.
AI.conclusion: Dictionary-based, intent-aware CIR with self-supervised dictionary pretraining improves fine-grained attribute understanding, retrieval recall, and result diversity without requiring heavy CIR supervision.

title: Mapping the Landscape of Artificial Intelligence in Life Cycle Assessment Using Large Language Models
summary: Integration of artificial intelligence (AI) into life cycle assessment (LCA) has accelerated in recent years, with numerous studies successfully adapting machine learning algorithms to support various stages of LCA. Despite this rapid development, comprehensive and broad synthesis of AI-LCA research remains limited. To address this gap, this study presents a detailed review of published work at the intersection of AI and LCA, leveraging large language models (LLMs) to identify current trends, emerging themes, and future directions. Our analyses reveal that as LCA research continues to expand, the adoption of AI technologies has grown dramatically, with a noticeable shift toward LLM-driven approaches, continued increases in ML applications, and statistically significant correlations between AI approaches and corresponding LCA stages. By integrating LLM-based text-mining methods with traditional literature review techniques, this study introduces a dynamic and effective framework capable of capturing both high-level research trends and nuanced conceptual patterns (themes) across the field. Collectively, these findings demonstrate the potential of LLM-assisted methodologies to support large-scale, reproducible reviews across broad research domains, while also evaluating pathways for computationally-efficient LCA in the context of rapidly developing AI technologies. In doing so, this work helps LCA practitioners incorporate state-of-the-art tools and timely insights into environmental assessments that can enhance the rigor and quality of sustainability-driven decisions and decision-making processes.

AI.tldr: A comprehensive AI-LCA review using LLM-based text mining to map trends, themes, and future directions, proposing a scalable, reproducible framework.
AI.motivation: Address the limited comprehensive synthesis of AI-LCA research and provide a dynamic, reproducible review framework to inform environmental decision-making.
AI.method: Systematic literature review of AI-LCA publications, augmented with large language model–based text mining integrated with traditional review methods to identify trends and conceptual themes.
AI.result: AI adoption in LCA has grown rapidly, with a shift toward LLM-driven approaches, increasing ML applications, and statistically significant correlations between AI methods and LCA stages; a framework capturing high-level trends and nuanced patterns.
AI.conclusion: LLM-assisted, text-mining–augmented reviews enable scalable, reproducible syntheses across broad domains and support computationally efficient LCA alongside timely, state-of-the-art insights for sustainability decisions.

title: Robust Helicopter Ship Deck Landing With Guaranteed Timing Using Shrinking-Horizon Model Predictive Control
comment: This version was submitted to the American Control Conference 2026 and has been accepted
summary: We present a runtime efficient algorithm for autonomous helicopter landings on moving ship decks based on Shrinking-Horizon Model Predictive Control (SHMPC). First, a suitable planning model capturing the relevant aspects of the full nonlinear helicopter dynamics is derived. Next, we use the SHMPC together with a touchdown controller stage to ensure a pre-specified maneuver time and an associated landing time window despite the presence of disturbances. A high disturbance rejection performance is achieved by designing an ancillary controller with disturbance feedback. Thus, given a target position and time, a safe landing with suitable terminal conditions is be guaranteed if the initial optimization problem is feasible. The efficacy of our approach is shown in simulation where all maneuvers achieve a high landing precision in strong winds while satisfying timing and operational constraints with maximum computation times in the millisecond range.

AI.tldr: SHMPC-based real-time planning and landing framework for autonomous helicopter landings on moving ship decks; derives a nonlinear planning model, uses shrinking-horizon MPC with a touchdown stage and an ancillary disturbance-feedback controller to guarantee safe landing within a time window under disturbances; demonstrates millisecond-scale computation and high precision in simulations.
AI.motivation: Enable robust, timely autonomous landings on mobile platforms (ship decks) under disturbances, with real-time computation constraints.
AI.method: 1) Derive a planning model capturing key nonlinear helicopter dynamics; 2) Apply Shrinking-Horizon Model Predictive Control to enforce a pre-specified maneuver time and landing window; 3) Incorporate a touchdown controller stage; 4) Design an ancillary disturbance-feedback controller for strong disturbance rejection; 5) Guarantee safe landing with appropriate terminal conditions if the initial optimization is feasible.
AI.result: Simulation results show high landing precision in strong winds, adherence to timing and operational constraints, with computation times in the millisecond range.
AI.conclusion: SHMPC-based approach yields a runtime-efficient, disturbance-robust solution for autonomous shipboard helicopter landings with feasibility-based guarantees under the stated conditions.

title: DisQ-HNet: A Disentangled Quantized Half-UNet for Interpretable Multimodal Image Synthesis Applications to Tau-PET Synthesis from T1 and FLAIR MRI
comment: 14 pages, 8 figures, 8 tables; includes PID guided vector quantized latent factorization and sobel edge conditioned Half-UNet decoder
summary: Tau positron emission tomography (tau-PET) provides an in vivo marker of Alzheimer's disease pathology, but cost and limited availability motivate MRI-based alternatives. We introduce DisQ-HNet (DQH), a framework that synthesizes tau-PET from paired T1-weighted and FLAIR MRI while exposing how each modality contributes to the prediction. The method combines (i) a Partial Information Decomposition (PID)-guided, vector-quantized encoder that partitions latent information into redundant, unique, and complementary components, and (ii) a Half-UNet decoder that preserves anatomical detail using pseudo-skip connections conditioned on structural edge cues rather than direct encoder feature reuse. Across multiple baselines (VAE, VQ-VAE, and UNet), DisQ-HNet maintains reconstruction fidelity and better preserves disease-relevant signal for downstream AD tasks, including Braak staging, tau localization, and classification. PID-based Shapley analysis provides modality-specific attribution of synthesized uptake patterns.

AI.tldr: Introduces DisQ-HNet (DQH) for MRI-to-tau-PET synthesis, using PID-guided, vector-quantized latent representations and a Half-UNet decoder with edge-conditioned pseudo-skip connections. Demonstrates maintained reconstruction fidelity and improved disease-relevant signal across Braak staging, tau localization, and classification; provides modality attribution via PID-Shapley analysis.
AI.motivation: Alzheimer's disease tau-PET is informative but costly and not always accessible; MRI-based synthesis could provide a practical surrogate while enabling interpretability.
AI.method: A PID-guided, vector-quantized encoder partitions latent information into redundant, unique, and complementary components. A Half-UNet decoder uses pseudo-skip connections conditioned on structural edge cues (not direct encoder features). The model synthesizes tau-PET from paired T1-weighted and FLAIR MRI. Compared against VAE, VQ-VAE, and UNet baselines. Assessments include Braak staging, tau localization, and classification. PID-based Shapley analysis yields modality-specific attribution.
AI.result: DisQ-HNet preserves reconstruction fidelity and better preserves disease-relevant signals for downstream AD tasks compared to baselines. PID-based analyses provide interpretable, modality-specific contributions to synthesized uptake patterns.
AI.conclusion: The framework delivers interpretable, accurate MRI-based tau-PET synthesis with explicit attribution of modality contributions, potentially expanding accessibility of tau-pathology assessment in AD.

title: Data-Driven Supervision of a Thermal-Hydraulic Process Towards a Physics-Based Digital Twin
summary: The real-time supervision of production processes is a common challenge across several industries. It targets process component monitoring and its predictive maintenance in order to ensure safety, uninterrupted production and maintain high efficiency level. The rise of advanced tools for the simulation of physical systems in addition to data-driven machine learning models offers the possibility to design numerical tools dedicated to efficient system monitoring. In that respect, the digital twin concept presents an adequate framework that proffers solution to these challenges. The main purpose of this paper is to develop such a digital twin dedicated to fault detection and diagnosis in the context of a thermal-hydraulic process supervision. Based on a numerical simulation of the system, in addition to machine learning methods, we propose different modules dedicated to process parameter change detection and their on-line estimation. The proposed fault detection and diagnosis algorithm is validated on a specific test scenario, with single one-off parameter change occurrences in the system. The numerical results show good accuracy in terms of parameter variation localization and the update of their values.

AI.tldr: Digital twin-based fault detection/diagnosis for a thermal-hydraulic process: integrates numerical simulation with machine learning for online parameter-change detection and estimation; validated on a test case with a single-change event; shows accurate localization and parameter updates.
AI.motivation: Real-time supervision of production processes across industries requires reliable monitoring, predictive maintenance, and safety. The digital twin framework offers an integrated, physics-informed and data-driven approach to monitor complex systems and detect faults efficiently.
AI.method: Develop a digital twin for fault detection and diagnosis in a thermal-hydraulic process. Combine numerical simulation with machine learning; implement modules for detecting parameter changes and online estimation; validate the fault detection/diagnosis algorithm on a test scenario with a single parameter change event.
AI.result: Numerical results demonstrate good accuracy in localizing parameter variations and in updating their values.
AI.conclusion: The proposed digital twin framework effectively supports fault detection and diagnosis in a thermal-hydraulic context, illustrating a successful integration of physics-based simulation and data-driven methods for real-time system monitoring.

title: Sapling-NeRF: Geo-Localised Sapling Reconstruction in Forests for Ecological Monitoring
summary: Saplings are key indicators of forest regeneration and overall forest health. However, their fine-scale architectural traits are difficult to capture with existing 3D sensing methods, which make quantitative evaluation difficult. Terrestrial Laser Scanners (TLS), Mobile Laser Scanners (MLS), or traditional photogrammetry approaches poorly reconstruct thin branches, dense foliage, and lack the scale consistency needed for long-term monitoring. Implicit 3D reconstruction methods such as Neural Radiance Fields (NeRF) and 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) are promising alternatives, but cannot recover the true scale of a scene and lack any means to be accurately geo-localised. In this paper, we present a pipeline which fuses NeRF, LiDAR SLAM, and GNSS to enable repeatable, geo-localised ecological monitoring of saplings. Our system proposes a three-level representation: (i) coarse Earth-frame localisation using GNSS, (ii) LiDAR-based SLAM for centimetre-accurate localisation and reconstruction, and (iii) NeRF-derived object-centric dense reconstruction of individual saplings. This approach enables repeatable quantitative evaluation and long-term monitoring of sapling traits. Our experiments in forest plots in Wytham Woods (Oxford, UK) and Evo (Finland) show that stem height, branching patterns, and leaf-to-wood ratios can be captured with increased accuracy as compared to TLS. We demonstrate that accurate stem skeletons and leaf distributions can be measured for saplings with heights between 0.5m and 2m in situ, giving ecologists access to richer structural and quantitative data for analysing forest dynamics.

AI.tldr: A GNSS-LiDAR-SLAM-NeRF fusion pipeline enabling geo-localised, repeatable sapling-level 3D reconstruction for ecological monitoring, outperforming TLS in stem height, branching, and leaf–wood metrics.
AI.motivation: Forest saplings require fine-scale, repeatable measurements of architecture for monitoring; existing 3D sensing methods struggle with thin branches, dense foliage, and scale consistency, hindering long-term ecological analyses.
AI.method: A three-level representation: (i) coarse Earth-frame localisation with GNSS; (ii) centimetre-accurate LiDAR-based SLAM for precise localisation and reconstruction; (iii) NeRF-derived object-centric dense reconstructions of individual saplings, fused into a geo-localised framework.
AI.result: Demonstrates improved accuracy in stem height, branching patterns, and leaf-to-wood ratios over TLS; accurately reconstructed stem skeletons and leaf distributions for saplings from 0.5–2 m height in situ; validated in Wytham Woods (UK) and Evo (Finland), enabling repeatable quantitative evaluation and long-term monitoring.
AI.conclusion: The integrated GNSS–LiDAR SLAM–NeRF pipeline enables repeatable, geo-localised ecological monitoring of saplings with richer structural data, advancing long-term forest dynamics studies.

title: DrivePTS: A Progressive Learning Framework with Textual and Structural Enhancement for Driving Scene Generation
summary: Synthesis of diverse driving scenes serves as a crucial data augmentation technique for validating the robustness and generalizability of autonomous driving systems. Current methods aggregate high-definition (HD) maps and 3D bounding boxes as geometric conditions in diffusion models for conditional scene generation. However, implicit inter-condition dependency causes generation failures when control conditions change independently. Additionally, these methods suffer from insufficient details in both semantic and structural aspects. Specifically, brief and view-invariant captions restrict semantic contexts, resulting in weak background modeling. Meanwhile, the standard denoising loss with uniform spatial weighting neglects foreground structural details, causing visual distortions and blurriness. To address these challenges, we propose DrivePTS, which incorporates three key innovations. Firstly, our framework adopts a progressive learning strategy to mitigate inter-dependency between geometric conditions, reinforced by an explicit mutual information constraint. Secondly, a Vision-Language Model is utilized to generate multi-view hierarchical descriptions across six semantic aspects, providing fine-grained textual guidance. Thirdly, a frequency-guided structure loss is introduced to strengthen the model's sensitivity to high-frequency elements, improving foreground structural fidelity. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our DrivePTS achieves state-of-the-art fidelity and controllability in generating diverse driving scenes. Notably, DrivePTS successfully generates rare scenes where prior methods fail, highlighting its strong generalization ability.

AI.tldr: DrivePTS introduces a three-pronged approach—progressive mutual-information-guided learning, Vision-Language driven multi-view semantic guidance, and frequency-aware structure loss—to improve fidelity, controllability, and detail in diffusion-based driving-scene generation, mitigating inter-condition dependency and enabling rare-scene synthesis.
AI.motivation: Current diffusion-based conditional driving-scene generation using HD maps and 3D boxes suffers from inter-condition dependency and insufficient semantic/structural detail. Brief captions and uniform denoising losses lead to weak background modeling and blurred foreground structures. There is a need for disentangled conditioning, richer semantic guidance, and high-frequency fidelity to enhance realism and controllability.
AI.method: 1) Progressive learning with an explicit mutual information constraint to reduce coupling between geometric conditions. 2) A Vision-Language Model generates multi-view, hierarchical descriptions across six semantic aspects to provide fine-grained textual guidance. 3) A frequency-guided structure loss emphasizes high-frequency components to improve foreground fidelity.
AI.result: The method achieves state-of-the-art fidelity and controllability in generating diverse driving scenes, including rare scenes that prior methods fail to produce, demonstrating strong generalization.
AI.conclusion: DrivePTS effectively addresses inter-condition dependencies, enriches semantic guidance, and sharpens structural details in conditional diffusion-based driving-scene generation, yielding high-fidelity, controllable, and diverse synthetic data for robust autonomous driving systems.

title: AutoQRA: Joint Optimization of Mixed-Precision Quantization and Low-rank Adapters for Efficient LLM Fine-Tuning
summary: Quantization followed by parameter-efficient fine-tuning has emerged as a promising paradigm for downstream adaptation under tight GPU memory constraints. However, this sequential pipeline fails to leverage the intricate interaction between quantization bit-width and LoRA rank.
