Specifically, a carefully optimized quantization allocation with low quantization error does not always translate to strong fine-tuning performance, and different bit-width and rank configurations can lead to significantly varying outcomes under the same memory budget. To address this limitation, we propose AutoQRA, a joint optimization framework that simultaneously optimizes the bit-width and LoRA rank configuration for each layer during the mixed quantized fine-tuning process. To tackle the challenges posed by the large discrete search space and the high evaluation cost associated with frequent fine-tuning iterations, AutoQRA decomposes the optimization process into two stages. First, it first conducts a global multi-fidelity evolutionary search, where the initial population is warm-started by injecting layer-wise importance priors. This stage employs specific operators and a performance model to efficiently screen candidate configurations. Second, trust-region Bayesian optimization is applied to locally refine promising regions of the search space and identify optimal configurations under the given memory budget. This approach enables active compensation for quantization noise in specific layers during training. Experiments show that AutoQRA achieves performance close to full-precision fine-tuning with a memory footprint comparable to uniform 4-bit methods.

AI.tldr: AutoQRA jointly optimizes per-layer quantization bit-width and LoRA rank during mixed-precision fine-tuning, using a two-stage search (global multi-fidelity evolutionary search with priors, followed by trust-region Bayesian optimization) to efficiently explore the discrete space and respect memory budgets.
AI.motivation: Sequentially applying quantization and LoRA fine-tuning ignores interactions between bit-width and rank, so a joint optimization is needed to achieve strong performance under strict memory constraints.
AI.method: A two-stage optimization framework: (1) global multi-fidelity evolutionary search with layer-wise importance priors to prune configurations using a performance model; (2) trust-region Bayesian optimization to locally refine promising regions under a fixed memory budget, enabling compensation for layer-wise quantization noise.
AI.result: AutoQRA achieves performance close to full-precision fine-tuning with a memory footprint comparable to uniform 4-bit methods.
AI.conclusion: Jointly optimizing bit-width and LoRA rank per layer during mixed-precision fine-tuning is effective for memory-constrained adaptation; the proposed two-stage search balances global exploration with local refinement to navigate the large discrete search space.

title: A Mathematical Theory of Agency and Intelligence
summary: To operate reliably under changing conditions, complex systems require feedback on how effectively they use resources, not just whether objectives are met. Current AI systems process vast information to produce sophisticated predictions, yet predictions can appear successful while the underlying interaction with the environment degrades. What is missing is a principled measure of how much of the total information a system deploys is actually shared between its observations, actions, and outcomes. We prove this shared fraction, which we term bipredictability, P, is intrinsic to any interaction, derivable from first principles, and strictly bounded: P can reach unity in quantum systems, P equal to, or smaller than 0.5 in classical systems, and lower once agency (action selection) is introduced. We confirm these bounds in a physical system (double pendulum), reinforcement learning agents, and multi turn LLM conversations. These results distinguish agency from intelligence: agency is the capacity to act on predictions, whereas intelligence additionally requires learning from interaction, self-monitoring of its learning effectiveness, and adapting the scope of observations, actions, and outcomes to restore effective learning. By this definition, current AI systems achieve agency but not intelligence. Inspired by thalamocortical regulation in biological systems, we demonstrate a feedback architecture that monitors P in real time, establishing a prerequisite for adaptive, resilient AI.

AI.tldr: Introduces bipredictability P, the fraction of total information shared among observations, actions, and outcomes in an interaction. Derives intrinsic bounds: P can reach 1 in quantum systems, ≤0.5 in classical systems, and lower with agency. Validates bounds across a double pendulum, RL agents, and multi-turn LLMs. Distinguishes agency (acting on predictions) from intelligence (learning, self-monitoring, adaptive observation/action/outcome scope). Proposes a thalamocortical-inspired feedback architecture to monitor P in real time for adaptive, resilient AI.
AI.motivation: Current AI systems optimize predictions but may deviate from effective environmental interaction. A principled, intrinsic measure of information sharing within the agent–environment loop is needed to assess resource use and learning efficacy under changing conditions.
AI.method: Theoretical derivation from first principles of a shared-information fraction P across observations, actions, and outcomes. Establishes bounds (quantum max 1, classical ≤0.5, further reduced by agency). Empirically validates P in a physical system (double pendulum), reinforcement learning agents, and multi-turn LLM conversations. Develops a real-time monitoring feedback architecture inspired by thalamocortical regulation to track P.
AI.result: P is intrinsic to interaction and provides a quantitative bound on information sharing. In classical systems with no agency, P ≤ 0.5; with agency, P decreases. In quantum systems, P can reach unity. The approach is supported by experiments in a double pendulum, RL agents, and LLM dialogues. Demonstrates that agency and intelligence are distinct concepts, and that monitoring P enables adaptive resilience.
AI.conclusion: Agency is the capacity to act on predictions; intelligence requires learning from interaction, self-monitoring learning effectiveness, and adapting the observed-action-outcome scope. The work motivates an architecture that monitors P in real time to enable adaptive, resilient AI, tying into biological thalamocortical regulation.

title: Pixel2Catch: Multi-Agent Sim-to-Real Transfer for Agile Manipulation with a Single RGB Camera
summary: To catch a thrown object, a robot must be able to perceive the object's motion and generate control actions in a timely manner. Rather than explicitly estimating the object's 3D position, this work focuses on a novel approach that recognizes object motion using pixel-level visual information extracted from a single RGB image. Such visual cues capture changes in the object's position and scale, allowing the policy to reason about the object's motion. Furthermore, to achieve stable learning in a high-DoF system composed of a robot arm equipped with a multi-fingered hand, we design a heterogeneous multi-agent reinforcement learning framework that defines the arm and hand as independent agents with distinct roles. Each agent is trained cooperatively using role-specific observations and rewards, and the learned policies are successfully transferred from simulation to the real world.

AI.tldr: A perception-control approach for catching thrown objects using pixel-level cues from a single RGB image, plus a heterogeneous multi-agent RL framework (arm and hand as separate agents) trained in simulation and transferred to the real world.
AI.motivation: Catching thrown objects requires timely perception of motion; explicit 3D pose estimation is costly and brittle. Leveraging 2D visual cues from a single frame enables fast, geometry-free motion recognition. Stabilizing learning for a high-DoF manipulator (arm+hand) requires coordinated, role-specific multi-agent RL.
AI.method: 1) Recognize object motion from pixel-level cues in a single RGB image by tracking changes in position and scale to infer motion without full 3D reconstruction. 2) Introduce a heterogeneous multi-agent reinforcement learning framework treating the robot arm and the multi-fingered hand as independent agents with distinct observations and rewards, trained cooperatively; policies transferred from simulation to real hardware.
AI.result: Policies learned in simulation were successfully transferred to the real world, demonstrating effective motion recognition from 2D cues and cooperative control between arm and hand for catching.
AI.conclusion: Shows viability of 2D visual-motion cues for dynamic manipulation tasks and the effectiveness of role-based multi-agent RL for coordinating high-DoF manipulators, with successful sim-to-real transfer.

title: SwiftNDC: Fast Neural Depth Correction for High-Fidelity 3D Reconstruction
summary: Depth-guided 3D reconstruction has gained popularity as a fast alternative to optimization-heavy approaches, yet existing methods still suffer from scale drift, multi-view inconsistencies, and the need for substantial refinement to achieve high-fidelity geometry. Here, we propose SwiftNDC, a fast and general framework built around a Neural Depth Correction field that produces cross-view consistent depth maps. From these refined depths, we generate a dense point cloud through back-projection and robust reprojection-error filtering, obtaining a clean and uniformly distributed geometric initialization for downstream reconstruction. This reliable dense geometry substantially accelerates 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) for mesh reconstruction, enabling high-quality surfaces with significantly fewer optimization iterations. For novel-view synthesis, SwiftNDC can also improve 3DGS rendering quality, highlighting the benefits of strong geometric initialization. We conduct a comprehensive study across five datasets, including two for mesh reconstruction, as well as three for novel-view synthesis. SwiftNDC consistently reduces running time for accurate mesh reconstruction and boosts rendering fidelity for view synthesis, demonstrating the effectiveness of combining neural depth refinement with robust geometric initialization for high-fidelity and efficient 3D reconstruction.

AI.tldr: SwiftNDC introduces a Neural Depth Correction field to produce cross-view consistent depth maps, enabling fast, high-fidelity 3D reconstruction via back-projected dense point clouds and robust reprojection filtering, which accelerates 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) for mesh reconstruction and improves novel-view synthesis.
AI.motivation: To overcome scale drift, multi-view inconsistencies, and heavy optimization in depth-based 3D reconstruction, seeking fast, high-quality geometry initialization.
AI.method: Propose Neural Depth Correction (NDC) field to refine depths into cross-view consistent maps, back-project to dense point clouds with reprojection-error filtering, generate reliable geometric initialization, and accelerate 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) for both mesh reconstruction and novel-view synthesis.
AI.result: SwiftNDC reduces running time for accurate mesh reconstruction and improves rendering fidelity in view synthesis across five datasets (two mesh, three view synthesis), demonstrating the value of neural depth refinement plus robust geometric initialization.
AI.conclusion: Neural depth refinement coupled with robust geometric initialization provides fast, high-fidelity 3D reconstruction and improved novel-view rendering by efficiently seeding 3DGS with consistent geometry.

title: CQSA: Byzantine-robust Clustered Quantum Secure Aggregation in Federated Learning
summary: Federated Learning (FL) enables collaborative model training without sharing raw data. However, shared local model updates remain vulnerable to inference and poisoning attacks. Secure aggregation schemes have been proposed to mitigate these attacks. In this work, we aim to understand how these techniques are implemented in quantum-assisted FL. Quantum Secure Aggregation (QSA) has been proposed, offering information-theoretic privacy by encoding client updates into the global phase of multipartite entangled states. Existing QSA protocols, however, rely on a single global Greenberger-Horne-Zeilinger (GHZ) state shared among all participating clients. This design poses fundamental challenges: fidelity of large-scale GHZ states deteriorates rapidly with the increasing number of clients; and (ii) the global aggregation prevents the detection of Byzantine clients. We propose Clustered Quantum Secure Aggregation (CQSA), a modular aggregation framework that reconciles the physical constraints of near-term quantum hardware along with the need for Byzantine-robustness in FL. CQSA randomly partitions the clients into small clusters, each performing local quantum aggregation using high-fidelity, low-qubit GHZ states. The server analyzes statistical relationships between cluster-level aggregates employing common statistical measures such as cosine similarity and Euclidean distance to identify malicious contributions. Through theoretical analysis and simulations under depolarizing noise, we demonstrate that CQSA ensures stable model convergence, achieves superior state fidelity over global QSA.

AI.tldr: CQSA introduces a clustered quantum secure aggregation framework for federated learning to address fidelity degradation and Byzantine-robustness limitations of global QSA. By partitioning clients into small random clusters and performing local quantum aggregation with high-fidelity GHZ states, and by analyzing cluster-level aggregates to detect malicious updates, CQSA achieves stable convergence and higher state fidelity under depolarizing noise than global QSA.
AI.motivation: Address two key limitations of existing quantum secure aggregation (QSA): (i) the use of a single global GHZ state whose fidelity deteriorates with the number of clients, and (ii) the inability to detect Byzantine (malicious) participants due to global aggregation.
AI.method: Randomly partition clients into small clusters; perform local quantum aggregation within each cluster using high-fidelity, low-qubit GHZ states; server analyzes relationships between cluster-level aggregates using statistical measures (cosine similarity, Euclidean distance) to identify malicious contributions; analyze theoretically and via simulations under depolarizing noise.
AI.result: CQSA yields stable model convergence and higher state fidelity compared to global QSA, demonstrating robustness to noise and improved detection of anomalous updates through cluster-level statistics.
AI.conclusion: A modular, hardware-friendly aggregation framework that reconciles near-term quantum hardware constraints with Byzantine-robustness requirements in federated learning, offering practical improvements over global QSA in fidelity and security.

title: Cognitive Models and AI Algorithms Provide Templates for Designing Language Agents
summary: While contemporary large language models (LLMs) are increasingly capable in isolation, there are still many difficult problems that lie beyond the abilities of a single LLM. For such tasks, there is still uncertainty about how best to take many LLMs as parts and combine them into a greater whole. This position paper argues that potential blueprints for designing such modular language agents can be found in the existing literature on cognitive models and artificial intelligence (AI) algorithms. To make this point clear, we formalize the idea of an agent template that specifies roles for individual LLMs and how their functionalities should be composed. We then survey a variety of existing language agents in the literature and highlight their underlying templates derived directly from cognitive models or AI algorithms. By highlighting these designs, we aim to call attention to agent templates inspired by cognitive science and AI as a powerful tool for developing effective, interpretable language agents.

AI.tldr: Proposes a formal agent template framework for modular LLM-based systems, derived from cognitive models and AI algorithms, and surveys existing language agents to map designs to templates, aiming for interpretable, effective modular agents.
AI.motivation: Single LLMs struggle with complex tasks; modular composition of LLMs requires clear templates and mappings to cognitive/AI principles to improve interpretability and performance.
AI.method: Define an agent template specifying roles and composition rules; perform a literature survey of language agents; extract underlying templates and map them to cognitive science/AI algorithm templates.
AI.result: A conceptual framework that identifies and categorizes agent templates used in the literature, illuminating a repertoire of templates rooted in cognitive science/AI; offers a tool for designing more interpretable modular language agents.
AI.conclusion: Agent templates from cognitive science and AI offer a powerful design paradigm for modular LLM agents; promotes interpretability and informed design; invites further empirical exploration.

title: Unleashing the Potential of Diffusion Models for End-to-End Autonomous Driving
summary: Diffusion models have become a popular choice for decision-making tasks in robotics, and more recently, are also being considered for solving autonomous driving tasks. However, their applications and evaluations in autonomous driving remain limited to simulation-based or laboratory settings. The full strength of diffusion models for large-scale, complex real-world settings, such as End-to-End Autonomous Driving (E2E AD), remains underexplored. In this study, we conducted a systematic and large-scale investigation to unleash the potential of the diffusion models as planners for E2E AD, based on a tremendous amount of real-vehicle data and road testing. Through comprehensive and carefully controlled studies, we identify key insights into the diffusion loss space, trajectory representation, and data scaling that significantly impact E2E planning performance. Moreover, we also provide an effective reinforcement learning post-training strategy to further enhance the safety of the learned planner. The resulting diffusion-based learning framework, Hyper Diffusion Planner} (HDP), is deployed on a real-vehicle platform and evaluated across 6 urban driving scenarios and 200 km of real-world testing, achieving a notable 10x performance improvement over the base model. Our work demonstrates that diffusion models, when properly designed and trained, can serve as effective and scalable E2E AD planners for complex, real-world autonomous driving tasks.

AI.tldr: A diffusion-model-based planner for end-to-end autonomous driving (E2E AD), named Hyper Diffusion Planner (HDP), is proposed and validated on real-vehicle data. It analyzes diffusion loss, trajectory representation, and data scaling, and adds an RL post-training safety boost. Real-world tests over 200 km and 6 urban scenarios show ~10x improvement over a base model, demonstrating diffusion models' potential as scalable E2E AD planners when well designed.
AI.motivation: Diffusion models offer promise for planning in robotics and autonomous driving but real-world, large-scale evaluations are scarce. This work aims to (i) understand how diffusion loss space and representation choices affect E2E AD planning, (ii) quantify data scaling effects, and (iii) enhance safety via reinforcement learning post-training, all on a real-vehicle platform.
AI.method: Systematic, large-scale study deploying a diffusion-based planner (HD P). The study investigates diffusion loss space, trajectory representations, and data scaling; integrates a reinforcement learning post-training strategy to improve safety; deploys the resulting HDP on a real-vehicle platform and tests across 6 urban scenarios, totaling 200 km of real-world driving.
AI.result: The HDP achieves about a 10x performance improvement over a base model in real-world testing, indicating diffusion models can serve as effective, scalable E2E AD planners when carefully designed and trained.
AI.conclusion: Diffusion models, properly designed and trained, can function as effective end-to-end autonomous driving planners in real-world settings. The work provides empirical guidance on loss configurations, trajectory representations, data scaling, and safety enhancements, supporting broader adoption of diffusion-style planning for complex driving tasks.

title: Quality-Aware Robust Multi-View Clustering for Heterogeneous Observation Noise
summary: Deep multi-view clustering has achieved remarkable progress but remains vulnerable to complex noise in real-world applications. Existing noisy robust methods predominantly rely on a simplified binary assumption, treating data as either perfectly clean or completely corrupted. This overlooks the prevalent existence of heterogeneous observation noise, where contamination intensity varies continuously across data. To bridge this gap, we propose a novel framework termed Quality-Aware Robust Multi-View Clustering (QARMVC). Specifically, QARMVC employs an information bottleneck mechanism to extract intrinsic semantics for view reconstruction. Leveraging the insight that noise disrupts semantic integrity and impedes reconstruction, we utilize the resulting reconstruction discrepancy to precisely quantify fine-grained contamination intensity and derive instance-level quality scores. These scores are integrated into a hierarchical learning strategy: at the feature level, a quality-weighted contrastive objective is designed to adaptively suppress the propagation of noise; at the fusion level, a high-quality global consensus is constructed via quality-weighted aggregation, which is subsequently utilized to align and rectify local views via mutual information maximization. Extensive experiments on five benchmark datasets demonstrate that QARMVC consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, particularly in scenarios with heterogeneous noise intensities.

AI.tldr: QARMVC is a noise-aware multi-view clustering framework that uses information bottleneck to learn intrinsic semantics, derives instance-level quality scores from reconstruction discrepancy, and applies quality-weighted contrastive and fusion objectives to achieve robust, consensus-driven clustering under heterogeneous noise.
AI.motivation: To address continuous, heterogeneous noise in multi-view data, where existing methods rely on binary clean/corrupted assumptions.
AI.method: Information bottleneck-based semantic extraction; reconstruction discrepancy quantifies contamination; derive per-instance quality scores; feature-level quality-weighted contrastive learning; fusion-level quality-weighted aggregation to form a global consensus; mutual information maximization to align/rectify local views.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on five benchmarks shows consistent superiority over state-of-the-art baselines, especially with heterogeneous noise.
AI.conclusion: Quality-aware, instance-level noise quantification and weighted aggregation improve robustness and consensus in multi-view clustering; potential for broader applicability under real-world noisy data.

title: Prior Knowledge-enhanced Spatio-temporal Epidemic Forecasting
summary: Spatio-temporal epidemic forecasting is critical for public health management, yet existing methods often struggle with insensitivity to weak epidemic signals, over-simplified spatial relations, and unstable parameter estimation. To address these challenges, we propose the Spatio-Temporal priOr-aware Epidemic Predictor (STOEP), a novel hybrid framework that integrates implicit spatio-temporal priors and explicit expert priors. STOEP consists of three key components: (1) Case-aware Adjacency Learning (CAL), which dynamically adjusts mobility-based regional dependencies using historical infection patterns; (2) Space-informed Parameter Estimating (SPE), which employs learnable spatial priors to amplify weak epidemic signals; and (3) Filter-based Mechanistic Forecasting (FMF), which uses an expert-guided adaptive thresholding strategy to regularize epidemic parameters. Extensive experiments on real-world COVID-19 and influenza datasets demonstrate that STOEP outperforms the best baseline by 11.1% in RMSE. The system has been deployed at one provincial CDC in China to facilitate downstream applications.

AI.tldr: Introduces STOEP, a hybrid spatio-temporal epidemic predictor that blends implicit priors with explicit expert priors for robust forecasting. It integrates Case-aware Adjacency Learning, Space-informed Parameter Estimating, and Filter-based Mechanistic Forecasting; achieves 11.1% RMSE improvement on COVID-19 and influenza datasets and is deployed at a provincial CDC in China.
AI.motivation: Public health forecasting needs to detect weak or noisy epidemic signals, better capture spatial dependencies beyond simple graph structures, and stabilize parameter estimation under data sparsity and nonstationarity. Existing methods struggle with sensitivity, spatial modeling, and parameter stability.
AI.method: STOEP comprises three components: (1) Case-aware Adjacency Learning (CAL) that dynamically revises regional adjacency based on mobility and historical infection patterns; (2) Space-informed Parameter Estimating (SPE) that uses learnable spatial priors to amplify weak signals; (3) Filter-based Mechanistic Forecasting (FMF) that applies expert-guided adaptive thresholding to regularize epidemic parameters within a mechanistic framework.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on real-world COVID-19 and influenza datasets shows STOEP outperforms the best baseline by 11.1% in RMSE. The approach has been deployed at a provincial CDC in China to support downstream applications.
AI.conclusion: The proposed STOEP framework effectively combines data-driven priors and expert knowledge to improve robustness and accuracy in spatio-temporal epidemic forecasting, with demonstrated real-world impact and potential for broader deployment.

title: Agentic AI for Intent-driven Optimization in Cell-free O-RAN
comment: Accepted by IEEE International Conference on Communications (ICC), Glasgow, UK, May 2026
summary: Agentic artificial intelligence (AI) is emerging as a key enabler for autonomous radio access networks (RANs), where multiple large language model (LLM)-based agents reason and collaborate to achieve operator-defined intents. The open RAN (O-RAN) architecture enables the deployment and coordination of such agents. However, most existing works consider simple intents handled by independent agents, while complex intents that require coordination among agents remain unexplored. In this paper, we propose an agentic AI framework for intent translation and optimization in cell-free O-RAN. A supervisor agent translates the operator intents into an optimization objective and minimum rate requirements. Based on this information, a user weighting agent retrieves relevant prior experience from a memory module to determine the user priority weights for precoding. If the intent includes an energy-saving objective, then an open radio unit (O-RU) management agent will also be activated to determine the set of active O-RUs by using a deep reinforcement learning (DRL) algorithm. A monitoring agent measures and monitors the user data rates and coordinates with other agents to guarantee the minimum rate requirements are satisfied. To enhance scalability, we adopt a parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) method that enables the same underlying LLM to be used for different agents. Simulation results show that the proposed agentic AI framework reduces the number of active O-RUs by 41.93% when compared with three baseline schemes in energy-saving mode. Using the PEFT method, the proposed framework reduces the memory usage by 92% when compared with deploying separate LLM agents.

AI.tldr: Agentic AI framework for intent translation and optimization in cell-free O-RAN, using multiple LLM-based agents coordinated by a supervisor and a memory-based weighting module; employs PEFT for scalability; achieves notable energy efficiency (41.93% fewer active O-RUs) and memory reductions (92% with PEFT).
AI.motivation: To enable coordinated handling of complex, operator-defined intents in open RAN (O-RAN) and to improve energy efficiency and scalability through multi-agent LLM coordination and memory reuse.
AI.method: Proposes a supervisor agent that converts operator intents into optimization objectives and rate constraints; a memory/weighting agent retrieves prior experiences to set user priority weights for precoding; an energy-saving path activates an O-RU management agent using deep reinforcement learning to select active O-RUs; a monitoring agent ensures minimum rate requirements; employs parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) to reuse a single LLM across agents.
AI.result: Simulation results show a 41.93% reduction in active O-RUs compared with three baselines in energy-saving mode; with PEFT, memory usage is reduced by 92% relative to deploying separate LLM agents.
AI.conclusion: The framework demonstrates the feasibility and benefits of agentic AI for complex, coordinated tasks in cell-free O-RAN, delivering energy efficiency and scalability; PEFT is crucial for practical deployment by mitigating memory and resource demands.

title: LeRobot: An Open-Source Library for End-to-End Robot Learning
summary: Robotics is undergoing a significant transformation powered by advances in high-level control techniques based on machine learning, giving rise to the field of robot learning. Recent progress in robot learning has been accelerated by the increasing availability of affordable teleoperation systems, large-scale openly available datasets, and scalable learning-based methods. However, development in the field of robot learning is often slowed by fragmented, closed-source tools designed to only address specific sub-components within the robotics stack. In this paper, we present \texttt{lerobot}, an open-source library that integrates across the entire robot learning stack, from low-level middleware communication for motor controls to large-scale dataset collection, storage and streaming. The library is designed with a strong focus on real-world robotics, supporting accessible hardware platforms while remaining extensible to new embodiments. It also supports efficient implementations for various state-of-the-art robot learning algorithms from multiple prominent paradigms, as well as a generalized asynchronous inference stack. Unlike traditional pipelines which heavily rely on hand-crafted techniques, \texttt{lerobot} emphasizes scalable learning approaches that improve directly with more data and compute. Designed for accessibility, scalability, and openness, \texttt{lerobot} lowers the barrier to entry for researchers and practitioners to robotics while providing a platform for reproducible, state-of-the-art robot learning.

AI.tldr: Introduces lerobot, an open-source library that unifies the entire robot learning stack—from low-level motor middleware to large-scale data collection and streaming—enabling scalable, data-driven robot learning across hardware platforms with asynchronous inference and reproducibility.
AI.motivation: Robot learning progress is hindered by fragmented, closed-source tools limited to subcomponents. A fully integrated, open, scalable platform is needed to accelerate development, experimentation, and deployment in real-world robotics.
AI.method: Design and implementation of lerobot, an open-source library spanning motor-control middleware, data collection/storage/streaming, and learning components. Supports multiple learning paradigms, scalable asynchronous inference, and cross-embodiment hardware, with a focus on accessibility, extensibility, and reproducibility.
AI.result: The library provides end-to-end integration for robot learning, enabling scalable data-driven methods, accessible hardware support, and reproducible pipelines. It consolidates tools across the stack, facilitating adoption and experimentation, though the abstract does not report empirical benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: lerobot aims to lower entry barriers and promote reusable, scalable, and open robot learning by unifying the stack, supporting real-world deployment, and encouraging reproducible research.

title: Guidance Matters: Rethinking the Evaluation Pitfall for Text-to-Image Generation
summary: Classifier-free guidance (CFG) has helped diffusion models achieve great conditional generation in various fields. Recently, more diffusion guidance methods have emerged with improved generation quality and human preference. However, can these emerging diffusion guidance methods really achieve solid and significant improvements? In this paper, we rethink recent progress on diffusion guidance. Our work mainly consists of four contributions. First, we reveal a critical evaluation pitfall that common human preference models exhibit a strong bias towards large guidance scales. Simply increasing the CFG scale can easily improve quantitative evaluation scores due to strong semantic alignment, even if image quality is severely damaged (e.g., oversaturation and artifacts). Second, we introduce a novel guidance-aware evaluation (GA-Eval) framework that employs effective guidance scale calibration to enable fair comparison between current guidance methods and CFG by identifying the effects orthogonal and parallel to CFG effects. Third, motivated by the evaluation pitfall, we design Transcendent Diffusion Guidance (TDG) method that can significantly improve human preference scores in the conventional evaluation framework but actually does not work in practice. Fourth, in extensive experiments, we empirically evaluate recent eight diffusion guidance methods within the conventional evaluation framework and the proposed GA-Eval framework. Notably, simply increasing the CFG scales can compete with most studied diffusion guidance methods, while all methods suffer severely from winning rate degradation over standard CFG. Our work would strongly motivate the community to rethink the evaluation paradigm and future directions of this field.

AI.tldr: Evaluation reveals a bias pitfall in diffusion guidance: larger CFG scales can inflate scores regardless of image quality; GA-Eval framework for fair comparison; TDG exploits pitfall but fails in practice; CFG scales often rival guidance methods; calls for reevaluating evaluation paradigms.
AI.motivation: Identify and address a bias in current evaluation practices for diffusion guidance methods, where human preference models favor large guidance scales, distorting judgments of method effectiveness.
AI.method: 1) Analyze eight diffusion guidance methods under conventional and GA-Eval frameworks. 2) Propose GA-Eval with guidance-scale calibration to decouple CFG effects from other guidance methods. 3) Design Transcendent Diffusion Guidance (TDG) to exploit the pitfall. 4) Extensive experiments comparing methods across frameworks.
AI.result: Under conventional evaluation, simply increasing CFG scales can match or outperform many guidance methods; however, this does not reflect true quality as it degrades image fidelity. GA-Eval reveals orthogonal/parallel effects to CFG, allowing fair comparisons. TDG improves scores in conventional metrics but fails in practice. Overall, most methods lose winning rate against standard CFG, highlighting evaluation pitfalls.
AI.conclusion: The community should rethink evaluation paradigms for diffusion guidance and focus on fair, scale-calibrated assessments to guide future directions, as CFG remains a strong baseline.

title: Support Tokens, Stability Margins, and a New Foundation for Robust LLMs
summary:
Self-attention is usually described as a flexible, content-adaptive way to mix a token with information from its past. We re-interpret causal self-attention transformers, the backbone of modern foundation models, within a probabilistic framework, much like how classical PCA is extended to probabilistic PCA. However, this re-formulation reveals a surprising and deeper structural insight: due to a change-of-variables phenomenon, a barrier constraint emerges on the self-attention parameters. This induces a highly structured geometry on the token space, providing theoretical insights into the dynamics of LLM decoding. This reveals a boundary where attention becomes ill-conditioned, leading to a margin interpretation similar to classical support vector machines. Just like support vectors, this naturally gives rise to the concept of support tokens.
  Furthermore, we show that LLMs can be interpreted as a stochastic process over the power set of the token space, providing a rigorous probabilistic framework for sequence modeling. We propose a Bayesian framework and derive a MAP estimation objective that requires only a minimal modification to standard LLM training: the addition of a smooth log-barrier penalty to the usual cross-entropy loss. We demonstrate that this provides more robust models without sacrificing out-of-sample accuracy and that it is straightforward to incorporate in practice.

AI.tldr: A probabilistic reinterpretation of causal self-attention reveals barrier constraints and a structured token geometry, introduces 'support tokens', and proposes a Bayesian MAP objective with a smooth log-barrier penalty for robust training with minimal changes.
AI.motivation: To illuminate LLM decoding dynamics by casting attention as probabilistic sequence modeling, uncover theoretical limits, and connect to margins and support vectors.
AI.method: Reformulate causal self-attention within a probabilistic framework (analogous to probabilistic PCA), derive a change-of-variables barrier on attention parameters, model LLMs as stochastic processes over the power set of tokens, and propose a MAP objective with a smooth log-barrier penalty added to cross-entropy.
AI.result: Identifies a barrier-induced structured geometry and a margin interpretation; introduces the concept of 'support tokens'; shows robustness gains without sacrificing out-of-sample accuracy and presents a practical, easily integrable training modification.
AI.conclusion: Provides a rigorous probabilistic foundation for LLM sequence modeling, explains attention conditioning through barrier geometry, and offers an actionable training modification to enhance robustness with minimal changes.

title: Requesting Expert Reasoning: Augmenting LLM Agents with Learned Collaborative Intervention
summary: Large Language Model (LLM) based agents excel at general reasoning but often fail in specialized domains where success hinges on long-tail knowledge absent from their training data. While human experts can provide this missing knowledge, their guidance is often unstructured and unreliable, making its direct integration into an agent's plan problematic. To address this, we introduce AHCE (Active Human-Augmented Challenge Engagement), a framework for on-demand Human-AI collaboration. At its core, the Human Feedback Module (HFM) employs a learned policy to treat the human expert as an interactive reasoning tool. Extensive experiments in Minecraft demonstrate the framework's effectiveness, increasing task success rates by 32% on normal difficulty tasks and nearly 70% on highly difficult tasks, all with minimal human intervention. Our work demonstrates that successfully augmenting agents requires learning how to request expert reasoning, moving beyond simple requests for help.

AI.tldr: AHCE introduces a learned Human Feedback Module to treat human experts as interactive reasoning tools, boosting LLM agents' performance in Minecraft with minimal supervision.
AI.motivation: LLMs excel at general reasoning but falter in domains requiring long-tail knowledge; human guidance is valuable but often unstructured and hard to integrate.
AI.method: Propose AHCE framework; implement a learned policy via the Human Feedback Module to actively solicit expert reasoning; evaluate on Minecraft across task difficulties.
AI.result: Task success rates increase by 32% on normal tasks and ~70% on highly difficult tasks, with minimal human intervention.
AI.conclusion: Effective on-demand human augmentation requires learning when and how to request expert reasoning, not just asking for help; the approach shows scalable improvement in specialized tasks.

title: Performance and Experimental Analysis of Strain-based Models for Continuum Robots
summary: Although strain-based models have been widely adopted in robotics, no comparison beyond the uniform bending test is commonly recognized to assess their performance. In addition, the increasing effort in prototyping continuum robots highlights the need to assess the applicability of these models and the necessity of comprehensive performance evaluation. To address this gap, this work investigates the shape reconstruction abilities of a third-order strain interpolation method, examining its ability to capture both individual and combined deformation effects. These results are compared and discussed against the Geometric-Variable Strain approach. Subsequently, simulation results are experimentally verified by reshaping a slender rod while recording the resulting configurations using cameras. The rod configuration is imposed using a manipulator displacing one of its tips and extracted through reflective markers, without the aid of any other external sensor -- i.e. strain gauges or wrench sensors placed along the rod. The experiments demonstrate good agreement between the model predictions and observed shapes, with average error of 0.58% of the rod length and average computational time of 0.32s per configuration, outperforming existing models.

AI.tldr: Third-order strain interpolation for continuum-robot shape reconstruction is evaluated against Geometric-Variable Strain; demonstrates good agreement with experimental shapes using minimal sensing (camera-based, no strain gauges); 0.58% rod-length error, 0.32s per configuration, outperforming existing models.
AI.motivation: There is a lack of comprehensive performance evaluation for strain-based models beyond simple uniform bending tests, especially as continuum-robot prototyping increases. The study aims to assess the applicability and performance of a third-order strain interpolation method and compare it to Geometric-Variable Strain across individual and combined deformations.
AI.method: The authors compare a third-order strain interpolation method with the Geometric-Variable Strain approach. They perform simulation-based assessments and then experimentally validate by reshaping a slender rod using a manipulator to impose tip displacement. Configurations are recorded via cameras and reflective markers, without strain gauges or wrench sensors. Shape reconstruction is compared to observed shapes, and computational time is measured.
AI.result: The models show good agreement with observed shapes. The average error is 0.58% of the rod length, and the average computational time per configuration is 0.32 seconds, indicating faster performance and improved accuracy over existing models.
AI.conclusion: Third-order strain interpolation is effective for shape reconstruction of slender rods in continuum-robot applications, capturing both individual and combined deformation effects. It provides accurate results with efficient computation and can operate sensorlessly in verification, outperforming the Geometric-Variable Strain approach under the tested conditions.

title: GIFSplat: Generative Prior-Guided Iterative Feed-Forward 3D Gaussian Splatting from Sparse Views
summary: Feed-forward 3D reconstruction offers substantial runtime advantages over per-scene optimization, which remains slow at inference and often fragile under sparse views. However, existing feed-forward methods still have potential for further performance gains, especially for out-of-domain data, and struggle to retain second-level inference time once a generative prior is introduced. These limitations stem from the one-shot prediction paradigm in existing feed-forward pipeline: models are strictly bounded by capacity, lack inference-time refinement, and are ill-suited for continuously injecting generative priors. We introduce GIFSplat, a purely feed-forward iterative refinement framework for 3D Gaussian Splatting from sparse unposed views. A small number of forward-only residual updates progressively refine current 3D scene using rendering evidence, achieve favorable balance between efficiency and quality. Furthermore, we distill a frozen diffusion prior into Gaussian-level cues from enhanced novel renderings without gradient backpropagation or ever-increasing view-set expansion, thereby enabling per-scene adaptation with generative prior while preserving feed-forward efficiency. Across DL3DV, RealEstate10K, and DTU, GIFSplat consistently outperforms state-of-the-art feed-forward baselines, improving PSNR by up to +2.1 dB, and it maintains second-scale inference time without requiring camera poses or any test-time gradient optimization.

AI.tldr: GIFSplat is a purely feed-forward iterative refinement framework for 3D Gaussian Splatting from sparse unposed views that distills a frozen diffusion prior into Gaussian cues, enabling per-scene adaptation while preserving feed-forward efficiency and achieving second-scale inference time.
AI.motivation: Existing feed-forward 3D reconstruction methods rely on a one-shot prediction paradigm, which is limited by model capacity, lacks inferences-time refinement, and struggles to incorporate generative priors; this harms performance on out-of-domain data and stabilizes runtime.
AI.method: A small number of forward-only residual updates progressively refine the 3D scene using rendering evidence. It distills a frozen diffusion prior into Gaussian-level cues from enhanced novel renderings without gradient backpropagation. The approach avoids camera poses and test-time gradient optimization, maintaining feed-forward efficiency while enabling per-scene adaptation with generative priors.
AI.result: GIFSplat outperforms state-of-the-art feed-forward baselines on DL3DV, RealEstate10K, and DTU, delivering up to +2.1 dB PSNR improvements. It preserves second-scale inference time and does not require camera poses or test-time gradient optimization.
AI.conclusion: The method demonstrates that feed-forward iterative refinement with an integrated generative prior can achieve high-quality, robust 3D reconstruction efficiently, addressing out-of-domain performance and runtime constraints while supporting per-scene adaptation.

title: Positional-aware Spatio-Temporal Network for Large-Scale Traffic Prediction
comment: Accepted for the 104th Transportation Research Board (TRB) Annual Meeting in 2025
summary: Traffic flow forecasting has emerged as an indispensable mission for daily life, which is required to utilize the spatiotemporal relationship between each location within a time period under a graph structure to predict future flow. However, the large travel demand for broader geographical areas and longer time spans requires models to distinguish each node clearly and possess a holistic view of the history, which has been paid less attention to in prior works. Furthermore, increasing sizes of data hinder the deployment of most models in real application environments. To this end, in this paper, we propose a lightweight Positional-aware Spatio-Temporal Network (PASTN) to effectively capture both temporal and spatial complexities in an end-to-end manner. PASTN introduces positional-aware embeddings to separate each node's representation, while also utilizing a temporal attention module to improve the long-range perception of current models. Extensive experiments verify the effectiveness and efficiency of PASTN across datasets of various scales (county, megalopolis and state). Further analysis demonstrates the efficacy of newly introduced modules either.

AI.tldr: A lightweight, end-to-end spatio-temporal network (PASTN) with positional-aware node embeddings and a temporal attention module for traffic flow forecasting, achieving strong performance and efficiency across multiple scales.
AI.motivation: Prior work underutilizes explicit node differentiation in large graphs and often lacks long-range temporal perception, while growing data sizes challenge deployment; a compact, scalable model that jointly captures spatial and temporal dependencies is needed.
AI.method: Introduce PASTN, which uses positional-aware embeddings to distinguish each node’s representation and a temporal attention module to enhance long-range temporal perception, within an end-to-end lightweight architecture.
AI.result: Empirical results on county, megalopolis, and state-scale datasets show PASTN’s effectiveness and efficiency; ablation/analysis confirms the utility of the newly introduced positional-aware embeddings and temporal attention modules.
AI.conclusion: PASTN provides an effective and efficient solution for capturing spatiotemporal dependencies in traffic forecasting and is suitable for deployment on large-scale datasets.

title: CourtGuard: A Model-Agnostic Framework for Zero-Shot Policy Adaptation in LLM Safety
summary: Current safety mechanisms for Large Language Models (LLMs) rely heavily on static, fine-tuned classifiers that suffer from adaptation rigidity, the inability to enforce new governance rules without expensive retraining. To address this, we introduce CourtGuard, a retrieval-augmented multi-agent framework that reimagines safety evaluation as Evidentiary Debate. By orchestrating an adversarial debate grounded in external policy documents, CourtGuard achieves state-of-the-art performance across 7 safety benchmarks, outperforming dedicated policy-following baselines without fine-tuning. Beyond standard metrics, we highlight two critical capabilities: (1) Zero-Shot Adaptability, where our framework successfully generalized to an out-of-domain Wikipedia Vandalism task (achieving 90\% accuracy) by swapping the reference policy; and (2) Automated Data Curation and Auditing, where we leveraged CourtGuard to curate and audit nine novel datasets of sophisticated adversarial attacks. Our results demonstrate that decoupling safety logic from model weights offers a robust, interpretable, and adaptable path for meeting current and future regulatory requirements in AI governance.

AI.tldr: CourtGuard reframes LLM safety as Evidentiary Debate via a retrieval-augmented, multi-agent framework that uses external policy documents. It achieves state-of-the-art results on 7 safety benchmarks without model fine-tuning, demonstrates zero-shot adaptability by swapping reference policies (e.g., 90% accuracy on Wikipedia Vandalism), and enables automated data curation/auditing with nine new adversarial datasets. The approach decouples safety logic from model weights, offering interpretable and adaptable governance for AI safety.
AI.motivation: Address rigidity and retraining costs of static classifiers used for LLM safety. The need for adaptable, interpretable, and governance-compliant safety that can incorporate new policies without expensive retraining is driving the development of modular, retrieval-based safety frameworks.
AI.method: A retrieval-augmented multi-agent system conducts adversarial debates grounded in external policy documents. Safety judgments emerge from the argumentation among agents. The safety logic is decoupled from the LLM weights. The framework supports zero-shot adaptation by swapping the reference policy and includes automated data curation/auditing of novel adversarial data.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance across 7 safety benchmarks without fine-tuning. Demonstrates zero-shot adaptability with 90% accuracy on an out-of-domain Wikipedia Vandalism task by swapping policies. Enables automated curation and auditing of nine new adversarial datasets.
AI.conclusion: Decoupling safety logic from model weights yields a robust, interpretable, and adaptable path for AI governance, capable of meeting evolving regulatory requirements by leveraging external policies and modular debate-based evaluation.

title: GraspLDP: Towards Generalizable Grasping Policy via Latent Diffusion
summary: This paper focuses on enhancing the grasping precision and generalization of manipulation policies learned via imitation learning. Diffusion-based policy learning methods have recently become the mainstream approach for robotic manipulation tasks. As grasping is a critical subtask in manipulation, the ability of imitation-learned policies to execute precise and generalizable grasps merits particular attention. Existing imitation learning techniques for grasping often suffer from imprecise grasp executions, limited spatial generalization, and poor object generalization. To address these challenges, we incorporate grasp prior knowledge into the diffusion policy framework. In particular, we employ a latent diffusion policy to guide action chunk decoding with grasp pose prior, ensuring that generated motion trajectories adhere closely to feasible grasp configurations. Furthermore, we introduce a self-supervised reconstruction objective during diffusion to embed the graspness prior: at each reverse diffusion step, we reconstruct wrist-camera images back-projected the graspness from the intermediate representations. Both simulation and real robot experiments demonstrate that our approach significantly outperforms baseline methods and exhibits strong dynamic grasping capabilities.

AI.tldr: Diffusion-based imitation learning for robotic grasping augmented with grasp priors and self-supervised reconstruction improves precision, generalization, and dynamic grasping in both simulation and real robots.
AI.motivation: Imitation-learning grasp policies often suffer from imprecise executions, limited spatial/generalization; diffusion-based methods need priors to enforce feasible grasps.
AI.method: Use a latent diffusion policy to guide action chunk decoding with a grasp pose prior; add a self-supervised reconstruction objective that, at each reverse diffusion step, reconstructs wrist-camera images to propagate graspness from intermediate representations.
AI.result: Empirical results show significant improvement over baselines and robust dynamic grasping in both simulated and real robot experiments.
AI.conclusion: Integrating grasp priors and self-supervised reconstruction into diffusion-based imitation learning yields more accurate, generalizable, and dynamic grasping policies, with strong transfer from simulation to real robots.

title: Causal Motion Diffusion Models for Autoregressive Motion Generation
comment: Accepted to CVPR 2026, Project website: https://yu1ut.com/CMDM-HP/
summary: Recent advances in motion diffusion models have substantially improved the realism of human motion synthesis. However, existing approaches either rely on full-sequence diffusion models with bidirectional generation, which limits temporal causality and real-time applicability, or autoregressive models that suffer from instability and cumulative errors. In this work, we present Causal Motion Diffusion Models (CMDM), a unified framework for autoregressive motion generation based on a causal diffusion transformer that operates in a semantically aligned latent space. CMDM builds upon a Motion-Language-Aligned Causal VAE (MAC-VAE), which encodes motion sequences into temporally causal latent representations. On top of this latent representation, an autoregressive diffusion transformer is trained using causal diffusion forcing to perform temporally ordered denoising across motion frames. To achieve fast inference, we introduce a frame-wise sampling schedule with causal uncertainty, where each subsequent frame is predicted from partially denoised previous frames. The resulting framework supports high-quality text-to-motion generation, streaming synthesis, and long-horizon motion generation at interactive rates. Experiments on HumanML3D and SnapMoGen demonstrate that CMDM outperforms existing diffusion and autoregressive models in both semantic fidelity and temporal smoothness, while substantially reducing inference latency.

AI.tldr: A novel framework CMDM combines a causally-temporal VAE (MAC-VAE) with a causal diffusion transformer to enable autoregressive, semantically-aligned motion generation in latent space, offering fast, streaming, long-horizon text-to-motion synthesis with improved fidelity and temporal smoothness over prior diffusion/autoregressive methods.
AI.motivation: Current motion synthesis either uses full-sequence bidirectional diffusion (harming causality/real-time performance) or autoregressive models that accumulate errors. There is a need for causally-consistent, efficient, long-horizon motion generation in a unified framework.
AI.method: Introduce MAC-VAE to encode motion into temporally causal latent representations aligned with language, then train an autoregressive diffusion transformer with causal diffusion forcing for temporally ordered denoising. Implement a frame-wise sampling schedule with causal uncertainty, predicting each frame from partially denoised previous frames. Enable text-to-motion, streaming, and long-horizon synthesis in a single model.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on HumanML3D and SnapMoGen shows CMDM achieves higher semantic fidelity and better temporal smoothness than existing diffusion and autoregressive baselines, while significantly reducing inference latency, enabling interactive-rate generation.
AI.conclusion: CMDM provides a unified, efficient framework for high-quality, streaming, and long-horizon motion generation driven by text, by combining causal latent representations with a causal diffusion transformer and a partial-denoising frame-wise sampling strategy.

title: X-REFINE: XAI-based RElevance input-Filtering and archItecture fiNe-tuning for channel Estimation
comment: This paper has been accepted for publication in the IEEE Transactions on Vehicular Technology (TVT) as a correspondence paper
summary: AI-native architectures are vital for 6G wireless communications. The black-box nature and high complexity of deep learning models employed in critical applications, such as channel estimation, limit their practical deployment. While perturbation-based XAI solutions offer input filtering, they often neglect internal structural optimization. We propose X-REFINE, an XAI-based framework for joint input-filtering and architecture fine-tuning. By utilizing a decomposition-based, sign-stabilized LRP epsilon rule, X-REFINE backpropagates predictions to derive high-resolution relevance scores for both subcarriers and hidden neurons. This enables a holistic optimization that identifies the most faithful model components. Simulation results demonstrate that X-REFINE achieves a superior interpretability-performance-complexity trade-off, significantly reducing computational complexity while maintaining robust bit error rate (BER) performance across different scenarios.

AI.tldr: X-REFINE is an XAI-based framework that jointly filters inputs and fine-tunes architecture for AI-native 6G channel estimation, using LRP-based relevance scores over subcarriers and neurons to improve interpretability while reducing complexity and preserving BER.
AI.motivation: Critical 6G tasks rely on opaque deep learning models; there is a need for interpretability and efficient architecture optimization to ensure reliability and real-time performance.
AI.method: Apply a decomposition-based, sign-stabilized LRP epsilon rule to backpropagate predictions, obtaining high-resolution relevance scores for subcarriers and hidden neurons; use these scores to drive joint input-filtering and architecture fine-tuning.
AI.result: Achieves a superior interpretability-performance-complexity trade-off, with reduced computational complexity while maintaining robust BER across scenarios.
AI.conclusion: X-REFINE enables faithful identification of influential model components, facilitating practical deployment of AI-native 6G systems with better explainability and efficiency.

title: Strategy Executability in Mathematical Reasoning: Leveraging Human-Model Differences for Effective Guidance
summary: Example-based guidance is widely used to improve mathematical reasoning at inference time, yet its effectiveness is highly unstable across problems and models-even when the guidance is correct and problem-relevant. We show that this instability arises from a previously underexplored gap between strategy usage-whether a reasoning strategy appears in successful solutions-and strategy executability-whether the strategy remains effective when instantiated as guidance for a target model. Through a controlled analysis of paired human-written and model-generated solutions, we identify a systematic dissociation between usage and executability: human- and model-derived strategies differ in structured, domain-dependent ways, leading to complementary strengths and consistent source-dependent reversals under guidance. Building on this diagnosis, we propose Selective Strategy Retrieval (SSR), a test-time framework that explicitly models executability by selectively retrieving and combining strategies using empirical, multi-route, source-aware signals. Across multiple mathematical reasoning benchmarks, SSR yields reliable and consistent improvements over direct solving, in-context learning, and single-source guidance, improving accuracy by up to $+13$ points on AIME25 and $+5$ points on Apex for compact reasoning models. Code and benchmark are publicly available at: https://github.com/lwd17/strategy-execute-pipeline.

AI.tldr: Selective Strategy Retrieval (SSR) improves math reasoning by aligning strategy usage with executability, addressing a usage–executability gap, and outperforming single-source guidance across benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Example-based guidance often yields unstable results because the strategies that appear in successful solutions (usage) do not always translate into effective, executable guidance for the target model. The paper identifies a dissociation between usage and executability and seeks to bridge it.
AI.method: Controlled analysis of paired human-written and model-generated solutions; introduces SSR, a test-time framework that selectively retrieves and combines multiple strategies using empirical, multi-route, source-aware signals to model executability.
AI.result: SSR yields reliable improvements over direct solving, in-context learning, and single-source guidance. Notable gains include up to +13 points on AIME25 and +5 points on Apex for compact reasoning models.
AI.conclusion: SSR provides a practical, source-aware approach to model executability that stabilizes and enhances guidance effectiveness in mathematical reasoning, with code and benchmark publicly available.

title: DySL-VLA: Efficient Vision-Language-Action Model Inference via Dynamic-Static Layer-Skipping for Robot Manipulation
summary: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models have shown remarkable success in robotic tasks like manipulation by fusing a language model's reasoning with a vision model's 3D understanding. However, their high computational cost remains a major obstacle for real-world applications that require real-time performance. We observe that the actions within a task have varying levels of importance: critical steps demand high precision, while less important ones can tolerate more variance. Leveraging this insight, we propose DySL-VLA, a novel framework that addresses computational cost by dynamically skipping VLA layers based on each action's importance. DySL-VLA categorizes its layers into two types: informative layers, which are consistently executed, and incremental layers, which can be selectively skipped. To intelligently skip layers without sacrificing accuracy, we invent a prior-post skipping guidance mechanism to determine when to initiate layer-skipping. We also propose a skip-aware two-stage knowledge distillation algorithm to efficiently train a standard VLA into a DySL-VLA. Our experiments indicate that DySL-VLA achieves 2.1% improvement in success length over Deer-VLA on the Calvin dataset, while simultaneously reducing trainable parameters by a factor of 85.7 and providing a 3.75x speedup relative to the RoboFlamingo baseline at iso-accuracy. Our code is available on https://github.com/PKU-SEC-Lab/DYSL_VLA.

AI.tldr: DySL-VLA enables dynamic layer skipping in Vision-Language-Action models to cut computation while preserving accuracy. It classifies VLA layers into always-informative and optionally-incremental, uses prior-post skipping guidance to decide when to skip, and employs a skip-aware two-stage knowledge distillation to train from a standard VLA. It reports strong gains: 2.1% higher success length than Deer-VLA on Calvin, 85.7x fewer trainable parameters, and 3.75x speedup over RoboFlamingo at iso-accuracy.
AI.motivation: VLA models provide strong reasoning by fusing language, vision, and action understanding, but are computationally heavy, hindering real-time robotics applications. Actions within tasks have varying importance, with critical steps requiring precision and others tolerating variance, suggesting opportunity for compute savings through selective layer execution.
AI.method: Propose DySL-VLA with two layer types: informative layers (always executed) and incremental layers (can be skipped). Introduce a prior-post skipping guidance mechanism to predict when to initiate skipping. Develop a skip-aware two-stage knowledge distillation to train a standard VLA into DySL-VLA. Compare against Deer-VLA and RoboFlamingo on the Calvin dataset, reporting improvements in task success metrics and substantial parameter reductions and speedups.
AI.result: DySL-VLA achieves 2.1% improvement in success length over Deer-VLA on Calvin; reduces trainable parameters by factor 85.7; provides 3.75x speedup relative to RoboFlamingo at iso-accuracy.
AI.conclusion: Dynamic, action-aware layer skipping can dramatically cut computation in VLA models with minimal impact on accuracy. The prior-post guidance and distillation strategy enable effective training of a sparse, faster VLA variant. Code is available for reproducibility.

title: Don't let the information slip away
summary: Real-time object detection has advanced rapidly in recent years. The YOLO series of detectors is among the most well-known CNN-based object detection models and cannot be overlooked. The latest version, YOLOv26, was recently released, while YOLOv12 achieved state-of-the-art (SOTA) performance with 55.2 mAP on the COCO val2017 dataset. Meanwhile, transformer-based object detection models, also known as DEtection TRansformer (DETR), have demonstrated impressive performance. RT-DETR is an outstanding model that outperformed the YOLO series in both speed and accuracy when it was released. Its successor, RT-DETRv2, achieved 53.4 mAP on the COCO val2017 dataset. However, despite their remarkable performance, all these models let information to slip away. They primarily focus on the features of foreground objects while neglecting the contextual information provided by the background. We believe that background information can significantly aid object detection tasks. For example, cars are more likely to appear on roads rather than in offices, while wild animals are more likely to be found in forests or remote areas rather than on busy streets. To address this gap, we propose an object detection model called Association DETR, which achieves state-of-the-art results compared to other object detection models on the COCO val2017 dataset.

AI.tldr: Introduces Association DETR to incorporate background context into DETR-based object detection, achieving state-of-the-art results on COCO val2017.
AI.motivation: Foreground-focused detectors often overlook background information that can provide valuable contextual cues for object presence and location. The paper argues that leveraging background context can improve detection performance.
AI.method: Proposes Association DETR, a DETR-based object detector that models associations between foreground objects and background/contextual information within the image to enhance detection accuracy.
AI.result: Claims state-of-the-art performance on the COCO val2017 dataset compared to other object detectors, surpassing prior DETR-based and YOLO-based methods.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates that incorporating background context via associations improves object detection and yields superior performance on COCO val2017.

title: Integrating Machine Learning Ensembles and Large Language Models for Heart Disease Prediction Using Voting Fusion
comment: 7 pages, 8 figures, (Accepted at a peer-reviewed conference)
summary: Cardiovascular disease is the primary cause of death globally, necessitating early identification, precise risk classification, and dependable decision-support technologies. The advent of large language models (LLMs) provides new zero-shot and few-shot reasoning capabilities, even though machine learning (ML) algorithms, especially ensemble approaches like Random Forest, XGBoost, LightGBM, and CatBoost, are excellent at modeling complex, non-linear patient data and routinely beat logistic regression. This research predicts cardiovascular disease using a merged dataset of 1,190 patient records, comparing traditional machine learning models (95.78% accuracy, ROC-AUC 0.96) with open-source large language models via OpenRouter APIs. Finally, a hybrid fusion of the ML ensemble and LLM reasoning under Gemini 2.5 Flash achieved the best results (96.62% accuracy, 0.97 AUC), showing that LLMs (78.9 % accuracy) work best when combined with ML models rather than used alone. Results show that ML ensembles achieved the highest performance (95.78% accuracy, ROC-AUC 0.96), while LLMs performed moderately in zero-shot (78.9%) and slightly better in few-shot (72.6%) settings. The proposed hybrid method enhanced the strength in uncertain situations, illustrating that ensemble ML is considered the best structured tabular prediction case, but it can be integrated with hybrid ML-LLM systems to provide a minor increase and open the way to more reliable clinical decision-support tools.

AI.tldr: Hybrid ML-LLM approach for cardiovascular disease risk prediction; traditional ML ensembles outperform LLMs alone on tabular data, with a small but consistent improvement when fused with LLM reasoning.
AI.motivation: To evaluate whether large language models can contribute to structured clinical prediction tasks and whether a hybrid system combining ML ensembles with LLM reasoning can improve performance and reliability for cardiovascular disease risk stratification.
AI.method: Dataset of 1,190 patient records. Compared traditional ML models (Random Forest, XGBoost, LightGBM, CatBoost) with open-source LLMs via OpenRouter APIs in zero-shot and few-shot settings. Also developed a hybrid fusion using Gemini 2.5 Flash.
AI.result: ML ensembles achieved 95.78% accuracy with ROC-AUC 0.96. LLMs performed 78.9% accuracy in zero-shot and 72.6% in few-shot settings (note: the few-shot value is described as better, though the numbers suggest otherwise, indicating a potential inconsistency). The proposed hybrid method achieved 96.62% accuracy and ROC-AUC 0.97, outperforming ML ensembles by a small margin.
AI.conclusion: Structured tabular prediction benefits most from ensemble ML methods; a hybrid ML-LLM approach can yield a marginal gain and improve decision-support reliability in uncertain contexts, suggesting a practical path for clinical deployment with careful benchmarking.

title: Correcting Human Labels for Rater Effects in AI Evaluation: An Item Response Theory Approach
comment: 16 pages, 5 figures, 1 table; The 16th Annual Learning Analytics and Knowledge Conference (LAK) Workshop on LLM Psychometrics, April 27, 2026, Bergen, Norway
summary: Human evaluations play a central role in training and assessing AI models, yet these data are rarely treated as measurements subject to systematic error. This paper integrates psychometric rater models into the AI pipeline to improve the reliability and validity of conclusions drawn from human judgments. The paper reviews common rater effects, severity and centrality, that distort observed ratings, and demonstrates how item response theory rater models, particularly the multi-faceted Rasch model, can separate true output quality from rater behavior. Using the OpenAI summarization dataset as an empirical example, we show how adjusting for rater severity produces corrected estimates of summary quality and provides diagnostic insight into rater performance. Incorporating psychometric modeling into human-in-the-loop evaluation offers more principled and transparent use of human data, enabling developers to make decisions based on adjusted scores rather than raw, error-prone ratings. This perspective highlights a path toward more robust, interpretable, and construct-aligned practices for AI development and evaluation.

AI.tldr: Incorporating psychometric rater models (e.g., multi-faceted Rasch) into AI human evaluations to separate true output quality from rater bias, demonstrated on the OpenAI summarization dataset; yields adjusted scores and diagnostic insights; improves robustness and interpretability.
AI.motivation: Human evaluations drive AI assessment but are noisy due to systematic rater error (severity, centrality, etc.). There is a need to treat ratings as measurements with measurement error and to separate true quality from rater behavior.
AI.method: Integrate item response theory (IRT) rater models, specifically the multi-faceted Rasch model, into the AI evaluation pipeline to model rater effects and extract a latent quality score for outputs. Apply to the OpenAI summarization dataset to adjust for rater severity and derive diagnostic metrics of rater performance.
AI.result: Adjusted quality estimates diverge from raw ratings, yielding corrected estimates of summary quality. The approach provides diagnostic insight into rater behavior and more principled evaluation.
AI.conclusion: Psychometric modeling of human evaluations yields more robust, interpretable, and construct-aligned assessments in AI development, enabling decisions based on adjusted scores rather than raw judgments.

title: Bayesian Preference Elicitation: Human-In-The-Loop Optimization of An Active Prosthesis
summary: Tuning active prostheses for people with amputation is time-consuming and relies on metrics that may not fully reflect user needs. We introduce a human-in-the-loop optimization (HILO) approach that leverages direct user preferences to personalize a standard four-parameter prosthesis controller efficiently. Our method employs preference-based Multiobjective Bayesian Optimization that uses a state-or-the-art acquisition function especially designed for preference learning, and includes two algorithmic variants: a discrete version (\textit{EUBO-LineCoSpar}), and a continuous version (\textit{BPE4Prost}). Simulation results on benchmark functions and real-application trials demonstrate efficient convergence, robust preference elicitation, and measurable biomechanical improvements, illustrating the potential of preference-driven tuning for user-centered prosthesis control.

AI.tldr: Preference-based human-in-the-loop optimization for active prostheses using discrete (EUBO-LineCoSpar) and continuous (BPE4Prost) variants, leveraging user preferences to efficiently personalize a four-parameter prosthesis controller.
AI.motivation: Tuning active prostheses is time-consuming and relies on metrics that may not capture user needs; there is a need for user-centered, efficient personalization.
AI.method: Preference-based Multiobjective Bayesian Optimization with a state-of-the-art acquisition function for preference learning. Two algorithmic variants are provided: a discrete version (EUBO-LineCoSpar) and a continuous version (BPE4Prost). The approach directly elicits user preferences to navigate a multiobjective trade-off in a four-parameter prosthesis controller.
AI.result: Simulation on benchmark functions and real-application trials show efficient convergence, robust preference elicitation, and measurable biomechanical improvements.
AI.conclusion: Preference-driven tuning is a promising approach for user-centered prosthesis control, enabling faster personalization and improved biomechanical outcomes; the two variants offer flexibility across discrete and continuous controller settings.

title: BetterScene: 3D Scene Synthesis with Representation-Aligned Generative Model
summary: We present BetterScene, an approach to enhance novel view synthesis (NVS) quality for diverse real-world scenes using extremely sparse, unconstrained photos. BetterScene leverages the production-ready Stable Video Diffusion (SVD) model pretrained on billions of frames as a strong backbone, aiming to mitigate artifacts and recover view-consistent details at inference time. Conventional methods have developed similar diffusion-based solutions to address these challenges of novel view synthesis. Despite significant improvements, these methods typically rely on off-the-shelf pretrained diffusion priors and fine-tune only the UNet module while keeping other components frozen, which still leads to inconsistent details and artifacts even when incorporating geometry-aware regularizations like depth or semantic conditions. To address this, we investigate the latent space of the diffusion model and introduce two components: (1) temporal equivariance regularization and (2) vision foundation model-aligned representation, both applied to the variational autoencoder (VAE) module within the SVD pipeline. BetterScene integrates a feed-forward 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) model to render features as inputs for the SVD enhancer and generate continuous, artifact-free, consistent novel views. We evaluate on the challenging DL3DV-10K dataset and demonstrate superior performance compared to state-of-the-art methods.

AI.tldr: BetterScene improves novel view synthesis (NVS) for real-world scenes with extremely sparse photos by enhancing the diffusion-based SVD pipeline. It regularizes the diffusion model's latent space via temporal equivariance and vision foundation model–aligned representations at the VAE stage and couples a 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) renderer to supply inputs to the SVD enhancer, yielding more view-consistent, artifact-free results. Evaluated on DL3DV-10K, it outperforms state-of-the-art methods.
AI.motivation: To reduce artifacts and inconsistency in diffusion-based NVS when using off-the-shelf priors and fine-tuning only the UNet, especially under extremely sparse unconstrained photographs.
AI.method: 1) Apply temporal equivariance regularization and vision foundation model–aligned representation to the VAE within Stable Video Diffusion (SVD). 2) Integrate a feed-forward 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) renderer to produce input features for the SVD enhancer to render continuous, view-consistent novel views.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on the challenging DL3DV-10K dataset shows superior performance compared with state-of-the-art diffusion-based NVS approaches.
AI.conclusion: By refining the VAE latent space with temporal and foundation-model-aligned constraints and incorporating 3DGS-based feature rendering, BetterScene achieves more artifact-free and consistent novel views on sparse real-world data, surpassing existing methods.

title: BrepCoder: A Unified Multimodal Large Language Model for Multi-task B-rep Reasoning
