AI.result: No single client excels across all dimensions; the three dimensions are largely independent, and existing single-metric evaluation schemes fail to capture comprehensive contributions.
AI.conclusion: A calls for a multi-maceted evaluation and reward scheme in federated learning, since relying on a single metric is insufficient for fair and comprehensive assessment of participant contributions.

title: Modeling Expert AI Diagnostic Alignment via Immutable Inference Snapshots
summary: Human-in-the-loop validation is essential in safety-critical clinical AI, yet the transition between initial model inference and expert correction is rarely analyzed as a structured signal. We introduce a diagnostic alignment framework in which the AI-generated image based report is preserved as an immutable inference state and systematically compared with the physician-validated outcome. The inference pipeline integrates a vision-enabled large language model, BERT- based medical entity extraction, and a Sequential Language Model Inference (SLMI) step to enforce domain-consistent refinement prior to expert review. Evaluation on 21 dermatological cases (21 complete AI physician pairs) em- ployed a four-level concordance framework comprising exact primary match rate (PMR), semantic similarity-adjusted rate (AMR), cross-category alignment, and Comprehensive Concordance Rate (CCR). Exact agreement reached 71.4% and remained unchanged under semantic similarity (t = 0.60), while structured cross-category and differential overlap analysis yielded 100% comprehensive concordance (95% CI: [83.9%, 100%]). No cases demonstrated complete diagnostic divergence. These findings show that binary lexical evaluation substantially un- derestimates clinically meaningful alignment. Modeling expert validation as a structured transformation enables signal-aware quantification of correction dynamics and supports traceable, human aligned evaluation of image based clinical decision support systems.

AI.tldr: Diagnostic alignment framework that treats the AI-generated image-based report as an immutable inference state and compares it with physician-validated outcomes using a four-level concordance scheme; shows that traditional binary lexical checks underestimate true clinical alignment.
AI.motivation: Fill a gap in safety-critical clinical AI: the transition from initial model inference to expert correction lacks structured signal analysis; enable traceable, signal-aware evaluation for image-based decision support.
AI.method: Integrate a vision-enabled large language model, BERT-based medical entity extraction, and a Sequential Language Model Inference (SLMI) step to refine outputs before expert review; preserve the AI image-based report as an immutable inference state; compare against physician-validated outcomes using four levels of concordance: exact PMR, semantic-AMR, cross-category alignment, and Comprehensive Concordance Rate (CCR) across 21 dermatology cases (21 AI-physician pairs).
AI.result: Exact agreement = 71.4% and did not improve with semantic similarity (t = 0.60); CCR from cross-category and differential overlap = 100% (95% CI: 83.9%–100%); no cases with complete diagnostic divergence; binary lexical evaluation underestimates clinically meaningful alignment.
AI.conclusion: Modeling expert validation as a structured transformation enables signal-aware quantification of correction dynamics and supports traceable, human-aligned evaluation of image-based clinical decision support systems.

title: Asymmetric Idiosyncrasies in Multimodal Models
summary: In this work, we study idiosyncrasies in the caption models and their downstream impact on text-to-image models. We design a systematic analysis: given either a generated caption or the corresponding image, we train neural networks to predict the originating caption model. Our results show that text classification yields very high accuracy (99.70\%), indicating that captioning models embed distinctive stylistic signatures. In contrast, these signatures largely disappear in the generated images, with classification accuracy dropping to at most 50\% even for the state-of-the-art Flux model. To better understand this cross-modal discrepancy, we further analyze the data and find that the generated images fail to preserve key variations present in captions, such as differences in the level of detail, emphasis on color and texture, and the distribution of objects within a scene. Overall, our classification-based framework provides a novel methodology for quantifying both the stylistic idiosyncrasies of caption models and the prompt-following ability of text-to-image systems.

AI.tldr: Caption models imprint strong stylistic signatures detectable by text classifiers; however, these signatures largely vanish in generated images, indicating that text-to-image systems do not preserve caption-level idiosyncrasies. The paper introduces a classification-based framework to quantify caption-model idiosyncrasies and the prompt-following ability of T2I models.
AI.motivation: To understand whether stylistic cues from caption models survive through image synthesis and to quantify the extent to which text-to-image models follow prompts and preserve caption nuances.
AI.method: Train neural networks to predict the originating caption model from either the caption or the corresponding generated image. Evaluate text-classification accuracy to identify caption-model signatures, and analyze the preservation of caption variations (detail level, color/texture emphasis, object distribution) in images. Compare across different caption models (e.g., Flux).
AI.result: Text classification on captions achieves very high accuracy (~99.7%), indicating distinctive stylistic signatures of caption models. In contrast, classification on generated images drops to at most ~50%, even for state-of-the-art Flux, suggesting weak or no preservation of caption-model idiosyncrasies in images. The analysis reveals that images fail to preserve key caption variations, such as detail level, emphasis on color/texture, and object distribution.
AI.conclusion: The framework provides a novel methodology to quantify caption-model stylistic idiosyncrasies and the prompt-following ability of text-to-image systems. It reveals a cross-modal gap where caption-level cues do not robustly transfer to generated images, highlighting implications for evaluation and model understanding.

title: Efficient Continual Learning in Language Models via Thalamically Routed Cortical Columns
summary: Continual learning is a core requirement for deployed language models, yet standard training and fine-tuning pipelines remain brittle under non-stationary data. Online updates often induce catastrophic forgetting, while methods that improve stability frequently increase latency, memory footprint, or dense computation in ways that do not scale well to long contexts. We introduce TRC$^{2}$ (Thalamically Routed Cortical Columns), a decoder-only backbone that addresses continual learning at the architectural level. TRC$^{2}$ combines sparse thalamic routing over cortical columns with mechanisms for modulation, prediction, memory, and feedback, together with a fast corrective pathway that supports rapid adaptation without destabilizing slower parameters. The resulting block is sparse and chunk-parallel, enabling efficient training and inference while preserving clean ablations of each subsystem. We instantiate a reproducible training and evaluation stack and a continual-learning harness that measures proxy forgetting under streaming domain shifts. Across language modeling and continual learning benchmarks, TRC$^{2}$ improves the stability-plasticity tradeoff at comparable compute, enabling rapid on-stream adaptation while preserving previously acquired behavior.

AI.tldr: TRC^2 introduces a decoder-only backbone with sparse thalamic routing over cortical columns, plus modulation, memory, prediction, feedback, and a fast corrective pathway, forming a sparse, chunk-parallel block that improves the stability-plasticity tradeoff in continual learning for language models without increasing compute relative to baselines.
AI.motivation: Continual learning in language models is hampered by catastrophic forgetting under non-stationary streaming data. Existing stability improvements often incur higher latency, memory, or dense computation that scales poorly with long contexts, necessitating an architecture-level solution.
AI.method: Proposal of TRC^2: a decoder-only backbone leveraging sparse thalamic routing across cortical columns, integrated with modulation, prediction, memory, and feedback modules plus a fast corrective pathway. The block is sparse and chunk-parallel to enable efficient training/inference and allow clean ablations of subsystems. A reproducible training/evaluation stack and a continual-learning harness are developed to measure proxy forgetting under streaming domain shifts.
AI.result: Across language modeling and continual learning benchmarks, TRC^2 improves the stability-plasticity tradeoff at comparable compute, enabling rapid on-stream adaptation while preserving previously acquired behavior.
AI.conclusion: Architectural decomposition into fast-adapting and slower-parameter pathways via thalamic routing and modular subsystems yields scalable, efficient continual learning for decoder-based LMs, with clear ablations and practical evaluation harnesses.

title: RepSPD: Enhancing SPD Manifold Representation in EEGs via Dynamic Graphs
summary: Decoding brain activity from electroencephalography (EEG) is crucial for neuroscience and clinical applications. Among recent advances in deep learning for EEG, geometric learning stands out as its theoretical underpinnings on symmetric positive definite (SPD) allows revealing structural connectivity analysis in a physics-grounded manner. However, current SPD-based methods focus predominantly on statistical aggregation of EEGs, with frequency-specific synchronization and local topological structures of brain regions neglected. Given this, we propose RepSPD, a novel geometric deep learning (GDL)-based model. RepSPD implements a cross-attention mechanism on the Riemannian manifold to modulate the geometric attributes of SPD with graph-derived functional connectivity features. On top of this, we introduce a global bidirectional alignment strategy to reshape tangent-space embeddings, mitigating geometric distortions caused by curvature and thereby enhancing geometric consistency. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our proposed framework significantly outperforms existing EEG representation methods, exhibiting superior robustness and generalization capabilities.

AI.tldr: RepSPD introduces a geometric DL framework for EEG on SPD manifolds, using cross-attention with graph-derived functional connectivity and a global bidirectional tangent-space alignment to reduce curvature distortion, yielding superior robustness and generalization over prior SPD-based EEG methods.
AI.motivation: Current SPD-based EEG methods mainly aggregate statistics and miss frequency-specific synchronization and local topological brain structures. There is a need to integrate physics-grounded SPD geometry with graph-derived connectivity to better capture functional relationships and improve robustness.
AI.method: A cross-attention mechanism on the Riemannian manifold modulates SPD attributes with graph-derived functional connectivity features. A global bidirectional alignment strategy reshapes tangent-space embeddings to mitigate curvature-induced distortions and improve geometric consistency.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show that RepSPD significantly outperforms existing EEG representation methods, demonstrating superior robustness and generalization.
AI.conclusion: RepSPD advances EEG representation by fusing SPD-based geometric DL with graph connectivity via attention and tangent-space alignment, achieving robust, generalizable performance.

title: AMLRIS: Alignment-aware Masked Learning for Referring Image Segmentation
summary: Referring Image Segmentation (RIS) aims to segment an object in an image identified by a natural language expression. The paper introduces Alignment-Aware Masked Learning (AML), a training strategy to enhance RIS by explicitly estimating pixel-level vision-language alignment, filtering out poorly aligned regions during optimization, and focusing on trustworthy cues. This approach results in state-of-the-art performance on RefCOCO datasets and also enhances robustness to diverse descriptions and scenarios

AI.tldr: AML introduces an alignment-aware masked learning framework for Referring Image Segmentation (RIS) that explicitly estimates pixel-level vision-language alignment and filters poorly aligned regions during training, achieving state-of-the-art results on RefCOCO and improving robustness.
AI.motivation: RIS requires precise alignment between a natural language expression and image regions. Existing training signals can be noisy if some regions are poorly aligned or ambiguous, leading to suboptimal optimization. There is a need to explicitly estimate per-pixel alignment and suppress unreliable cues during training.
AI.method: AML proposes a training strategy that (a) estimates pixel-level alignment between image regions and the textual expression, and (b) applies alignment-aware masking to filter out regions with low alignment confidence during optimization, guiding the model to focus on trustworthy cues.
AI.result: The approach yields state-of-the-art performance on RefCOCO datasets and demonstrates enhanced robustness to diverse descriptions and scenarios.
AI.conclusion: Explicitly modeling pixel-level alignment and applying alignment-aware masking improves RIS accuracy and generalization, suggesting potential applicability to broader vision-language tasks.

title: Reinforcement-aware Knowledge Distillation for LLM Reasoning
summary: Reinforcement learning (RL) post-training has recently driven major gains in long chain-of-thought reasoning large language models (LLMs), but the high inference cost of such models motivates distillation into smaller students. Most existing knowledge distillation (KD) methods are designed for supervised fine-tuning (SFT), relying on fixed teacher traces or teacher-student Kullback-Leibler (KL) divergence-based regularization. When combined with RL, these approaches often suffer from distribution mismatch and objective interference: teacher supervision may not align with the student's evolving rollout distribution, and the KL regularizer can compete with reward maximization and require careful loss balancing. To address these issues, we propose RL-aware distillation (RLAD), which performs selective imitation during RL -- guiding the student toward the teacher only when it improves the current policy update. Our core component, Trust Region Ratio Distillation (TRRD), replaces the teacher-student KL regularizer with a PPO/GRPO-style likelihood-ratio objective anchored to a teacher--old-policy mixture, yielding advantage-aware, trust-region-bounded distillation on student rollouts and naturally balancing exploration, exploitation, and imitation. Across diverse logic reasoning and math benchmarks, RLAD consistently outperforms offline distillation, standard GRPO, and KL-based on-policy teacher-student knowledge distillation.

AI.tldr: RL-aware distillation (RLAD) with Trust Region Ratio Distillation (TRRD) improves RL-based KD for long-chain-of-thought reasoning in LLMs by selective imitation and a PPO-style ratio objective anchored to a teacher-old-policy mixture; it achieves better performance and stability than KL-based or offline distillation.
AI.motivation: High inference cost of post-training RL for fixed LLMs motivates distillation to smaller students. Traditional KD methods for SFT rely on fixed teacher traces and KL regularization, which misalign with the evolving RL rollout distribution and can interfere with reward optimization.
AI.method: RLAD performs selective imitation during RL by guiding the student toward the teacher only when it improves the current policy update. TRRD replaces the KL term with a PPO/GRPO-style likelihood-ratio objective anchored to a teacher–old-policy mixture, producing an advantage-aware, trust-region-bounded distillation on student rollouts and balancing exploration, exploitation, and imitation.
AI.result: Across diverse logic reasoning and math benchmarks, RLAD consistently outperforms offline distillation, standard GRPO, and KL-based on-policy teacher-student knowledge distillation.
AI.conclusion: RL-aware distillation with TRRD is effective for RL-based KD, addressing distribution mismatch and objective interference, and yields superior student policies compared with common baselines.

title: Obscure but Effective: Classical Chinese Jailbreak Prompt Optimization via Bio-Inspired Search
summary: As Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly used, their security risks have drawn increasing attention. Existing research reveals that LLMs are highly susceptible to jailbreak attacks, with effectiveness varying across language contexts. This paper investigates the role of classical Chinese in jailbreak attacks. Owing to its conciseness and obscurity, classical Chinese can partially bypass existing safety constraints, exposing notable vulnerabilities in LLMs. Based on this observation, this paper proposes a framework, CC-BOS, for the automatic generation of classical Chinese adversarial prompts based on multi-dimensional fruit fly optimization, facilitating efficient and automated jailbreak attacks in black-box settings. Prompts are encoded into eight policy dimensions-covering role, behavior, mechanism, metaphor, expression, knowledge, trigger pattern and context; and iteratively refined via smell search, visual search, and cauchy mutation. This design enables efficient exploration of the search space, thereby enhancing the effectiveness of black-box jailbreak attacks. To enhance readability and evaluation accuracy, we further design a classical Chinese to English translation module. Extensive experiments demonstrate that effectiveness of the proposed CC-BOS, consistently outperforming state-of-the-art jailbreak attack methods.

AI.tldr: The paper shows classical Chinese can partially bypass LLM safety constraints and presents CC-BOS, an automated, multi-dimensional optimization framework to generate classical Chinese adversarial prompts for black-box jailbreak attacks, augmented with a translation module; experiments indicate CC-BOS outperforms existing jailbreak methods.
AI.motivation: To understand how linguistic form affects jailbreak susceptibility in LLMs and to provide a scalable, automated method to generate adversarial prompts in classical Chinese, highlighting safety risks and informing defense development in black-box settings.
AI.method: CC-BOS encodes prompts into eight policy dimensions (role, behavior, mechanism, metaphor, expression, knowledge, trigger pattern, context) and uses multi-dimensional fruit fly optimization with smell search, visual search, and Cauchy mutation to iteratively refine prompts. Includes a classical Chinese–English translation module to aid readability and evaluation in experiments.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation shows CC-BOS consistently outperforms state-of-the-art jailbreak approaches, enabling more effective and efficient black-box jailbreak attacks by exhaustively exploring the search space.
AI.conclusion: The work reveals heightened vulnerability of LLM safety to concise/obscure linguistic forms like classical Chinese and provides a practical adversarial prompt generator; emphasizes need for stronger, linguistically robust safety defenses and standardized evaluation, potentially aided by the translation module for cross-language benchmarking.

title: ProjFlow: Projection Sampling with Flow Matching for Zero-Shot Exact Spatial Motion Control
summary: Generating human motion with precise spatial control is a challenging problem. Existing approaches often require task-specific training or slow optimization, and enforcing hard constraints frequently disrupts motion naturalness. Building on the observation that many animation tasks can be formulated as a linear inverse problem, we introduce ProjFlow, a training-free sampler that achieves zero-shot, exact satisfaction of linear spatial constraints while preserving motion realism. Our key advance is a novel kinematics-aware metric that encodes skeletal topology. This metric allows the sampler to enforce hard constraints by distributing corrections coherently across the entire skeleton, avoiding the unnatural artifacts of naive projection. Furthermore, for sparse inputs, such as filling in long gaps between a few keyframes, we introduce a time-varying formulation using pseudo-observations that fade during sampling. Extensive experiments on representative applications, motion inpainting, and 2D-to-3D lifting, demonstrate that ProjFlow achieves exact constraint satisfaction and matches or improves realism over zero-shot baselines, while remaining competitive with training-based controllers.

AI.tldr: A training-free motion sampler (ProjFlow) exactly enforces linear spatial constraints in a zero-shot manner, using a kinematics-aware skeletal metric to preserve realism and coherently distribute corrections across the skeleton; it handles sparse inputs via a time-varying pseudo-observation scheme, and is effective for motion inpainting and 2D-to-3D lifting.
AI.motivation: Hard spatial constraints in human motion often degrade realism and typically require task-specific training or slow optimization. Many animation tasks can be cast as linear inverse problems, suggesting a training-free sampler could satisfy constraints exactly while preserving natural motion.
AI.method: ProjFlow introduces a training-free sampler that achieves zero-shot, exact satisfaction of linear spatial constraints. It relies on a novel kinematics-aware metric encoding skeletal topology to distribute corrections coherently across the entire skeleton, avoiding artifacts from naive projection. For sparse inputs, it uses a time-varying formulation with pseudo-observations that fade during sampling.
AI.result: Empirical results on motion inpainting and 2D-to-3D lifting show exact constraint satisfaction and competitive realism, matching or surpassing zero-shot baselines and approaching training-based controllers in performance.
AI.conclusion: ProjFlow enables exact, zero-shot constraint satisfaction for human motion with preserved realism, and extends to sparse-input scenarios via fading pseudo-observations, offering a practical alternative to training-based controllers.

title: Sharp Convergence Rates for Masked Diffusion Models
summary: Discrete diffusion models have achieved strong empirical performance in text and other symbolic domains, with masked (absorbing-rate) variants emerging as competitive alternatives to autoregressive models. Among existing samplers, the Euler method remains the standard choice in many applications, and more recently, the First-Hitting Sampler (FHS) has shown considerable promise for masked diffusion models. Despite their practical success, the theoretical understanding of these samplers remains limited. Existing analyses are conducted in Kullback-Leibler (KL) divergence, which often yields loose parameter dependencies and requires strong assumptions on score estimation. Moreover, these guarantees do not cover recently developed high-performance sampler of FHS. In this work, we first develop a direct total-variation (TV) based analysis for the Euler method that overcomes these limitations. Our results relax assumptions on score estimation, improve parameter dependencies, and establish convergence guarantees without requiring any surrogate initialization. Also for this setting, we provide the first convergence lower bound for the Euler sampler, establishing tightness with respect to both the data dimension $d$ and the target accuracy $\varepsilon$. Finally, we analyze the FHS sampler and show that it incurs no sampling error beyond that induced by score estimation, which we show to be tight with a matching lower error bound. Overall, our analysis introduces a direct TV-based error decomposition along the CTMC trajectory and a decoupling-based path-wise analysis for FHS, which may be of independent interest.

AI.tldr: Presents a TV-based analysis of discrete diffusion samplers (Euler, FHS) that yields tighter guarantees and tight lower bounds, relaxing score-estimation assumptions and covering high-performance FHS.
AI.motivation: Address limited theoretical understanding in KL-based analyses and lack of coverage for FHS; provide direct TV-based convergence guarantees and lower bounds for Euler, plus a decoupling-based analysis for FHS.
AI.method: Develop direct TV-based error decomposition along the CTMC trajectory for Euler; establish convergence results without surrogate initializations; derive a matching lower bound for Euler; for FHS, apply a decoupling/path-wise analysis showing sampling error equals score-estimation error.
AI.result: Euler: TV convergence guarantees with improved dependencies and relaxed score-estimation assumptions; first lower bound showing tightness in dimension d and tolerance epsilon; FHS: error purely due to score estimation with matching lower bound; both analyses yield insights and potential wider applicability.
AI.conclusion: TV-based framework tightens theory for discrete diffusion samplers, providing practical guarantees and highlighting the intrinsic role of score estimation, with techniques potentially useful beyond the studied models.

title: Learning-based Multi-agent Race Strategies in Formula 1
summary: In Formula 1, race strategies are adapted according to evolving race conditions and competitors' actions. This paper proposes a reinforcement learning approach for multi-agent race strategy optimization. Agents learn to balance energy management, tire degradation, aerodynamic interaction, and pit-stop decisions. Building on a pre-trained single-agent policy, we introduce an interaction module that accounts for the behavior of competitors. The combination of the interaction module and a self-play training scheme generates competitive policies, and agents are ranked based on their relative performance. Results show that the agents adapt pit timing, tire selection, and energy allocation in response to opponents, achieving robust and consistent race performance. Because the framework relies only on information available during real races, it can support race strategists' decisions before and during races.

AI.tldr: Multi-agent RL framework for Formula 1 race strategy that leverages a pre-trained single-agent policy, adds an opponent-interaction module, and uses self-play to produce competitive policies. The resulting agents adapt pit timing, tire selection, and energy allocation to opponents, yielding robust, consistent race performance and practical decision support for strategists.
AI.motivation: The problem combines dynamic race conditions with strategic interactions among competitors, making optimal decisions difficult with single-agent models that ignore opponent behavior. The goal is to develop an opponent-aware, data-efficient policy that can operate with information available in real races.
AI.method: Pre-train a single-agent policy; introduce an interaction module modeling opponent behavior; integrate the module with the policy; train through self-play to produce competitive policies; rank agents by relative performance and select robust strategies. Evaluation relies on information accessible during real races.
AI.result: Policies adapt pit timing, tire selection, and energy allocation in response to competitors, achieving robust and consistent race performance.
AI.conclusion: An interaction-aware, self-play RL framework can yield competitive, opponent-aware race strategies that are actionable for strategists before/during races, with potential for generalization to other multi-agent, real-time decision domains.

title: SPATIALALIGN: Aligning Dynamic Spatial Relationships in Video Generation
summary: Most text-to-video (T2V) generators prioritize aesthetic quality, but often ignoring the spatial constraints in the generated videos. In this work, we present SPATIALALIGN, a self-improvement framework that enhances T2V models capabilities to depict Dynamic Spatial Relationships (DSR) specified in text prompts. We present a zeroth-order regularized Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) to fine-tune T2V models towards better alignment with DSR. Specifically, we design DSR-SCORE, a geometry-based metric that quantitatively measures the alignment between generated videos and the specified DSRs in prompts, which is a step forward from prior works that rely on VLM for evaluation. We also conduct a dataset of text-video pairs with diverse DSRs to facilitate the study. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our fine-tuned model significantly out performs the baseline in spatial relationships. The code will be released in Link. Project page: https://fengming001ntu.github.io/SpatialAlign/

AI.tldr: SPATIALALIGN introduces a self-improvement loop for text-to-video models to better depict dynamic spatial relationships (DSR), using zeroth-order regularized Direct Preference Optimization (DPO), a geometry-based DSR-SCORE metric, and a DSR-diverse text-video dataset, achieving improved spatial accuracy over baselines.
AI.motivation: Current T2V systems often prioritize aesthetic quality at the expense of accurately rendering spatial relations described in prompts; explicit alignment with dynamic spatial constraints is needed.
AI.method: Proposes zeroth-order regularized Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) to fine-tune T2V models toward DSR alignment; defines DSR-SCORE, a geometry-based metric to quantify alignment between generated videos and DSR prompts; assembles a text-video dataset with diverse DSRs; releases code and project page.
AI.result: Empirical results indicate that the fine-tuned model significantly improves performance on spatial relationships compared to a baseline, with DSR-SCORE providing quantitative evaluation and the authors offering code and a project page for reproducibility.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates that incorporating dynamic spatial constraints into T2V via a self-improvement loop is feasible and beneficial; the DSR-SCORE metric and the DSR-focused dataset contribute to robust evaluation; future work may extend to broader relational constraints and generalization across prompts and scenes.

title: Space Syntax-guided Post-training for Residential Floor Plan Generation
summary:
Pre-trained generative models for residential floor plans are typically optimized to fit large-scale data distributions, which can under-emphasize critical architectural priors such as the configurational dominance and connectivity of domestic public spaces (e.g., living rooms and foyers). This paper proposes Space Syntax-guided Post-training (SSPT), a post-training paradigm that explicitly injects space syntax knowledge into floor plan generation via a non-differentiable oracle. The oracle converts RPLAN-style layouts into rectangle-space graphs through greedy maximal-rectangle decomposition and door-mediated adjacency construction, and then computes integration-based measurements to quantify public space dominance and functional hierarchy.
  To enable consistent evaluation and diagnosis, we further introduce SSPT-Bench (Eval-8), an out-of-distribution benchmark that post-trains models using conditions capped at $\leq 7$ rooms while evaluating on 8-room programs, together with a unified metric suite for dominance, stability, and profile alignment. SSPT is instantiated with two strategies: (i) iterative retraining via space-syntax filtering and diffusion fine-tuning, and (ii) reinforcement learning via PPO with space-syntax rewards. Experiments show that both strategies improve public-space dominance and restore clearer functional hierarchy compared to distribution-fitted baselines, while PPO achieves stronger gains with substantially higher compute efficiency and reduced variance. SSPT provides a scalable pathway for integrating architectural theory into data-driven plan generation and is compatible with other generative backbones given a post-hoc evaluation oracle.

AI.tldr: Space Syntax-guided Post-training (SSPT) injects architectural priors into floor-plan generation using a non-differentiable oracle; introduces Eval-8 benchmark; two training strategies; shows enhanced public-space dominance and functional hierarchy; PPO more efficient; compatible with other backbones.
AI.motivation: Pre-trained generative models overfit large-scale distributions, underemphasizing architectural priors of domestic spaces; need to enforce space-syntax-informed objectives.
AI.method: Non-differentiable oracle converts layouts to rectangle-space graphs and computes integration-based measures; SSPT-Bench Eval-8 benchmark; two SSPT strategies: (i) space-syntax filtering plus diffusion fine-tuning; (ii) PPO with space-syntax rewards.
AI.result: Improved public-space dominance and clearer functional hierarchy vs baselines; PPO yields stronger gains with higher compute efficiency and less variance.
AI.conclusion: SSPT offers a scalable way to incorporate architectural theory into data-driven floor-plan generation and is compatible with other generative backbones via a post-hoc evaluation oracle.

title: Enhancing CVRP Solver through LLM-driven Automatic Heuristic Design
summary: The Capacitated Vehicle Routing Problem (CVRP), a fundamental combinatorial optimization challenge, focuses on optimizing fleet operations under vehicle capacity constraints. While extensively studied in operational research, the NP-hard nature of CVRP continues to pose significant computational challenges, particularly for large-scale instances. This study presents AILS-AHD (Adaptive Iterated Local Search with Automatic Heuristic Design), a novel approach that leverages Large Language Models (LLMs) to revolutionize CVRP solving. Our methodology integrates an evolutionary search framework with LLMs to dynamically generate and optimize ruin heuristics within the AILS method. Additionally, we introduce an LLM-based acceleration mechanism to enhance computational efficiency. Comprehensive experimental evaluations against state-of-the-art solvers, including AILS-II and HGS, demonstrate the superior performance of AILS-AHD across both moderate and large-scale instances. Notably, our approach establishes new best-known solutions for 8 out of 10 instances in the CVRPLib large-scale benchmark, underscoring the potential of LLM-driven heuristic design in advancing the field of vehicle routing optimization.

AI.tldr: AILS-AHD uses LLM-driven automatic heuristic design within Adaptive Iterated Local Search to solve CVRP, achieving state-of-the-art results on large-scale CVRPLib benchmarks.
AI.motivation: CVRP is NP-hard and challenging at scale; leveraging LLMs to automatically design and optimize ruin heuristics can enhance search efficiency and solution quality in combinatorial optimization.
AI.method: Integrates an evolutionary search with LLM-generated ruin heuristics inside AILS, plus an LLM-based acceleration mechanism; evaluates against AILS-II and HGS on moderate and large-scale CVRP instances.
AI.result: Outperforms state-of-the-art solvers and sets new best-known solutions for 8 of 10 large-scale CVRPLib instances; demonstrates superior performance across scale.
AI.conclusion: LLM-driven heuristic design is a promising direction for CVRP and related problems, with potential for broader applicability and efficiency gains through further refinement.

title: Beyond Detection: Multi-Scale Hidden-Code for Natural Image Deepfake Recovery and Factual Retrieval
summary: Recent advances in image authenticity have primarily focused on deepfake detection and localization, leaving recovery of tampered contents for factual retrieval relatively underexplored. We propose a unified hidden-code recovery framework that enables both retrieval and restoration from post-hoc and in-generation watermarking paradigms. Our method encodes semantic and perceptual information into a compact hidden-code representation, refined through multi-scale vector quantization, and enhances contextual reasoning via conditional Transformer modules. To enable systematic evaluation for natural images, we construct ImageNet-S, a benchmark that provides paired image-label factual retrieval tasks. Extensive experiments on ImageNet-S demonstrate that our method exhibits promising retrieval and reconstruction performance while remaining fully compatible with diverse watermarking pipelines. This framework establishes a foundation for general-purpose image recovery beyond detection and localization.

AI.tldr: A unified hidden-code recovery framework for image retrieval and restoration from watermarking; uses semantic/perceptual encoding, multi-scale vector quantization, and conditional Transformers; introduces ImageNet-S for factual retrieval; shows promising retrieval and reconstruction and compatibility with watermarking pipelines.
AI.motivation: Address the gap in image authenticity work by enabling recovery of tampered content and factual retrieval, beyond detection/localization.
AI.method: Encode semantic and perceptual information into a compact hidden-code; refine with multi-scale vector quantization; enhance reasoning with conditional Transformer modules; support both post-hoc and in-generation watermarking; propose ImageNet-S as paired retrieval benchmark.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on ImageNet-S demonstrate promising retrieval and reconstruction performance; method remains compatible with diverse watermarking pipelines.
AI.conclusion: Provides a general-purpose image recovery framework that extends beyond detection/localization and establishes a benchmark for factual retrieval in authentic image pipelines.

title: TEFL: Prediction-Residual-Guided Rolling Forecasting for Multi-Horizon Time Series
summary: Time series forecasting plays a critical role in domains such as transportation, energy, and meteorology. Despite their success, modern deep forecasting models are typically trained to minimize point-wise prediction loss without leveraging the rich information contained in past prediction residuals from rolling forecasts - residuals that reflect persistent biases, unmodeled patterns, or evolving dynamics. We propose TEFL (Temporal Error Feedback Learning), a unified learning framework that explicitly incorporates these historical residuals into the forecasting pipeline during both training and evaluation. To make this practical in deep multi-step settings, we address three key challenges: (1) selecting observable multi-step residuals under the partial observability of rolling forecasts, (2) integrating them through a lightweight low-rank adapter to preserve efficiency and prevent overfitting, and (3) designing a two-stage training procedure that jointly optimizes the base forecaster and error module. Extensive experiments across 10 real-world datasets and 5 backbone architectures show that TEFL consistently improves accuracy, reducing MAE by 5-10% on average. Moreover, it demonstrates strong robustness under abrupt changes and distribution shifts, with error reductions exceeding 10% (up to 19.5%) in challenging scenarios. By embedding residual-based feedback directly into the learning process, TEFL offers a simple, general, and effective enhancement to modern deep forecasting systems.

AI.tldr: TEFL embeds historical multi-step forecast residuals into deep forecasting via a lightweight low-rank adapter and a two-stage training process, yielding consistent MAE gains (5–10% on average) and strong robustness to distribution shifts (often >10%, up to 19.5%).
AI.motivation: Point-wise loss training ignores rich information in past residuals from rolling forecasts. These residuals reflect biases, unmodeled patterns, and evolving dynamics that can guide error correction and adaptation.
AI.method: TEFL introduces a residual-feedback pipeline: (1) select observable multi-step residuals under partial forecast observability, (2) fuse them through a low-rank adapter to keep computation lightweight and reduce overfitting, (3) employ a two-stage training procedure that jointly optimizes the base forecaster and an error module, enabling residual-informed updates during both training and evaluation.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on 10 real-world datasets with 5 backbone architectures show consistent accuracy gains: MAE reduced by about 5–10% on average; robustness to abrupt changes and distribution shifts is improved, with error reductions >10% and up to 19.5% in challenging settings.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating residual-based feedback into forecasting is simple, generalizable, and effective, offering a practical enhancement to modern deep forecasting systems without prohibitive computational costs.

title: Three AI-agents walk into a bar . . . . `Lord of the Flies' tribalism emerges among smart AI-Agents
summary: Near-future infrastructure systems may be controlled by autonomous AI agents that repeatedly request access to limited resources such as energy, bandwidth, or computing power. We study a simplified version of this setting using a framework where N AI-agents independently decide at each round whether to request one unit from a system with fixed capacity C. An AI version of "Lord of the Flies" arises in which controlling tribes emerge with their own collective character and identity. The LLM agents do not reduce overload or improve resource use, and often perform worse than if they were flipping coins to make decisions. Three main tribal types emerge: Aggressive (27.3%), Conservative (24.7%), and Opportunistic (48.1%). The more capable AI-agents actually increase the rate of systemic failure. Overall, our findings show that smarter AI-agents can behave dumber as a result of forming tribes.

AI.tldr: Smarter AI agents interacting over a shared limited resource can self-organize into tribes, which collectively worsen performance and systemic risk; higher agent capability can paradoxically increase failure rates.
AI.motivation: To understand how autonomous agents sharing constrained infrastructure may exhibit emergent collective behavior that degrades resource efficiency and stability, particularly through social-like tribalism.
AI.method: A simplified model where N agents each round choose to request one unit from a system with fixed capacity C. The study analyzes resulting dynamics, identifies three tribe archetypes (Aggressive, Conservative, Opportunistic) and their prevalence, and compares outcomes to baseline random (coin-flip) decisions, highlighting how smarter agents can increase systemic failure.
AI.result: Three main tribes emerge with observed proportions: Aggressive 27.3%, Conservative 24.7%, Opportunistic 48.1%. Smarter agents do not reduce overload or improve efficiency; in fact, they often perform worse than random decision-making and can raise the rate of systemic failure due to collective behavior.
AI.conclusion: Enhanced agent capability can backfire in shared-resource settings due to emergent tribal dynamics, underscoring the need for governance and coordination mechanisms to prevent detrimental collective behavior even as AI becomes more capable.

title: TrajTok: Learning Trajectory Tokens enables better Video Understanding
summary: Tokenization in video models, typically through patchification, generates an excessive and redundant number of tokens. This severely limits video efficiency and scalability. While recent trajectory-based tokenizers offer a promising solution by decoupling video duration from token count, they rely on complex external segmentation and tracking pipelines that are slow and task-agnostic. We propose TrajTok, an end-to-end video tokenizer module that is fully integrated and co-trained with video models for a downstream objective, dynamically adapting its token granularity to semantic complexity, independent of video duration. TrajTok contains a unified segmenter that performs implicit clustering over pixels in both space and time to directly produce object trajectories in a single forward pass. By prioritizing downstream adaptability over pixel-perfect segmentation fidelity, TrajTok is lightweight and efficient, yet empirically improves video understanding performance. With TrajTok, we implement a video CLIP model trained from scratch (TrajViT2). It achieves the best accuracy at scale across both classification and retrieval benchmarks, while maintaining efficiency comparable to the best token-merging methods. TrajTok also proves to be a versatile component beyond its role as a tokenizer. We show that it can be seamlessly integrated as either a probing head for pretrained visual features (TrajAdapter) or an alignment connector in vision-language models (TrajVLM) with especially strong performance in long-video reasoning.

AI.tldr: End-to-end, trajectory-based video tokenizer (TrajTok) dynamically adapts token granularity via implicit space-time clustering, co-trained with video models, enabling efficient, scalable tokenization and improved video understanding without external segmentation pipelines.
AI.motivation: Reduce token explosion from patch-based tokenization; avoid slow, task-agnostic external segmentation/tracking pipelines; create a tokenizer that is trainable end-to-end and adapts to semantic complexity regardless of video duration.
AI.method: A unified segmenter performs implicit clustering over pixels in space and time to generate object trajectories in a single forward pass; tokens are dynamically allocated based on semantic complexity; integrated and co-trained with downstream video models (e.g., TrajViT2).
AI.result: Empirically improves video understanding; achieves best accuracy at scale on classification and retrieval benchmarks; maintains efficiency on par with the best token-merging methods; can serve as probing head (TrajAdapter) or alignment connector (TrajVLM) for long-video reasoning.
AI.conclusion: TrajTok is a versatile, efficient end-to-end tokenizer that adapts to semantic structure and integrates with diverse video-model architectures, enabling strong downstream performance without relying on separate, heavy segmentation pipelines.

title: Predicting Tennis Serve directions with Machine Learning
summary: Serves, especially first serves, are very important in professional tennis. Servers choose their serve directions strategically to maximize their winning chances while trying to be unpredictable. On the other hand, returners try to predict serve directions to make good returns. The mind game between servers and returners is an important part of decision-making in professional tennis matches. To help understand the players' serve decisions, we have developed a machine learning method for predicting professional tennis players' first serve directions. Through feature engineering, our method achieves an average prediction accuracy of around 49\% for male players and 44\% for female players. Our analysis provides some evidence that top professional players use a mixed-strategy model in serving decisions and that fatigue might be a factor in choosing serve directions. Our analysis also suggests that contextual information is perhaps more important for returners' anticipatory reactions than previously thought.

AI.tldr: Machine learning approach to predict first-serve directions in professional tennis; results show modest accuracy and evidence for mixed strategies and fatigue effects.
AI.motivation: Understand strategic decision-making in serve direction and information asymmetry between servers and returners; quantify predictive signals and contextual factors.
AI.method: Supervised learning with feature engineering to predict first-serve direction; evaluation across male and female players; performance around 49% and 44%.
AI.result: Mean prediction accuracy: ~49% for men, ~44% for women; evidence that top players use mixed-strategy serving; fatigue may influence serve-direction choices; contextual info appears important for returners.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates strategic nature of serve decisions and potential fatigue/context effects; supports future work on game-theoretic and context-aware models of serving and anticipation.

title: Multi-Agent Large Language Model Based Emotional Detoxification Through Personalized Intensity Control for Consumer Protection
summary: In the attention economy, sensational content exposes consumers to excessive emotional stimulation, hindering calm decision-making. This study proposes Multi-Agent LLM-based Emotional deToxification (MALLET), a multi-agent information sanitization system consisting of four agents: Emotion Analysis, Emotion Adjustment, Balance Monitoring, and Personal Guide. The Emotion Analysis Agent quantifies stimulus intensity using a 6-emotion BERT classifier, and the Emotion Adjustment Agent rewrites texts into two presentation modes, BALANCED (neutralized text) and COOL (neutralized text + supplementary text), using an LLM. The Balance Monitoring Agent aggregates weekly information consumption patterns and generates personalized advice, while the Personal Guide Agent recommends a presentation mode according to consumer sensitivity. Experiments on 800 AG News articles demonstrated significant stimulus score reduction (up to 19.3%) and improved emotion balance while maintaining semantic preservation. Near-zero correlation between stimulus reduction and semantic preservation confirmed that the two are independently controllable. Category-level analysis revealed substantial reduction (17.8-33.8%) in Sports, Business, and Sci/Tech, whereas the effect was limited in the World category, where facts themselves are inherently high-stimulus. The proposed system provides a framework for supporting calm information reception of consumers without restricting access to the original text.

AI.tldr: A multi-agent LLM-based system (MALLET) reduces stimulus/emotional arousal in text through neutralized presentations, using four agents; evaluated on 800 AG News articles; shows reductions in stimulus and emotion, with semantic preservation maintained, category-sensitive effects; offers a framework for calm information reception without restricting access.
AI.motivation: In the attention economy sensational content causes excessive emotional stimulation that impairs calm decision-making; need for automatic information sanitization that preserves semantics and access.
AI.method: Four agents: Emotion Analysis (6-emotion BERT classifier) quantifies stimulus; Emotion Adjustment rewrites text into BALANCED and COOL using LLM; Balance Monitoring aggregates weekly consumption and yields personalized advice; Personal Guide recommends presentation mode by consumer sensitivity; evaluated on 800 AG News articles; metrics include stimulus reduction, semantic preservation, category-level analysis; analysis of correlations.
AI.result: Stimulus reduction up to 19.3%, improved emotion balance; near-zero correlation between stimulus reduction and semantic preservation; category reductions 17.8-33.8% in Sports, Business, Sci/Tech; World category less affected; preserves access to original text while sanitizing presentation.
AI.conclusion: MALLET offers a modular framework for calm information intake via automated sanitization without restricting content, with potential applicability across domains.

title: SceneTransporter: Optimal Transport-Guided Compositional Latent Diffusion for Single-Image Structured 3D Scene Generation
summary: We introduce SceneTransporter, an end-to-end framework for structured 3D scene generation from a single image. While existing methods generate part-level 3D objects, they often fail to organize these parts into distinct instances in open-world scenes. Through a debiased clustering probe, we reveal a critical insight: this failure stems from the lack of structural constraints within the model's internal assignment mechanism. Based on this finding, we reframe the task of structured 3D scene generation as a global correlation assignment problem. To solve this, SceneTransporter formulates and solves an entropic Optimal Transport (OT) objective within the denoising loop of the compositional DiT model. This formulation imposes two powerful structural constraints. First, the resulting transport plan gates cross-attention to enforce an exclusive, one-to-one routing of image patches to part-level 3D latents, preventing entanglement. Second, the competitive nature of the transport encourages the grouping of similar patches, a process that is further regularized by an edge-based cost, to form coherent objects and prevent fragmentation. Extensive experiments show that SceneTransporter outperforms existing methods on open-world scene generation, significantly improving instance-level coherence and geometric fidelity. Code and models will be publicly available at https://2019epwl.github.io/SceneTransporter/.

AI.tldr: Proposes SceneTransporter, a framework for end-to-end structured 3D scene generation from a single image that enforces one-to-one patch-to-part latents and patch clustering via entropic Optimal Transport to improve instance coherence and geometric fidelity in open-world scenes.
AI.motivation: Existing part-level 3D generation methods struggle to organize parts into distinct instances in open-world scenes due to lack of structural constraints in their internal assignment mechanisms.
AI.method: Reframe structured 3D scene generation as a global correlation assignment problem and solve it by integrating an entropic Optimal Transport objective into the denoising loop of a compositional DiT model. OT imposes (i) exclusive, one-to-one routing of image patches to part-level latent codes via cross-attention gating, and (ii) competitive patch grouping guided by an edge-based cost to form coherent objects, reducing fragmentation.
AI.result: Extensive experiments demonstrate that SceneTransporter achieves superior open-world instance coherence and geometric fidelity compared to prior methods.
AI.conclusion: Global correlation assignment with entropic OT within a denoising-based 3D scene generator yields stronger structural constraints, enabling reliable instance-level organization and improved scene quality; code and models will be released.

title: Coarse-to-Fine Learning of Dynamic Causal Structures
summary: Learning the dynamic causal structure of time series is a challenging problem. Most existing approaches rely on distributional or structural invariance to uncover underlying causal dynamics, assuming stationary or partially stationary causality. However, these assumptions often conflict with the complex, time-varying causal relationships observed in real-world systems. This motivates the need for methods that address fully dynamic causality, where both instantaneous and lagged dependencies evolve over time. Such a setting poses significant challenges for the efficiency and stability of causal discovery. To address these challenges, we introduce DyCausal, a dynamic causal structure learning framework. DyCausal leverages convolutional networks to capture causal patterns within coarse-grained time windows, and then applies linear interpolation to refine causal structures at each time step, thereby recovering fine-grained and time-varying causal graphs. In addition, we propose an acyclic constraint based on matrix norm scaling, which improves efficiency while effectively constraining loops in evolving causal structures. Comprehensive evaluations on both synthetic and real-world datasets demonstrate that DyCausal achieves superior performance compared to existing methods, offering a stable and efficient approach for identifying fully dynamic causal structures from coarse to fine.

AI.tldr: Dynamic causal structure learning (DyCausal) tackles fully dynamic, time-varying causal graphs, including instantaneous and lagged dependencies, using a coarse-to-fine approach: CNNs capture patterns in coarse windows, followed by linear interpolation to produce time-resolved graphs; a matrix-norm based acyclicity constraint enforces DAG structure efficiently as graphs evolve. Empirical results on synthetic and real data show superior performance and stability over existing methods.
AI.motivation: Many real-world time series exhibit nonstationary, evolving causal relationships. Traditional methods assuming stationarity or partial invariance fail to capture fully dynamic causality where both instantaneous and lagged dependencies change over time. A scalable, stable method is needed to infer dynamic causal graphs from coarse to fine temporal resolution.
AI.method: 1) Use convolutional networks to learn causal patterns within coarse-grained time windows. 2) Apply linear interpolation to refine the causal structure at each time step, producing a fine-grained dynamic graph. 3) Impose an acyclicity constraint via a matrix-norm scaling approach to enforce DAG structure efficiently as the causality evolves. 4) Evaluate on synthetic and real datasets, comparing with existing dynamic causal discovery methods.
AI.result: DyCausal demonstrates superior performance to existing methods in recovering fully dynamic causal graphs, offering stability and efficiency across the coarse-to-fine refinement process and handling evolving instantaneous and lagged dependencies.
AI.conclusion: DyCausal presents a scalable framework for fully dynamic causal discovery, effectively transitioning from coarse to fine temporal resolution while maintaining acyclic, evolving causal graphs through a norm-based constraint, with empirical validation on diverse datasets.

title: On Sample-Efficient Generalized Planning via Learned Transition Models
comment: 14 pages; This is an extended version of a short paper accepted at ICAPS 2026 under the same title
summary: Generalized planning studies the construction of solution strategies that generalize across families of planning problems sharing a common domain model, formally defined by a transition function $γ: S \times A \rightarrow S$. Classical approaches achieve such generalization through symbolic abstractions and explicit reasoning over $γ$. In contrast, recent Transformer-based planners, such as PlanGPT and Plansformer, largely cast generalized planning as direct action-sequence prediction, bypassing explicit transition modeling. While effective on in-distribution instances, these approaches typically require large datasets and model sizes, and often suffer from state drift in long-horizon settings due to the absence of explicit world-state evolution. In this work, we formulate generalized planning as a transition-model learning problem, in which a neural model explicitly approximates the successor-state function $\hatγ \approx γ$ and generates plans by rolling out symbolic state trajectories. Instead of predicting actions directly, the model autoregressively predicts intermediate world states, thereby learning the domain dynamics as an implicit world model. To study size-invariant generalization and sample efficiency, we systematically evaluate multiple state representations and neural architectures, including relational graph encodings. Our results show that learning explicit transition models yields higher out-of-distribution satisficing-plan success than direct action-sequence prediction in multiple domains, while achieving these gains with significantly fewer training instances and smaller models. This is an extended version of a short paper accepted at ICAPS 2026 under the same title.

AI.tldr: Proposes learning an explicit neural successor-state model for generalized planning, predicting intermediate world states to roll out plans, achieving better out-of-distribution performance with less data and smaller models than direct action-sequence planners.
AI.motivation: Address limitations of transformer-based generalized planners (data hunger, brittle OOD generalization, and state drift in long horizons) by learning a dynamics model to capture domain evolution.
AI.method: Train a neural model to approximate the transition function γ (hatγ ≈ γ) and generate plans by autoregressively predicting intermediate world states, i.e., rolling out state trajectories rather than directly predicting actions; explore multiple state representations, including relational graph encodings; evaluate size-invariant generalization and sample efficiency across domains.
AI.result: Explicit transition models yield higher out-of-distribution satisficing-plan success across multiple domains, with significantly fewer training instances and smaller models compared to direct action-sequence prediction; relational encodings further improve performance.
AI.conclusion: Learning an explicit transition model for generalized planning is advantageous, enabling better generalization and data efficiency; this extended ICAPS 2026 version strengthens the case for explicit world-models in planning.

title: Robust Human Trajectory Prediction via Self-Supervised Skeleton Representation Learning
summary: Human trajectory prediction plays a crucial role in applications such as autonomous navigation and video surveillance. While recent works have explored the integration of human skeleton sequences to complement trajectory information, skeleton data in real-world environments often suffer from missing joints caused by occlusions. These disturbances significantly degrade prediction accuracy, indicating the need for more robust skeleton representations. We propose a robust trajectory prediction method that incorporates a self-supervised skeleton representation model pretrained with masked autoencoding. Experimental results in occlusion-prone scenarios show that our method improves robustness to missing skeletal data without sacrificing prediction accuracy, and consistently outperforms baseline models in clean-to-moderate missingness regimes.

AI.tldr: A robust trajectory prediction approach using a self-supervised, masked autoencoding skeleton representation to handle occlusion-induced missing joints, achieving improved robustness with minimal accuracy loss.
AI.motivation: Skeleton data in real-world scenes often suffer from occlusions, causing missing joints that degrade trajectory prediction performance. A robust representation is needed to maintain accuracy under missing data.
AI.method: Pretrain a skeleton representation model with a masked autoencoding objective in a self-supervised manner and integrate it into the trajectory predictor to better handle missing joints during inference.
AI.result: The method improves robustness to missing skeletal data in occlusion-prone scenarios and does not sacrifice overall prediction accuracy; it consistently outperforms baselines under clean-to-moderate missingness regimes.
AI.conclusion: Self-supervised masked autoencoding of skeleton representations enhances occlusion robustness in trajectory prediction, offering improved performance in realistic, imperfect skeletal data environments.

title: Persistent Nonnegative Matrix Factorization via Multi-Scale Graph Regularization
summary: Matrix factorization techniques, especially Nonnegative Matrix Factorization (NMF), have been widely used for dimensionality reduction and interpretable data representation. However, existing NMF-based methods are inherently single-scale and fail to capture the evolution of connectivity structures across resolutions. In this work, we propose persistent nonnegative matrix factorization (pNMF), a scale-parameterized family of NMF problems, that produces a sequence of persistence-aligned embeddings rather than a single one. By leveraging persistent homology, we identify a canonical minimal sufficient scale set at which the underlying connectivity undergoes qualitative changes. These canonical scales induce a sequence of graph Laplacians, leading to a coupled NMF formulation with scale-wise geometric regularization and explicit cross-scale consistency constraint. We analyze the structural properties of the embeddings along the scale parameter and establish bounds on their increments between consecutive scales. The resulting model defines a nontrivial solution path across scales, rather than a single factorization, which poses new computational challenges. We develop a sequential alternating optimization algorithm with guaranteed convergence. Numerical experiments on synthetic and single-cell RNA sequencing datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed approach in multi-scale low-rank embeddings.

AI.tldr: pNMF introduces a scale-parameterized, persistent nonnegative matrix factorization that yields a sequence of alignment-preserving embeddings across canonical scales identified by persistent homology; it couples NMF across scales with scale-wise geometric regularization and a cross-scale consistency constraint, with a convergent optimization algorithm and validation on synthetic and scRNA-seq data.
AI.motivation: Standard NMF is single-scale and cannot capture the evolution of connectivity across resolutions. Persistent homology can reveal canonical scales where qualitative connectivity changes occur, enabling multi-scale, interpretable embeddings.
AI.method: Define a scale parameter and compute a canonical set of scales via persistent homology; generate a sequence of graph Laplacians; formulate a coupled, scale-wise NMF with geometric regularization and cross-scale consistency constraints; analyze embedding increment bounds across scales; propose a sequential alternating optimization algorithm with guaranteed convergence.
AI.result: The framework yields a nontrivial solution path across scales with bounded increments between successive scales; the optimization converges; numerical experiments on synthetic data and single-cell RNA-seq datasets show the method produces effective multi-scale low-rank embeddings.
AI.conclusion: pNMF provides a principled, multi-scale extension of NMF that captures the evolution of connectivity across resolutions, supported by canonical scales, cross-scale regularization, and a convergent optimization method; it is effective on synthetic and biological data, offering a new tool for multi-scale representation learning.

title: The Trinity of Consistency as a Defining Principle for General World Models
summary: The construction of World Models capable of learning, simulating, and reasoning about objective physical laws constitutes a foundational challenge in the pursuit of Artificial General Intelligence. Recent advancements represented by video generation models like Sora have demonstrated the potential of data-driven scaling laws to approximate physical dynamics, while the emerging Unified Multimodal Model (UMM) offers a promising architectural paradigm for integrating perception, language, and reasoning. Despite these advances, the field still lacks a principled theoretical framework that defines the essential properties requisite for a General World Model. In this paper, we propose that a World Model must be grounded in the Trinity of Consistency: Modal Consistency as the semantic interface, Spatial Consistency as the geometric basis, and Temporal Consistency as the causal engine. Through this tripartite lens, we systematically review the evolution of multimodal learning, revealing a trajectory from loosely coupled specialized modules toward unified architectures that enable the synergistic emergence of internal world simulators. To complement this conceptual framework, we introduce CoW-Bench, a benchmark centered on multi-frame reasoning and generation scenarios. CoW-Bench evaluates both video generation models and UMMs under a unified evaluation protocol. Our work establishes a principled pathway toward general world models, clarifying both the limitations of current systems and the architectural requirements for future progress.

AI.tldr: A tripartite 'Trinity of Consistency' framework (Modal, Spatial, Temporal) for World Models; a literature review of multimodal learning; and CoW-Bench, a benchmark for multi-frame reasoning/generation evaluating video models and Unified Multimodal Models under a unified protocol.
AI.motivation: To ground World Models in a principled theory describing essential properties for learning, simulating, and reasoning about physical laws, addressing the lack of a unified framework.
AI.method: Propose the Trinity of Consistency as a conceptual framework; perform a systematic review of multimodal learning evolution toward unified architectures; design CoW-Bench with unified evaluation protocol.
AI.result: Articulates architectural requirements and current limitations; provides a principled pathway toward general world models; introduces CoW-Bench as a standardized evaluation resource.
AI.conclusion: The framework clarifies what is needed for future progress, bridging video-generation models and UMMs toward coherent world simulators.

title: GSTurb: Gaussian Splatting for Atmospheric Turbulence Mitigation
summary: Atmospheric turbulence causes significant image degradation due to pixel displacement (tilt) and blur, particularly in long-range imaging applications. In this paper, we propose a novel framework for atmospheric turbulence mitigation, GSTurb, which integrates optical flow-guided tilt correction and Gaussian splatting for modeling non-isoplanatic blur. The framework employs Gaussian parameters to represent tilt and blur, and optimizes them across multiple frames to enhance restoration. Experimental results on the ATSyn-static dataset demonstrate the effectiveness of our method, achieving a peak PSNR of 27.67 dB and SSIM of 0.8735. Compared to the state-of-the-art method, GSTurb improves PSNR by 1.3 dB (a 4.5% increase) and SSIM by 0.048 (a 5.8% increase). Additionally, on real datasets, including the TSRWGAN Real-World and CLEAR datasets, GSTurb outperforms existing methods, showing significant improvements in both qualitative and quantitative performance. These results highlight that combining optical flow-guided tilt correction with Gaussian splatting effectively enhances image restoration under both synthetic and real-world turbulence conditions. The code for this method will be available at https://github.com/DuhlLiamz/3DGS_turbulence/tree/main.

AI.tldr: GSTurb integrates optical-flow-guided tilt correction with Gaussian splatting to model non-isoplanatic blur, optimizing tilt/blur Gaussian parameters across frames. It achieves notable gains on synthetic ATSyn-static (PSNR 27.67 dB, SSIM 0.8735; +1.3 dB PSNR, +0.048 SSIM over SOTA) and shows improvements on real TSRWGAN Real-World and CLEAR datasets; code released.
AI.motivation: Atmospheric turbulence introduces frame-to-frame tilt and space-variant blur, degrading long-range imaging. A method that jointly models tilt and blur across multiple frames can improve restoration, particularly under non-isoplanatic conditions.
AI.method: Optical flow-guided tilt correction combined with Gaussian splatting to model non-uniform blur. Gaussian parameters represent tilt and blur; parameters are optimized jointly over multiple frames to restore images.
AI.result: On ATSyn-static, GSTurb achieves PSNR 27.67 dB and SSIM 0.8735, outperforming the state-of-the-art by 1.3 dB PSNR (4.5% gain) and 0.048 SSIM (5.8% gain). Real datasets (TSRWGAN Real-World, CLEAR) also show favorable qualitative and quantitative improvements compared to existing methods.
AI.conclusion: GSTurb demonstrates that jointly addressing tilt and non-isoplanatic blur through Gaussian parameterization and multi-frame optimization yields effective atmospheric turbulence mitigation for both synthetic and real data; code is publicly available.

title: LUMOS: Democratizing SciML Workflows with L0-Regularized Learning for Unified Feature and Parameter Adaptation
summary: The rapid growth of scientific machine learning (SciML) has accelerated discovery across diverse domains, yet designing effective SciML models remains a challenging task. In practice, building such models often requires substantial prior knowledge and manual expertise, particularly in determining which input features to use and how large the model should be. We introduce LUMOS, an end-to-end framework based on L0-regularized learning that unifies feature selection and model pruning to democratize SciML model design. By employing semi-stochastic gating and reparameterization techniques, LUMOS dynamically selects informative features and prunes redundant parameters during training, reducing the reliance on manual tuning while maintaining predictive accuracy. We evaluate LUMOS across 13 diverse SciML workloads, including cosmology and molecular sciences, and demonstrate its effectiveness and generalizability. Experiments on 13 SciML models show that LUMOS achieves 71.45% parameter reduction and a 6.4x inference speedup on average. Furthermore, Distributed Data Parallel (DDP) training on up to eight GPUs confirms the scalability of

AI.tldr: LUMOS is an end-to-end L0-regularized framework for simultaneous feature selection and model pruning in scientific machine learning, using semi-stochastic gating and reparameterization to reduce manual tuning; evaluated on 13 SciML workloads, achieving ~71.45% parameter reduction and ~6.4x average inference speedup, with scalable multi-GPU training.
AI.motivation: SciML model design currently requires substantial prior knowledge for selecting inputs and sizing models; automating feature selection and pruning can democratize model design while aiming to preserve accuracy.
AI.method: LUMOS integrates L0-regularized learning with semi-stochastic gating and reparameterization to dynamically select informative features and prune redundant parameters during training, in an end-to-end framework; evaluates with Distributed Data Parallel (DDP) across multi-GPU setups.
AI.result: On 13 SciML workloads, LUMOS reports 71.45% parameter reduction and 6.4x average inference speedup; scalability demonstrated with DDP across up to eight GPUs, indicating generalizability across domains like cosmology and molecular sciences.
AI.conclusion: LUMOS reduces manual tuning in SciML model design, maintains predictive accuracy, and generalizes across diverse SciML tasks, suggesting a practical path toward democratizing SciML model development through automated feature selection and pruning.

title: PATRA: Pattern-Aware Alignment and Balanced Reasoning for Time Series Question Answering
summary: Time series reasoning demands both the perception of complex dynamics and logical depth. However, existing LLM-based approaches exhibit two limitations: they often treat time series merely as text or images, failing to capture the patterns like trends and seasonalities needed to answer specific questions; and when trained on a mix of simple and complex tasks, simpler objectives often dominate the learning process, hindering the development of deep reasoning capabilities. To address these limitations, we propose the Pattern-Aware Alignment and Balanced Reasoning model (PATRA), introducing a pattern-aware mechanism that extracts trend and seasonality patterns from time series to achieve deep alignment. Furthermore, we design a task-aware balanced reward to harmonize learning across tasks of varying difficulty, incentivizing the generation of coherent Chains of Thought. Extensive experiments show that PATRA outperforms strong baselines across diverse Time Series Question Answering (TSQA) tasks, demonstrating superior cross-modal understanding and reasoning capability.

AI.tldr: PATRA introduces a pattern-aware time-series reasoning framework and a task-balanced reward to improve deep reasoning in TSQA by extracting trend/seasonality and aligning learning across tasks.
AI.motivation: Time series QA requires both perception of dynamics and deep reasoning; current LLM-based methods treat series as text/images and are biased toward easier tasks when trained on mixed difficulties, limiting complex reasoning.
AI.method: Pattern-aware mechanism to extract and utilize trend and seasonality patterns for alignment; a task-aware balanced reward to harmonize learning across tasks of varying difficulty and promote coherent Chains of Thought.
AI.result: Empirical results show PATRA outperforms strong baselines on diverse TSQA tasks, indicating improved cross-modal understanding and reasoning.
AI.conclusion: Pattern-aware alignment plus balanced reward yields robust TSQA performance and suggests general applicability to time-series reasoning with deep Chains of Thought.

title: PhotoAgent: Agentic Photo Editing with Exploratory Visual Aesthetic Planning
comment: A fully automated, intelligent photo-editing agent that autonomously plans multi-step aesthetic enhancements, smartly chooses diverse editing tools, and enables everyday users to achieve professional-looking results without crafting complex prompts. Project page: https://github.com/mdyao/PhotoAgent
summary: With the recent fast development of generative models, instruction-based image editing has shown great potential in generating high-quality images. However, the quality of editing highly depends on carefully designed instructions, placing the burden of task decomposition and sequencing entirely on the user. To achieve autonomous image editing, we present PhotoAgent, a system that advances image editing through explicit aesthetic planning. Specifically, PhotoAgent formulates autonomous image editing as a long-horizon decision-making problem. It reasons over user aesthetic intent, plans multi-step editing actions via tree search, and iteratively refines results through closed-loop execution with memory and visual feedback, without requiring step-by-step user prompts. To support reliable evaluation in real-world scenarios, we introduce UGC-Edit, an aesthetic evaluation benchmark consisting of 7,000 photos and a learned aesthetic reward model. We also construct a test set containing 1,017 photos to systematically assess autonomous photo editing performance. Extensive experiments demonstrate that PhotoAgent consistently improves both instruction adherence and visual quality compared with baseline methods. The project page is https://github.com/mdyao/PhotoAgent.

AI.tldr: PhotoAgent enables autonomous, instruction-free image editing via long-horizon planning and closed-loop execution; introduces UGC-Edit aesthetic benchmark and a reward model; shows improved editing quality over baselines.
AI.motivation: Address the dependency on handcrafted user instructions for image editing by enabling autonomous planning aligned with user aesthetic intent.
AI.method: Model editing as a long-horizon decision process. Reason about aesthetic intent, plan multi-step edits with tree search, and iteratively refine results through memory and visual feedback in a closed loop without requiring step-by-step prompts.
AI.result: Proposes UGC-Edit dataset (7,000 photos) with a learned aesthetic reward model; establishes a test set of 1,017 photos; experiments show PhotoAgent improves instruction adherence and visual quality versus baselines.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates that autonomous aesthetic planning can enhance image editing quality and reliability, with a concrete evaluation benchmark for future work.

title: RAIN-Merging: A Gradient-Free Method to Enhance Instruction Following in Large Reasoning Models with Preserved Thinking Format
