Train and compare Random Forest, XGBoost, and LightGBM models. Evaluate across forecasting horizons. Perform feature selection to a 5-feature subset. Apply Shapley value-based counterfactual analysis on the best-performing model.
AI.result: Models achieved balanced performance with 77.6% sensitivity and 96.3% specificity at a 10% rebalancing rate; results robust across forecasting horizons. The tabular approach outperformed LSTM networks. Reducing to five features maintained accuracy with simpler interpretation. Shapley-based counterfactuals identified minimal input changes to reverse fouling predictions.
AI.conclusion: Interpretable machine learning models are viable for predicting membrane fouling during CRRT. Integrating prediction with counterfactual analysis offers practical clinical value to guide therapeutic adjustments and improve patient management.

title: Efficient Encoder-Free Fourier-based 3D Large Multimodal Model
summary: Large Multimodal Models (LMMs) that process 3D data typically rely on heavy, pre-trained visual encoders to extract geometric features. While recent 2D LMMs have begun to eliminate such encoders for efficiency and scalability, extending this paradigm to 3D remains challenging due to the unordered and large-scale nature of point clouds. This leaves a critical unanswered question: How can we design an LMM that tokenizes unordered 3D data effectively and efficiently without a cumbersome encoder? We propose Fase3D, the first efficient encoder-free Fourier-based 3D scene LMM. Fase3D tackles the challenges of scalability and permutation invariance with a novel tokenizer that combines point cloud serialization and the Fast Fourier Transform (FFT) to approximate self-attention. This design enables an effective and computationally minimal architecture, built upon three key innovations: First, we represent large scenes compactly via structured superpoints. Second, our space-filling curve serialization followed by an FFT enables efficient global context modeling and graph-based token merging. Lastly, our Fourier-augmented LoRA adapters inject global frequency-aware interactions into the LLMs at a negligible cost. Fase3D achieves performance comparable to encoder-based 3D LMMs while being significantly more efficient in computation and parameters. Project website: https://tev-fbk.github.io/Fase3D.

AI.tldr: Encoder-free, Fourier-based 3D LMM (Fase3D) that tokenizes unordered point clouds via space-filling curve serialization and FFT, enabling scalable global context modeling with structured superpoints and Fourier-augmented LoRA adapters; achieves comparable performance to encoder-based 3D LMMs with much lower compute and parameter cost.
AI.motivation: To circumvent the reliance on heavy pre-trained visual encoders for 3D LMMs and address the permutation-invariance and scalability challenges of large point clouds, by designing an effective tokenizer and global-context mechanism that does not rely on traditional encoders.
AI.method: 1) Represent large 3D scenes with structured superpoints to compactify data. 2) Serialize via a space-filling curve and apply FFT to approximate self-attention for global context modeling and graph-based token merging. 3) Use Fourier-augmented LoRA adapters to inject global frequency-aware interactions into LLMs with negligible extra cost.
AI.result: Fase3D achieves performance comparable to encoder-based 3D LMMs while significantly reducing computation and parameter count, demonstrating encoder-free viability for 3D LMMs.
AI.conclusion: An encoder-free, Fourier-based tokenizer coupled with frequency-aware adapters enables scalable, permutation-invariant 3D LMMs that match encoder-based models in performance but with substantial efficiency gains; further exploration may focus on robustness to diverse scenes and integration with more LLM backbones.

title: NoRA: Breaking the Linear Ceiling of Low-Rank Adaptation via Manifold Expansion
summary: Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) dominates parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT). However, it faces a critical ``linear ceiling'' in complex reasoning tasks: simply increasing the rank yields diminishing returns due to intrinsic linear constraints. We introduce NoRA (Non-linear Rank Adaptation), a weight-level parallel adapter that injects SiLU gating and structural dropout to induce manifold expansion. On the SlimOrca benchmark, NoRA breaks this linear barrier: NoRA remarkably at rank 64 (PPL 3.89) outperforms LoRA at rank 512 (PPL 3.90), demonstrating superior spectral efficiency. This advantage generalizes to mathematical reasoning, where NoRA achieves a perplexity of 1.97 on MathInstruct, significantly surpassing LoRA's saturation point of 2.07. Mechanism analysis via Singular Value Decomposition (SVD) confirms that NoRA activates the dormant tail of the singular value spectrum, effectively preventing the rank collapse observed in linear methods.

AI.tldr: NoRA introduces a non-linear, weight-level parallel adapter with SiLU gating and structural dropout to expand the model's effective rank, breaking LoRA's linear performance ceiling. It achieves higher spectral efficiency on SlimOrca (rank 64, PPL 3.89 vs LoRA rank 512, PPL 3.90) and MathInstruct (PPL 1.97 vs LoRA 2.07). SVD analysis shows NoRA activates the tail of the singular value spectrum, mitigating rank collapse observed in linear adapters.
AI.motivation: Address the linear limitation of parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) methods like LoRA, where simply increasing rank yields diminishing returns due to intrinsic linear constraints. Propose a non-linear mechanism to expand the solution manifold.
AI.method: Introduce NoRA, a weight-level parallel adapter that injects SiLU gating and structural dropout to induce non-linear transformations in the adaptation path. The approach is analyzed via singular value decomposition (SVD) to assess spectral expansion and rank behavior.
AI.result: Empirically, NoRA outperforms LoRA at substantially lower ranks on SlimOrca (PPL 3.89 at rank 64 vs LoRA's 3.90 at rank 512). On MathInstruct, NoRA achieves PPL 1.97, surpassing LoRA's saturation at 2.07. SVD indicates activation of dormant tail singular values, suggesting a broader effective rank and avoidance of rank collapse seen in linear adapters.
AI.conclusion: Non-linear Rank Adaptation via gating and structural dropout yields higher spectral efficiency than linear PEFT methods, enabling better performance at lower ranks and offering a mechanism to overcome the intrinsic limits of linear adapters. Further validation across diverse tasks and analysis of computational overhead would reinforce the approach.

title: DyaDiT: A Multi-Modal Diffusion Transformer for Socially Favorable Dyadic Gesture Generation
summary: Generating realistic conversational gestures are essential for achieving natural, socially engaging interactions with digital humans. However, existing methods typically map a single audio stream to a single speaker's motion, without considering social context or modeling the mutual dynamics between two people engaging in conversation. We present DyaDiT, a multi-modal diffusion transformer that generates contextually appropriate human motion from dyadic audio signals. Trained on Seamless Interaction Dataset, DyaDiT takes dyadic audio with optional social-context tokens to produce context-appropriate motion. It fuses information from both speakers to capture interaction dynamics, uses a motion dictionary to encode motion priors, and can optionally utilize the conversational partner's gestures to produce more responsive motion. We evaluate DyaDiT on standard motion generation metrics and conduct quantitative user studies, demonstrating that it not only surpasses existing methods on objective metrics but is also strongly preferred by users, highlighting its robustness and socially favorable motion generation. Code and models will be released upon acceptance.

AI.tldr: A diffusion-based multi-modal transformer (DyaDiT) generates dyadic conversational gestures by fusing both speakers' audio with optional social-context tokens, leveraging a motion dictionary and motion priors, trained on the Seamless Interaction Dataset; it outperforms prior methods on objective metrics and user studies; code/models will be released.
AI.motivation: Existing gesture generation typically maps a single audio stream to one speaker's motion, neglecting social context and mutual interaction dynamics in dyadic conversations; there is a need for context-aware, interactive gesture generation for natural digital-human interactions.
AI.method: DyaDiT is a multi-modal diffusion transformer that ingests dyadic audio signals with optional social-context tokens. It fuses information from both speakers to capture interaction dynamics, uses a motion dictionary to encode motion priors, and can optionally utilize the conversational partner's gestures to produce more responsive motion. Trained on the Seamless Interaction Dataset, it outputs context-appropriate human motion.
AI.result: Evaluations on standard motion generation metrics and quantitative user studies show DyaDiT surpasses existing methods on objective metrics and is strongly preferred by users, indicating robust and socially favorable motion generation.
AI.conclusion: DyaDiT effectively models dyadic interaction in gesture generation by jointly leveraging dual-audio, social context, and motion priors, enabling more natural and responsive gestures for digital humans; code and models are to be released upon acceptance.

title: Generalization Bounds of Stochastic Gradient Descent in Homogeneous Neural Networks
summary: Algorithmic stability is among the most potent techniques in generalization analysis. However, its derivation usually requires a stepsize $η_t = \mathcal{O}(1/t)$ under non-convex training regimes, where $t$ denotes iterations. This rigid decay of the stepsize potentially impedes optimization and may not align with practical scenarios. In this paper, we derive the generalization bounds under the homogeneous neural network regimes, proving that this regime enables slower stepsize decay of order $Ω(1/\sqrt{t})$ under mild assumptions. We further extend the theoretical results from several aspects, e.g., non-Lipschitz regimes. This finding is broadly applicable, as homogeneous neural networks encompass fully-connected and convolutional neural networks with ReLU and LeakyReLU activations.

AI.tldr: In homogeneous neural networks, stability-based generalization bounds permit a slower learning-rate decay, Ω(1/√t), instead of the usual Ω(1/t); they extend to non-Lipschitz cases and apply to ReLU/LeakyReLU architectures.
AI.motivation: To relax the restrictive 1/t stepsize decay in algorithmic stability analyses, which can impede optimization and misalign with practical training.
AI.method: Derive stability-based generalization bounds under homogeneous network regimes by exploiting homogeneity; relax assumptions to non-Lipschitz activations; demonstrate applicability to fully-connected and convolutional nets with ReLU/LeakyReLU.
AI.result: Theoretical generalization bounds hold with stepsize decay of order Ω(1/√t) under mild assumptions; extended to non-Lipschitz regimes.
AI.conclusion: Homogeneous neural networks enable slower stepsize decay in stability-based generalization bounds, broadening applicability to common architectures and activations.

title: AgentVista: Evaluating Multimodal Agents in Ultra-Challenging Realistic Visual Scenarios
comment: The project website is available at \url{https://agentvista-bench.github.io/}, and the code is available at \url{https://github.com/hkust-nlp/AgentVista}
summary: Real-world multimodal agents solve multi-step workflows grounded in visual evidence. For example, an agent can troubleshoot a device by linking a wiring photo to a schematic and validating the fix with online documentation, or plan a trip by interpreting a transit map and checking schedules under routing constraints. However, existing multimodal benchmarks mainly evaluate single-turn visual reasoning or specific tool skills, and they do not fully capture the realism, visual subtlety, and long-horizon tool use that practical agents require. We introduce AgentVista, a benchmark for generalist multimodal agents that spans 25 sub-domains across 7 categories, pairing realistic and detail-rich visual scenarios with natural hybrid tool use. Tasks require long-horizon tool interactions across modalities, including web search, image search, page navigation, and code-based operations for both image processing and general programming. Comprehensive evaluation of state-of-the-art models exposes significant gaps in their ability to carry out long-horizon multimodal tool use. Even the best model in our evaluation, Gemini-3-Pro with tools, achieves only 27.3% overall accuracy, and hard instances can require more than 25 tool-calling turns. We expect AgentVista to accelerate the development of more capable and reliable multimodal agents for realistic and ultra-challenging problem solving.

AI.tldr: Introduces AgentVista, a large-scale multimodal-agent benchmark spanning 25 sub-domains and 7 categories for long-horizon multimodal tool use; current SOTA underperforms (27.3% accuracy), highlighting gaps and need for more capable agents.
AI.motivation: To address the gap between existing benchmarks (focused on single-turn visual reasoning or limited tool skills) and real-world multi-step workflows that require visual reasoning and long-horizon cross-modal tool use.
AI.method: Construct a benchmark with 25 sub-domains across 7 categories featuring realistic, detail-rich visuals and natural hybrid tool use. Tasks demand long-horizon tool interactions across modalities (web search, image search, page navigation, code-based image processing and programming). Evaluate state-of-the-art models to assess capabilities in long-horizon multimodal tool use.
AI.result: Comprehensive evaluation reveals significant gaps in current models' ability to perform long-horizon multimodal tool use. The best model (Gemini-3-Pro with tools) attains only 27.3% overall accuracy; hard instances may require more than 25 tool-calling turns.
AI.conclusion: AgentVista is poised to accelerate development of more capable and reliable multimodal agents for realistic and ultra-challenging problem solving.

title: MSINO: Curvature-Aware Sobolev Optimization for Manifold Neural Networks
comment: 32 pages, 6 figures. Submitted for journal consideration
summary:
We introduce Manifold Sobolev Informed Neural Optimization (MSINO), a curvature aware training framework for neural networks defined on Riemannian manifolds. The method replaces standard Euclidean derivative supervision with a covariant Sobolev loss that aligns gradients using parallel transport and improves stability via a Laplace Beltrami smoothness regularization term.
  Building on classical results in Riemannian optimization and Sobolev theory on manifolds, we derive geometry dependent constants that yield (i) a Descent Lemma with a manifold Sobolev smoothness constant, (ii) a Sobolev Polyak Lojasiewicz inequality giving linear convergence guarantees for Riemannian gradient descent and stochastic gradient descent under explicit step size bounds, and (iii) a two step Newton Sobolev method with local quadratic contraction in curvature controlled neighborhoods.
  Unlike prior Sobolev training in Euclidean space, MSINO provides training time guarantees that explicitly track curvature and transported Jacobians. Applications include surface imaging, physics informed learning settings, and robotics on Lie groups such as SO(3) and SE(3). The framework unifies value and gradient based learning with curvature aware convergence guarantees for neural training on manifolds.

AI.tldr: A curvature-aware neural optimization framework on manifolds (MSINO) using covariant Sobolev loss, parallel transport, and Laplace–Beltrami regularization to provide convergence guarantees with geometry-dependent constants.
AI.motivation: To address unstable gradient supervision and ill-conditioning when training neural networks on non-Euclidean domains by incorporating intrinsic geometry into optimization.
AI.method: Replace Euclidean derivatives with covariant Sobolev loss; align gradients via parallel transport; add Laplace-Beltrami smoothness regularization; derive a Descent Lemma with manifold Sobolev smoothness constant, a Sobolev Polyak–Lojasiewicz inequality for linear convergence of Riemannian gradient descent/SGD with explicit step-size bounds, and a two-step Newton–Sobolev method with local quadratic contraction in curvature-controlled neighborhoods.
AI.result: Derives geometry-dependent constants yielding convergence guarantees for Riemannian gradient descent/SGD and a two-step Newton method; provides training-time guarantees that explicitly track curvature and transported Jacobians; applicable to surface imaging, physics-informed learning, and robotics on Lie groups like SO(3) and SE(3).
AI.conclusion: MSINO unifies value- and gradient-based learning on manifolds with explicit curvature-aware convergence guarantees, offering broader applicability and improved stability for neural training on curved spaces.

title: Learning Continuous Wasserstein Barycenter Space for Generalized All-in-One Image Restoration
summary: Despite substantial advances in all-in-one image restoration for addressing diverse degradations within a unified model, existing methods remain vulnerable to out-of-distribution degradations, thereby limiting their generalization in real-world scenarios. To tackle the challenge, this work is motivated by the intuition that multisource degraded feature distributions are induced by different degradation-specific shifts from an underlying degradation-agnostic distribution, and recovering such a shared distribution is thus crucial for achieving generalization across degradations. With this insight, we propose BaryIR, a representation learning framework that aligns multisource degraded features in the Wasserstein barycenter (WB) space, which models a degradation-agnostic distribution by minimizing the average of Wasserstein distances to multisource degraded distributions. We further introduce residual subspaces, whose embeddings are mutually contrasted while remaining orthogonal to the WB embeddings. Consequently, BaryIR explicitly decouples two orthogonal spaces: a WB space that encodes the degradation-agnostic invariant contents shared across degradations, and residual subspaces that adaptively preserve the degradation-specific knowledge. This disentanglement mitigates overfitting to in-distribution degradations and enables adaptive restoration grounded on the degradation-agnostic shared invariance. Extensive experiments demonstrate that BaryIR performs competitively against state-of-the-art all-in-one methods. Notably, BaryIR generalizes well to unseen degradations (\textit{e.g.,} types and levels) and shows remarkable robustness in learning generalized features, even when trained on limited degradation types and evaluated on real-world data with mixed degradations.

AI.tldr: BaryIR aligns multisource degraded features into a Wasserstein barycenter space to model a degradation-agnostic content, while residual subspaces capture degradation-specific details; this disentanglement improves generalization to unseen degradations.
AI.motivation: To overcome poor generalization of all-in-one image restoration models under out-of-distribution degradations by learning a degradation-agnostic representation and separate degradation-specific components.
AI.method: Propose BaryIR, which minimizes the Wasserstein distance from multisource degraded features to a shared WB space and introduces residual subspaces orthogonal to the WB embeddings; these subspaces are mutually contrasted while WB embeddings capture degradation-agnostic content.
AI.result: Empirical results show BaryIR is competitive with state-of-the-art all-in-one methods and generalizes well to unseen degradations (types and levels); robust even when trained on limited degradation types and evaluated on real-world mixed degradations.
AI.conclusion: Disentangling degradation-agnostic content (WB space) from degradation-specific knowledge (residual subspaces) mitigates overfitting to in-distribution degradations and enables adaptive restoration with strong generalization to unseen degradations.

title: Scaling Laws of Global Weather Models
summary: Data-driven models are revolutionizing weather forecasting. To optimize training efficiency and model performance, this paper analyzes empirical scaling laws within this domain. We investigate the relationship between model performance (validation loss) and three key factors: model size ($N$), dataset size ($D$), and compute budget ($C$). Across a range of models, we find that Aurora exhibits the strongest data-scaling behavior: increasing the training dataset by 10x reduces validation loss by up to 3.2x. GraphCast demonstrates the highest parameter efficiency, yet suffers from limited hardware utilization. Our compute-optimal analysis indicates that, under fixed compute budgets, allocating resources to longer training durations yields greater performance gains than increasing model size. Furthermore, we analyze model shape and uncover scaling behaviors that differ fundamentally from those observed in language models: weather forecasting models consistently favor increased width over depth. These findings suggest that future weather models should prioritize wider architectures and larger effective training datasets to maximize predictive performance.

AI.tldr: Empirical scaling laws for weather-forecasting models show data scaling (Aurora) yields notable loss reductions, parameter efficiency varies (GraphCast) with hardware limits, and compute should favor longer training over bigger models. Weather models scale width over depth, differing from language models; future work should prioritize wider architectures and larger effective data.
AI.motivation: To understand how validation loss scales with model size N, dataset size D, and compute budget C in data-driven weather forecasting, to guide efficient model design and training.
AI.method: Empirical analysis across multiple weather models (notably Aurora and GraphCast) examining how validation loss scales with N (parameters), D (dataset size), and C (compute). The study includes a compute-optimal analysis under fixed compute budgets and an investigation of model shape (width vs depth).
AI.result: Aurora shows the strongest data-scaling: increasing D by 10x reduces validation loss by up to 3.2x. GraphCast offers high parameter efficiency but suffers from limited hardware utilization. Under fixed compute budgets, allocating resources to longer training durations yields greater performance gains than increasing model size. Weather models favor increased width over depth, with scaling behaviors distinct from language models.
AI.conclusion: Future weather models should prioritize wider architectures and larger effective training datasets to maximize predictive performance, with attention to efficient hardware utilization and longer training times under compute constraints.

title: Phys-3D: Physics-Constrained Real-Time Crowd Tracking and Counting on Railway Platforms
summary: Accurate, real-time crowd counting on railway platforms is essential for safety and capacity management. We propose to use a single camera mounted in a train, scanning the platform while arriving. While hardware constraints are simple, counting remains challenging due to dense occlusions, camera motion, and perspective distortions during train arrivals. Most existing tracking-by-detection approaches assume static cameras or ignore physical consistency in motion modeling, leading to unreliable counting under dynamic conditions. We propose a physics-constrained tracking framework that unifies detection, appearance, and 3D motion reasoning in a real-time pipeline. Our approach integrates a transfer-learned YOLOv11m detector with EfficientNet-B0 appearance encoding within DeepSORT, while introducing a physics-constrained Kalman model (Phys-3D) that enforces physically plausible 3D motion dynamics through pinhole geometry. To address counting brittleness under occlusions, we implement a virtual counting band with persistence. On our platform benchmark, MOT-RailwayPlatformCrowdHead Dataset(MOT-RPCH), our method reduces counting error to 2.97%, demonstrating robust performance despite motion and occlusions. Our results show that incorporating first-principles geometry and motion priors enables reliable crowd counting in safety-critical transportation scenarios, facilitating effective train scheduling and platform safety management.

AI.tldr: A motion-aware, physics-constrained tracking framework for real-time crowd counting from a moving train camera, integrating YOLOv11m and EfficientNet-B0 in DeepSORT, with a Phys-3D Kalman filter and occlusion-robust counting band, achieving 2.97% error on MOT-RPCH.
AI.motivation: Real-time, accurate crowd counting on moving platforms is critical for safety and efficiency, but challenging due to occlusions, camera motion, and perspective distortion; existing methods assume static cameras or lack physical motion constraints.
AI.method: Single-camera platform; transfer-learned YOLOv11m detector; EfficientNet-B0 appearance encoding; DeepSORT data association; physics-constrained 3D Kalman filter (Phys-3D) enforcing pinhole-geometry motion priors; virtual counting band with persistence to mitigate occlusions; real-time processing.
AI.result: On MOT-RPCH dataset, counting error reduced to 2.97%, robust to motion and occlusions; demonstration of improved reliability for safety-critical transport scenarios.
AI.conclusion: Embedding first-principles geometry and motion priors into tracking-based counting improves reliability for rail platform safety and scheduling applications; approach generalizable to moving-camera counting with 3D motion constraints.

title: Exploratory Memory-Augmented LLM Agent via Hybrid On- and Off-Policy Optimization
summary: Exploration remains the key bottleneck for large language model agents trained with reinforcement learning. While prior methods exploit pretrained knowledge, they fail in environments requiring the discovery of novel states. We propose Exploratory Memory-Augmented On- and Off-Policy Optimization (EMPO$^2$), a hybrid RL framework that leverages memory for exploration and combines on- and off-policy updates to make LLMs perform well with memory while also ensuring robustness without it. On ScienceWorld and WebShop, EMPO$^2$ achieves 128.6% and 11.3% improvements over GRPO, respectively. Moreover, in out-of-distribution tests, EMPO$^2$ demonstrates superior adaptability to new tasks, requiring only a few trials with memory and no parameter updates. These results highlight EMPO$^2$ as a promising framework for building more exploratory and generalizable LLM-based agents.

AI.tldr: A memory-augmented hybrid RL framework (EMPO^2) for LLM agents that combines on-policy and off-policy learning with exploratory memory to improve exploration, robustness, and generalization; shows large gains on ScienceWorld and WebShop and better OOD adaptation with minimal trials.
AI.motivation: Exploration bottleneck in reinforcement learning for LLM-based agents, especially in environments with unseen states; a need for memory-enabled exploration and robust policy updates.
AI.method: Hybrid RL framework that uses a memory module to drive exploration and combines on-policy and off-policy updates to train LLMs; leverages memory for exploration while ensuring robustness when memory is absent.
AI.result: Empirical improvements of 128.6% on ScienceWorld and 11.3% on WebShop over GRPO baselines; superior out-of-distribution adaptability, requiring only a few trials with memory and no parameter updates.
AI.conclusion: EMPO^2 is a promising framework for building more exploratory and generalizable LLM-based agents.

title: Uni-Animator: Towards Unified Visual Colorization
summary: We propose Uni-Animator, a novel Diffusion Transformer (DiT)-based framework for unified image and video sketch colorization. Existing sketch colorization methods struggle to unify image and video tasks, suffering from imprecise color transfer with single or multiple references, inadequate preservation of high-frequency physical details, and compromised temporal coherence with motion artifacts in large-motion scenes. To tackle imprecise color transfer, we introduce visual reference enhancement via instance patch embedding, enabling precise alignment and fusion of reference color information. To resolve insufficient physical detail preservation, we design physical detail reinforcement using physical features that effectively capture and retain high-frequency textures. To mitigate motion-induced temporal inconsistency, we propose sketch-based dynamic RoPE encoding that adaptively models motion-aware spatial-temporal dependencies. Extensive experimental results demonstrate that Uni-Animator achieves competitive performance on both image and video sketch colorization, matching that of task-specific methods while unlocking unified cross-domain capabilities with high detail fidelity and robust temporal consistency.

AI.tldr: A unified Diffusion Transformer-based framework (Uni-Animator) for colorizing sketches in images and video, addressing imprecise color transfer, detail preservation, and temporal coherence with reference-enhanced embedding, physical detail reinforcement, and dynamic RoPE for motion-aware spatio-temporal modeling.
AI.motivation: Unify image and video sketch colorization, overcoming task-specific limitations (color alignment with references, high-frequency detail preservation, temporal coherence in motion) in a single framework.
AI.method: Integrates a Diffusion Transformer backbone; introduces instance patch embedding for visual reference enhancement; utilizes physical feature-based detail reinforcement; employs sketch-based dynamic RoPE encoding for adaptive spatio-temporal modeling; end-to-end training for both image and video tasks.
AI.result: Competitive performance with task-specific methods on both image and video sketch colorization; achieves high detail fidelity and robust temporal consistency; enables cross-domain unified capabilities.
AI.conclusion: Uni-Animator delivers unified, high-fidelity colorization for images and videos with strong reference handling and motion-aware coherence, enabling cross-domain applicability.

title: Learning Disease-Sensitive Latent Interaction Graphs From Noisy Cardiac Flow Measurements
summary: Cardiac blood flow patterns contain rich information about disease severity and clinical interventions, yet current imaging and computational methods fail to capture underlying relational structures of coherent flow features. We propose a physics-informed, latent relational framework to model cardiac vortices as interacting nodes in a graph. Our model combines a neural relational inference architecture with physics-inspired interaction energy and birth-death dynamics, yielding a latent graph sensitive to disease severity and intervention level. We first apply this to computational fluid dynamics simulations of aortic coarctation. Learned latent graphs reveal that as the aortic radius narrows, vortex interactions become stronger and more frequent. This leads to a higher graph entropy, correlating monotonically with coarctation severity ($R^2=0.78$, Spearman $|ρ|=0.96$). We then extend this method to ultrasound datasets of left ventricles under varying levels of left ventricular assist device support. Again the latent graph representation captures the weakening of coherent vortical structures, thereby demonstrating cross-modal generalisation. Results show latent interaction graphs and entropy serve as robust and interpretable markers of cardiac disease and intervention.

AI.tldr: A physics-informed latent relational graph framework for cardiac vortices that jointly models interactions and dynamic birth-death processes; demonstrates strong correlation with disease severity and cross-modal generalization across CFD and ultrasound.
AI.motivation: Current imaging and computation fail to capture relational structures of coherent cardiac flow features; need a relational, physics-informed representation to quantify disease severity and intervention effects.
AI.method: A neural relational inference (NRI) framework augmented with physics-inspired interaction energy and birth-death dynamics, modeling vortices as nodes in a latent graph; cross-modal validation on CFD simulations of aortic coarctation and ultrasound data of LV with LVAD support.
AI.result: In CFD, latent graphs show stronger/frequent vortex interactions as aortic radius narrows; graph entropy correlates with coarctation severity (R^2=0.78, |ρ|=0.96). In ultrasound LV+LVAD data, latent graphs capture weakening of coherent vortices; cross-modal generalization observed.
AI.conclusion: Latent interaction graphs and entropy can serve as robust, interpretable markers of cardiac disease and intervention, with cross-modal applicability.

title: FairQuant: Fairness-Aware Mixed-Precision Quantization for Medical Image Classification
summary: Compressing neural networks by quantizing model parameters offers useful trade-off between performance and efficiency. Methods like quantization-aware training and post-training quantization strive to maintain the downstream performance of compressed models compared to the full precision models. However, these techniques do not explicitly consider the impact on algorithmic fairness. In this work, we study fairness-aware mixed-precision quantization schemes for medical image classification under explicit bit budgets. We introduce FairQuant, a framework that combines group-aware importance analysis, budgeted mixed-precision allocation, and a learnable Bit-Aware Quantization (BAQ) mode that jointly optimizes weights and per-unit bit allocations under bitrate and fairness regularization. We evaluate the method on Fitzpatrick17k and ISIC2019 across ResNet18/50, DeiT-Tiny, and TinyViT. Results show that FairQuant configurations with average precision near 4-6 bits recover much of the Uniform 8-bit accuracy while improving worst-group performance relative to Uniform 4- and 8-bit baselines, with comparable fairness metrics under shared budgets.

AI.tldr: FairQuant: a fairness-aware mixed-precision quantization framework for medical image classification under explicit bit budgets; achieves near 4-6 bit average precision and improved worst-group fairness compared with uniform baselines.
AI.motivation: Quantization improves efficiency but often ignores fairness across demographic or clinical groups. In medical imaging, uneven performance across groups can be critical. The work aims to integrate fairness into mixed-precision quantization under a fixed bitrate constraint.
AI.method: Proposes FairQuant, combining group-aware importance analysis, budgeted mixed-precision allocation, and a learnable Bit-Aware Quantization (BAQ) mode that jointly optimizes weights and per-unit bit allocations under bitrate and fairness regularization.
AI.result: Evaluated on Fitzpatrick17k and ISIC2019 using ResNet18/50, DeiT-Tiny, and TinyViT. Configurations with average precision around 4-6 bits recover much of Uniform 8-bit accuracy and improve worst-group performance versus Uniform 4- and 8-bit baselines, with comparable fairness metrics under shared budgets.
AI.conclusion: FairQuant demonstrates that fairness-aware mixed-precision quantization can maintain or recover competitive accuracy at low bitrates while enhancing worst-group fairness under fixed budgets, indicating practical viability for fairness-constrained medical imaging deployment.

title: ColoDiff: Integrating Dynamic Consistency With Content Awareness for Colonoscopy Video Generation
summary: Colonoscopy video generation delivers dynamic, information-rich data critical for diagnosing intestinal diseases, particularly in data-scarce scenarios. High-quality video generation demands temporal consistency and precise control over clinical attributes, but faces challenges from irregular intestinal structures, diverse disease representations, and various imaging modalities. To this end, we propose ColoDiff, a diffusion-based framework that generates dynamic-consistent and content-aware colonoscopy videos, aiming to alleviate data shortage and assist clinical analysis. At the inter-frame level, our TimeStream module decouples temporal dependency from video sequences through a cross-frame tokenization mechanism, enabling intricate dynamic modeling despite irregular intestinal structures. At the intra-frame level, our Content-Aware module incorporates noise-injected embeddings and learnable prototypes to realize precise control over clinical attributes, breaking through the coarse guidance of diffusion models. Additionally, ColoDiff employs a non-Markovian sampling strategy that cuts steps by over 90% for real-time generation. ColoDiff is evaluated across three public datasets and one hospital database, based on both generation metrics and downstream tasks including disease diagnosis, modality discrimination, bowel preparation scoring, and lesion segmentation. Extensive experiments show ColoDiff generates videos with smooth transitions and rich dynamics. ColoDiff presents an effort in controllable colonoscopy video generation, revealing the potential of synthetic videos in complementing authentic representation and mitigating data scarcity in clinical settings.

AI.tldr: ColoDiff uses diffusion-based generation to create dynamic, controllable colonoscopy videos for data-scarce settings, featuring a TimeStream module for cross-frame temporal modeling and a Content-Aware module for precise attribute control, plus a fast non-Markovian sampling strategy; evaluated on multiple datasets showing smooth dynamics and effective downstream utility.
AI.motivation: Address data scarcity and the need for temporally consistent, clinically controllable colonoscopy videos across diverse anatomy, disease representations, and imaging modalities.
AI.method: ColoDiff is a diffusion-based framework. TimeStream decouples inter-frame temporal dependency via cross-frame tokenization to model dynamics despite irregular structures. Content-Aware module injects noise-perturbed embeddings and learns prototypes to enable fine-grained control over clinical attributes. A non-Markovian sampling strategy reduces sampling steps by over 90% for real-time generation.
AI.result: Evaluations on three public datasets and one hospital database using generation metrics and downstream tasks (disease diagnosis, modality discrimination, bowel preparation scoring, lesion segmentation) show ColoDiff produces smooth transitions and rich dynamics.
AI.conclusion: ColoDiff demonstrates controllable, dynamic colonoscopy video generation, highlighting synthetic videos as a complement to real data to mitigate data scarcity in clinical settings.

title: RhythmBERT: A Self-Supervised Language Model Based on Latent Representations of ECG Waveforms for Heart Disease Detection
summary: Electrocardiogram (ECG) analysis is crucial for diagnosing heart disease, but most self-supervised learning methods treat ECG as a generic time series, overlooking physiologic semantics and rhythm-level structure. Existing contrastive methods utilize augmentations that distort morphology, whereas generative approaches employ fixed-window segmentation, which misaligns cardiac cycles. To address these limitations, we propose RhythmBERT, a generative ECG language model that considers ECG as a language paradigm by encoding P, QRS, and T segments into symbolic tokens via autoencoder-based latent representations. These discrete tokens capture rhythm semantics, while complementary continuous embeddings retain fine-grained morphology, enabling a unified view of waveform structure and rhythm. RhythmBERT is pretrained on approximately 800,000 unlabeled ECG recordings with a masked prediction objective, allowing it to learn contextual representations in a label-efficient manner. Evaluations show that despite using only a single lead, RhythmBERT achieves comparable or superior performance to strong 12-lead baselines. This generalization extends from prevalent conditions such as atrial fibrillation to clinically challenging cases such as subtle ST-T abnormalities and myocardial infarction. Our results suggest that considering ECG as structured language offers a scalable and physiologically aligned pathway for advancing cardiac analysis.

AI.tldr: RhythmBERT: a generative ECG language model that tokenizes P/QRS/T segments into discrete rhythm tokens with continuous morphology, enabling label-efficient pretraining on 800k ECGs and strong single-lead performance with robust generalization.
AI.motivation: ECG analysis benefits from preserving physiologic rhythm structure and morphology; conventional self-supervised methods either distort morphology via augmentations or rely on fixed-window segmentation that misaligns cardiac cycles.
AI.method: Encode ECG into symbolic tokens for P, QRS, and T segments via autoencoder-based latent representations; complement with continuous embeddings to preserve morphology; pretrain a generative masked prediction objective on ~800k unlabeled ECG recordings to learn contextual representations.
AI.result: RhythmBERT achieves comparable or superior performance to strong 12-lead baselines using only a single lead and generalizes across atrial fibrillation, subtle ST-T abnormalities, and myocardial infarction, suggesting ECG can be effectively treated as a structured language with scalable analysis.
AI.conclusion: Treating ECG as structured language with discrete rhythm tokens and continuous morphology embeddings offers a scalable, physiologically aligned path for data-efficient cardiac analysis.

title: Physics-informed neural particle flow for the Bayesian update step
summary: The Bayesian update step poses significant computational challenges in high-dimensional nonlinear estimation. While log-homotopy particle flow filters offer an alternative to stochastic sampling, existing formulations usually yield stiff differential equations. Conversely, existing deep learning approximations typically treat the update as a black-box task or rely on asymptotic relaxation, neglecting the exact geometric structure of the finite-horizon probability transport. In this work, we propose a physics-informed neural particle flow, which is an amortized inference framework. To construct the flow, we couple the log-homotopy trajectory of the prior to posterior density function with the continuity equation describing the density evolution. This derivation yields a governing partial differential equation (PDE), referred to as the master PDE. By embedding this PDE as a physical constraint into the loss function, we train a neural network to approximate the transport velocity field. This approach enables purely unsupervised training, eliminating the need for ground-truth posterior samples. We demonstrate that the neural parameterization acts as an implicit regularizer, mitigating the numerical stiffness inherent to analytic flows and reducing online computational complexity. Experimental validation on multimodal benchmarks and a challenging nonlinear scenario confirms better mode coverage and robustness compared to state-of-the-art baselines.

AI.tldr: A physics-informed, amortized neural particle flow for Bayesian updates that unsupervisedly learns transport velocity via a master PDE constraint, reducing stiffness and online cost while improving mode coverage on multimodal nonlinear problems.
AI.motivation: High-dimensional nonlinear Bayesian updates are computationally challenging. Existing log-homotopy flows are stiff; deep learning approaches either treat updates as black boxes or neglect finite-horizon transport structure, limiting accuracy and efficiency.
AI.method: Derive a master PDE by coupling the log-homotopy prior-to-posterior density evolution with the continuity equation, and embed this PDE as a physical constraint in the loss for training a neural network that approximates the transport velocity field; enables purely unsupervised (no ground-truth posterior) amortized inference.
AI.result: Experimental validation on multimodal benchmarks and a challenging nonlinear scenario shows improved mode coverage and robustness compared with state-of-the-art baselines, along with reduced online computational burden and mitigated numerical stiffness.
AI.conclusion: Physics-informed neural particle flow provides an unsupervised, regularized, computationally efficient framework for high-dimensional Bayesian updates by leveraging the finite-horizon transport geometry through a master PDE constraint.

title: EmbodMocap: In-the-Wild 4D Human-Scene Reconstruction for Embodied Agents
summary: Human behaviors in the real world naturally encode rich, long-term contextual information that can be leveraged to train embodied agents for perception, understanding, and acting. However, existing capture systems typically rely on costly studio setups and wearable devices, limiting the large-scale collection of scene-conditioned human motion data in the wild. To address this, we propose EmbodMocap, a portable and affordable data collection pipeline using two moving iPhones. Our key idea is to jointly calibrate dual RGB-D sequences to reconstruct both humans and scenes within a unified metric world coordinate frame. The proposed method allows metric-scale and scene-consistent capture in everyday environments without static cameras or markers, bridging human motion and scene geometry seamlessly. Compared with optical capture ground truth, we demonstrate that the dual-view setting exhibits a remarkable ability to mitigate depth ambiguity, achieving superior alignment and reconstruction performance over single iphone or monocular models. Based on the collected data, we empower three embodied AI tasks: monocular human-scene-reconstruction, where we fine-tune on feedforward models that output metric-scale, world-space aligned humans and scenes; physics-based character animation, where we prove our data could be used to scale human-object interaction skills and scene-aware motion tracking; and robot motion control, where we train a humanoid robot via sim-to-real RL to replicate human motions depicted in videos. Experimental results validate the effectiveness of our pipeline and its contributions towards advancing embodied AI research.

AI.tldr: A portable dual iPhone RGB-D pipeline, EmbodMocap, reconstructs metric-scale humans and scenes in natural environments, enabling downstream tasks like monocular human-scene reconstruction, physics-based animation, and robot motion control, without markers or static cameras, by joint calibration of dual views to mitigate depth ambiguity.
AI.motivation: Existing capture systems are costly and limit large-scale, in-the-wild motion data with scene context; there is a need for scalable, metric-scale, scene-consistent data for embodied AI.
AI.method: Joint calibration of dual moving iPhone RGB-D streams to reconstruct humans and scenes in a unified metric world frame; no fixed cameras or markers; leveraging dual-view to reduce depth ambiguity; evaluation against ground-truth optical capture; fine-tuning feedforward models for monocular reconstruction, proving data for physics-based animation and sim-to-real RL for robot motion.
AI.result: Dual-view setup mitigates depth ambiguity and yields better alignment and reconstruction than single-view/monocular methods; dataset enables downstream tasks and demonstrates feasibility of scaling with consumer devices; experimental results validate pipeline.
AI.conclusion: EmbodMocap offers a cost-effective, scalable solution bridging human motion and scene geometry in natural environments, advancing embodied AI research and practical applications in perception, understanding, and acting.

title: PRAC: Principal-Random Subspace for LLM Activation Compression and Memory-Efficient Training
summary: Activations have become the primary memory bottleneck in large-batch LLM training. However, existing compression methods fail to exploit the spectral structure of activations, resulting in slow convergence or limited compression. To address this, we bridge the relationship between the algorithm's fast convergence and the requirements for subspace projection, and show that an effective compression should yield an unbiased estimate of the original activation with low variance. We propose Principal-Random Subspace for LLM Activation Compression (PRAC), which novelly decomposes activations into two components: a principal subspace captured via SVD to retain dominant information, and a random subspace sampled from the orthogonal complement to approximate the tail. By introducing a precise scaling factor, we prove that PRAC yields an unbiased gradient estimator with minimum variance under certain conditions. Extensive experiments on pre-training and fine-tuning tasks demonstrate that PRAC achieves up to 36% total memory reduction with negligible performance degradation and minimal computational cost.

AI.tldr: PRAC decomposes activations into a dominant SVD-derived principal subspace and a random subspace from the orthogonal complement to compress activations in LLM training; it yields an unbiased, low-variance gradient estimator with memory savings up to 36% and minimal overhead, validated on pre-training and fine-tuning tasks.
AI.motivation: Activation memory is the primary bottleneck in large-batch LLM training. Existing compression methods fail to exploit the spectral structure of activations, leading to slow convergence or limited compression.
AI.method: Split activations into two components: a principal subspace captured via SVD to retain dominant information, and a random subspace sampled from the orthogonal complement to approximate the tail. Apply a precise scaling factor to ensure the gradient estimator is unbiased with minimum variance under certain conditions.
AI.result: Experiments show up to 36% total memory reduction with negligible performance degradation and minimal computational cost across pre-training and fine-tuning tasks.
AI.conclusion: PRAC leverages spectral structure for activation compression, achieving efficient memory savings while preserving convergence properties, with a theoretical guarantee of unbiasedness and low-variance gradient estimates under specified conditions.

title: Through BrokenEyes: How Eye Disorders Impact Face Detection?
summary: Vision disorders significantly impact millions of lives, altering how visual information is processed and perceived. In this work, a computational framework was developed using the BrokenEyes system to simulate five common eye disorders: Age-related macular degeneration, cataract, glaucoma, refractive errors, and diabetic retinopathy and analyze their effects on neural-like feature representations in deep learning models. Leveraging a combination of human and non-human datasets, models trained under normal and disorder-specific conditions revealed critical disruptions in feature maps, particularly for cataract and glaucoma, which align with known neural processing challenges in these conditions. Evaluation metrics such as activation energy and cosine similarity quantified the severity of these distortions, providing insights into the interplay between degraded visual inputs and learned representations.

AI.tldr: A BrokenEyes-based framework simulates five eye disorders to study their impact on deep-learning feature representations, revealing notable disruptions, especially for cataract and glaucoma, quantified by activation energy and cosine similarity.
AI.motivation: Vision disorders alter visual processing; understanding how degraded inputs affect learned representations improves robustness and interpretability of vision models.
AI.method: Develop a computational framework using the BrokenEyes system to simulate Age-related Macular Degeneration, cataract, glaucoma, refractive errors, and diabetic retinopathy on DL models. Use human and non-human datasets; train models under normal and disorder-specific conditions; analyze neural-like feature maps.
AI.result: Disruptions in feature maps were observed, with cataract and glaucoma showing the most pronounced distortions, aligning with known neural processing challenges; activation energy and cosine similarity quantified distortion severity.
AI.conclusion: The framework provides quantitative insight into how degraded visual inputs reshape learned representations and highlights disorder-specific vulnerabilities, informing robustness and interpretability studies.

title: Learning Physical Operators using Neural Operators
summary: Neural operators have emerged as promising surrogate models for solving partial differential equations (PDEs), but struggle to generalise beyond training distributions and are often constrained to a fixed temporal discretisation. This work introduces a physics-informed training framework that addresses these limitations by decomposing PDEs using operator splitting methods, training separate neural operators to learn individual non-linear physical operators while approximating linear operators with fixed finite-difference convolutions. This modular mixture-of-experts architecture enables generalisation to novel physical regimes by explicitly encoding the underlying operator structure. We formulate the modelling task as a neural ordinary differential equation (ODE) where these learned operators constitute the right-hand side, enabling continuous-in-time predictions through standard ODE solvers and implicitly enforcing PDE constraints. Demonstrated on incompressible and compressible Navier-Stokes equations, our approach achieves better convergence and superior performance when generalising to unseen physics. The method remains parameter-efficient, enabling temporal extrapolation beyond training horizons, and provides interpretable components whose behaviour can be verified against known physics.

AI.tldr: A physics-informed neural operator framework using operator splitting and a mixture-of-experts to learn nonlinear PDE operators, while fixing linear parts with finite-difference convolutions; cast as a neural ODE RHS for continuous-time predictions, enabling generalization to unseen physics.
AI.motivation: Overcome generalization limits of neural operators and fixed temporal discretizations by exploiting the known operator structure of PDEs and enforcing PDE constraints, enabling temporal extrapolation and interpretability.
AI.method: Decompose PDEs via operator splitting; train separate neural operators for nonlinear components; use fixed finite-difference convolutions for linear parts; employ a modular mixture-of-experts architecture; formulate the system as a neural ODE with the learned operators as the RHS; enable continuous-time propagation with standard ODE solvers and PDE-consistency.
AI.result: Improved convergence and superior generalization to unseen physics (including both incompressible and compressible Navier–Stokes); parameter-efficient, allowing temporal extrapolation beyond training horizons; interpretable components verifiable against physics.
AI.conclusion: The framework inherits generalization and interpretability by leveraging explicit operator structure through splitting, achieving accurate, continuous-time PDE surrogate models that respect physics and generalize to novel regimes.

title: Plug-and-Play Diffusion Meets ADMM: Dual-Variable Coupling for Robust Medical Image Reconstruction
summary: Plug-and-Play diffusion prior (PnPDP) frameworks have emerged as a powerful paradigm for solving imaging inverse problems by treating pretrained generative models as modular priors. However, we identify a critical flaw in prevailing PnP solvers (e.g., based on HQS or Proximal Gradient): they function as memoryless operators, updating estimates solely based on instantaneous gradients. This lack of historical tracking inevitably leads to non-vanishing steady-state bias, where the reconstruction fails to strictly satisfy physical measurements under heavy corruption. To resolve this, we propose Dual-Coupled PnP Diffusion, which restores the classical dual variable to provide integral feedback, theoretically guaranteeing asymptotic convergence to the exact data manifold. However, this rigorous geometric coupling introduces a secondary challenge: the accumulated dual residuals exhibit spectrally colored, structured artifacts that violate the Additive White Gaussian Noise (AWGN) assumption of diffusion priors, causing severe hallucinations. To bridge this gap, we introduce Spectral Homogenization (SH), a frequency-domain adaptation mechanism that modulates these structured residuals into statistically compliant pseudo-AWGN inputs. This effectively aligns the solver's rigorous optimization trajectory with the denoiser's valid statistical manifold. Extensive experiments on CT and MRI reconstruction demonstrate that our approach resolves the bias-hallucination trade-off, achieving state-of-the-art fidelity with significantly accelerated convergence.

AI.tldr: A dual-coupled PnP diffusion framework with spectral homogenization to address bias and hallucinations in imaging reconstruction, achieving asymptotic convergence to the data manifold and pseudo-AWGN consistency, with state-of-the-art CT/MRI results and faster convergence.
AI.motivation: Prevailing memoryless PnP solvers incur non-vanishing steady-state bias under heavy corruption due to lack of history. Need integral feedback to satisfy measurements.
AI.method: Introduce dual variable to couple with diffusion prior (Dual-Coupled PnP Diffusion); add Spectral Homogenization to convert structured residuals into pseudo-AWGN; apply to CT/MRI reconstruction.
AI.result: Demonstrates bias-hallucination trade-off resolution, state-of-the-art fidelity, accelerated convergence on CT and MRI datasets.
AI.conclusion: Combines rigorous optimization with valid statistical manifold of denoisers; SH aligns residual statistics with model assumptions; promising but requires further validation on general tasks and analysis of computational complexity.

title: Regularized Online RLHF with Generalized Bilinear Preferences
comment: 43 pages, 1 table (ver2: more colorful boxes, fixed some typos)
summary: We consider the problem of contextual online RLHF with general preferences, where the goal is to identify the Nash Equilibrium. We adopt the Generalized Bilinear Preference Model (GBPM) to capture potentially intransitive preferences via low-rank, skew-symmetric matrices. We investigate general preference learning with any strongly convex regularizer and regularization strength $η^{-1}$, generalizing beyond prior work limited to reverse KL-regularization. Central to our analysis is proving that the dual gap of the greedy policy is bounded by the square of the estimation error, a result derived solely from strong convexity and the skew-symmetry of GBPM. Building on this insight and a feature diversity assumption, we establish two regret bounds via two simple algorithms: (1) Greedy Sampling achieves polylogarithmic, $e^{\mathcal{O}(η)}$-free regret $\tilde{\mathcal{O}}(ηd^4 (\log T)^2)$. (2) Explore-Then-Commit achieves $\mathrm{poly}(d)$-free regret $\tilde{\mathcal{O}}(\sqrt{ηr T})$ by exploiting the low-rank structure; this is the first statistically efficient guarantee for online RLHF in high-dimensions.

AI.tldr: Contextual online RLHF with general preferences via GBPM, enabling low-rank, skew-symmetric modeling of potentially intransitive preferences; two algorithms achieve significant regret guarantees in high dimensions.
AI.motivation: Extend online RLHF to general preference settings with strong convex regularization and skew-symmetric GBPM, enabling analysis beyond reverse KL and handling intransitive preferences to identify Nash equilibria.
AI.method: Adopt Generalized Bilinear Preference Model (GBPM) with low-rank, skew-symmetric matrices; leverage strong convexity of any regularizer with strength η^{-1}; prove dual gap of the greedy policy is bounded by the square of estimation error; assume feature diversity; develop two algorithms: Greedy Sampling and Explore-Then-Commit that exploit low-rank structure.
AI.result: Regret bounds: (1) Greedy Sampling achieves polylogarithmic, e^{O(η)}-free regret ~ Õ(η d^4 (log T)^2); (2) Explore-Then-Commit achieves poly(d)-free regret ~ Õ(√(η r T)) leveraging low-rank structure; claimed to be the first statistically efficient guarantee for online RLHF in high dimensions.
AI.conclusion: Provides a general, high-dimensional framework for contextual online RLHF with general preferences, yielding two practical algorithms with strong regret guarantees under mild assumptions (strong convexity, skew-symmetry, feature diversity).

title: Multidimensional Task Learning: A Unified Tensor Framework for Computer Vision Tasks
summary: This paper introduces Multidimensional Task Learning (MTL), a unified mathematical framework based on Generalized Einstein MLPs (GE-MLPs) that operate directly on tensors via the Einstein product. We argue that current computer vision task formulations are inherently constrained by matrix-based thinking: standard architectures rely on matrix-valued weights and vectorvalued biases, requiring structural flattening that restricts the space of naturally expressible tasks. GE-MLPs lift this constraint by operating with tensor-valued parameters, enabling explicit control over which dimensions are preserved or contracted without information loss. Through rigorous mathematical derivations, we demonstrate that classification, segmentation, and detection are special cases of MTL, differing only in their dimensional configuration within a formally defined task space. We further prove that this task space is strictly larger than what matrix-based formulations can natively express, enabling principled task configurations such as spatiotemporal or cross modal predictions that require destructive flattening under conventional approaches. This work provides a mathematical foundation for understanding, comparing, and designing computer vision tasks through the lens of tensor algebra.

AI.tldr: MTL with GE-MLPs operates on tensors via the Einstein product, expanding task space beyond matrix-based architectures; classifies CV tasks (classification, segmentation, detection) as special cases; enables spatiotemporal and cross-modal configurations without flattening.
AI.motivation: Current CV architectures rely on matrix-valued weights and flattening, which restricts expressivity and loses multi-dimensional structure; a tensor-based framework is proposed to preserve and leverage this structure.
AI.method: Introduce Generalized Einstein MLPs (GE-MLPs) that use tensor-valued parameters and the Einstein product. Formalize a multidimensional task space in which CV tasks map to specific dimensional configurations. Prove that this space strictly contains what matrix-based formulations can express; demonstrate CV tasks as special cases of MTL (classification, segmentation, detection) and show capabilities for spatiotemporal/cross-modal predictions.
AI.result: Rigorous mathematical derivations showing that classification, segmentation, and detection are special cases of MTL under appropriate dimensional configurations. Proven that the tensor-based task space is strictly larger than the matrix-based space, enabling previously inaccessible task configurations without destructive flattening.
AI.conclusion: MTL provides a tensor-algebra–based foundation for understanding, comparing, and designing CV tasks, challenging matrix-centric approaches and enabling flexible task configurations such as spatiotemporal and cross-modal predictions.

title: Bound to Disagree: Generalization Bounds via Certifiable Surrogates
summary: Generalization bounds for deep learning models are typically vacuous, not computable or restricted to specific model classes. In this paper, we tackle these issues by providing new disagreement-based certificates for the gap between the true risk of any two predictors. We then bound the true risk of the predictor of interest via a surrogate model that enjoys tight generalization guarantees, and evaluating our disagreement bound on an unlabeled dataset. We empirically demonstrate the tightness of the obtained certificates and showcase the versatility of the approach by training surrogate models leveraging three different frameworks: sample compression, model compression and PAC-Bayes theory. Importantly, such guarantees are achieved without modifying the target model, nor adapting the training procedure to the generalization framework.

AI.tldr: Disagreement-based certificates bound the gap between true risks of predictors; use a surrogate model with tight generalization guarantees to bound the target predictor's risk, evaluated on unlabeled data; demonstrated across sample compression, model compression, and PAC-Bayes, without altering the target model.
AI.motivation: Classic generalization bounds for deep learning are often vacuous or inapplicable. There is a need for computable, model-agnostic risk certificates that can be evaluated without changing the target training procedure.
AI.method: Introduce disagreement-based certificates for the risk gap between two predictors. Bound the true risk of the target predictor by leveraging a surrogate model that comes with strong generalization guarantees; compute the disagreement bound on an unlabeled dataset; instantiate surrogate models using (i) sample compression, (ii) model compression, and (iii) PAC-Bayes frameworks; no modification to the target model or training procedure is required.
AI.result: Empirically shows that the certificates are tight; surrogate models provide tight generalization guarantees that translate into non-vacuous risk bounds for the target predictor; demonstrates versatility across three surrogate frameworks.
AI.conclusion: The approach yields nonintrusive, generalization certificates for deep models that can be evaluated with unlabeled data. It provides a practical, framework-agnostic route to certify predictive risk gaps without retraining or altering the target model, though computational considerations and the tightness of bounds may depend on the surrogate chosen.

title: DyGnROLE: Modeling Asymmetry in Dynamic Graphs with Node-Role-Oriented Latent Encoding
summary: Real-world dynamic graphs are often directed, with source and destination nodes exhibiting asymmetrical behavioral patterns and temporal dynamics. However, existing dynamic graph architectures largely rely on shared parameters for processing source and destination nodes, with limited or no systematic role-aware modeling. We propose DyGnROLE (Dynamic Graph Node-Role-Oriented Latent Encoding), a transformer-based architecture that explicitly disentangles source and destination representations. By using separate embedding vocabularies and role-semantic positional encodings, the model captures the distinct structural and temporal contexts unique to each role. Critical to the effectiveness of these specialized embeddings in low-label regimes is a self-supervised pretraining objective we introduce: Temporal Contrastive Link Prediction (TCLP). The pretraining uses the full unlabeled interaction history to encode informative structural biases, enabling the model to learn role-specific representations without requiring annotated data. Evaluation on future edge classification demonstrates that DyGnROLE substantially outperforms a diverse set of state-of-the-art baselines, establishing role-aware modeling as an effective strategy for dynamic graph learning.

AI.tldr: DyGnROLE introduces role-aware dynamic graph learning for directed graphs, using separate source/destination embeddings and role-aware positional encodings, pretrained with Temporal Contrastive Link Prediction, yielding strong gains on future edge classification.
AI.motivation: In directed dynamic graphs, source and destination nodes exhibit asymmetric patterns and temporal dynamics. Shared-parameter architecturesblur these roles, hindering role-specific representation learning, especially with limited labels.
AI.method: A transformer-based architecture that disentangles source and destination representations via separate embedding vocabularies and role-semantic positional encodings. A self-supervised pretraining objective—Temporal Contrastive Link Prediction (TCLP)—uses full interaction histories to encode structural biases and learn role-specific representations without labels.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on future edge classification shows DyGnROLE substantially outperforms diverse state-of-the-art baselines, demonstrating the effectiveness of explicit role-aware modeling in dynamic graphs.
AI.conclusion: Explicit role-aware modeling with separate role-specific embeddings and TCLP pretraining is advantageous for dynamic, directed graphs, enabling better generalization in low-label scenarios.

title: MovieTeller: Tool-augmented Movie Synopsis with ID Consistent Progressive Abstraction
summary: With the explosive growth of digital entertainment, automated video summarization has become indispensable for applications such as content indexing, personalized recommendation, and efficient media archiving. Automatic synopsis generation for long-form videos, such as movies and TV series, presents a significant challenge for existing Vision-Language Models (VLMs). While proficient at single-image captioning, these general-purpose models often exhibit critical failures in long-duration contexts, primarily a lack of ID-consistent character identification and a fractured narrative coherence. To overcome these limitations, we propose MovieTeller, a novel framework for generating movie synopses via tool-augmented progressive abstraction. Our core contribution is a training-free, tool-augmented, fact-grounded generation process. Instead of requiring costly model fine-tuning, our framework directly leverages off-the-shelf models in a plug-and-play manner. We first invoke a specialized face recognition model as an external "tool" to establish Factual Groundings--precise character identities and their corresponding bounding boxes. These groundings are then injected into the prompt to steer the VLM's reasoning, ensuring the generated scene descriptions are anchored to verifiable facts. Furthermore, our progressive abstraction pipeline decomposes the summarization of a full-length movie into a multi-stage process, effectively mitigating the context length limitations of current VLMs. Experiments demonstrate that our approach yields significant improvements in factual accuracy, character consistency, and overall narrative coherence compared to end-to-end baselines.

AI.tldr: MovieTeller introduces a training-free, tool-augmented pipeline that uses an external face-recognition 'tool' to ground identities and a progressive abstraction strategy to generate fact-grounded, coherent movie synopses with improved character consistency over end-to-end baselines.
AI.motivation: Long-form video summarization with vision-language models suffers from ID inconsistency and poor narrative coherence due to context length limits and lack of factual grounding; need scalable, plug-and-play approaches.
AI.method: Leverage off-the-shelf face recognition to obtain character identities and bounding boxes; inject grounding into prompts for VLMs; apply multi-stage progressive abstraction to decompose full-length movie summarization; training-free and tool-augmented (no fine-tuning).
AI.result: Empirical results show significant improvements in factual accuracy, character consistency, and overall narrative coherence compared to end-to-end baselines.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates effectiveness of tool-augmented, training-free approach for long-form video summarization and paves the way for integrating external verifiable grounding with LMs to handle complex narratives.

title: Prediction of Diffusion Coefficients in Mixtures with Tensor Completion
summary: Predicting diffusion coefficients in mixtures is crucial for many applications, as experimental data remain scarce, and machine learning (ML) offers promising alternatives to established semi-empirical models. Among ML models, matrix completion methods (MCMs) have proven effective in predicting thermophysical properties, including diffusion coefficients in binary mixtures. However, MCMs are restricted to single-temperature predictions, and their accuracy depends strongly on the availability of high-quality experimental data for each temperature of interest. In this work, we address this challenge by presenting a hybrid tensor completion method (TCM) for predicting temperature-dependent diffusion coefficients at infinite dilution in binary mixtures. The TCM employs a Tucker decomposition and is jointly trained on experimental data for diffusion coefficients at infinite dilution in binary systems at 298 K, 313 K, and 333 K. Predictions from the semi-empirical SEGWE model serve as prior knowledge within a Bayesian training framework. The TCM then extrapolates linearly to any temperature between 268 K and 378 K, achieving markedly improved prediction accuracy compared to established models across all studied temperatures. To further enhance predictive performance, the experimental database was expanded using active learning (AL) strategies for targeted acquisition of new diffusion data by pulsed-field gradient (PFG) NMR measurements. Diffusion coefficients at infinite dilution in 19 solute + solvent systems were measured at 298 K, 313 K, and 333 K. Incorporating these results yields a substantial improvement in the TCM's predictive accuracy. These findings highlight the potential of combining data-efficient ML methods with adaptive experimentation to advance predictive modeling of transport properties.

AI.tldr: A hybrid tensor completion framework (TCM) using Tucker decomposition combines multi-temperature diffusion data with Bayesian priors from SEGWE and active learning data to predict temperature-dependent, infinite-dilution diffusion coefficients in binary mixtures, achieving superior accuracy and enabling extrapolation across 268–378 K.
AI.motivation: Diffusion coefficients are scarce experimentally, and single-temperature matrix completion methods miss temperature dependence. Accurate temperature-dependent predictions are valuable for mixture transport properties and process design.
AI.method: Train a Tucker-based MCM (TCM) on D∞ for binary systems at 298, 313, 333 K; incorporate SEGWE predictions as priors within a Bayesian framework; perform linear temperature extrapolation to 268–378 K; expand the dataset via active learning using PFG-NMR to measure D∞ in 19 solute–solvent pairs.
AI.result: TCM yields markedly improved predictions across studied temperatures vs established models; incorporating AL data substantially improves accuracy; demonstrates data-efficient ML with adaptive experimentation for transport-property prediction.
AI.conclusion: Hybrid ML with priors and AL can extend reliable diffusion predictions across temperatures, offering a practical route for transport-property modeling in mixtures.

title: Large Multimodal Models as General In-Context Classifiers
comment: CVPR Findings 2026. Project website at https://circle-lmm.github.io/
summary: Which multimodal model should we use for classification? Previous studies suggest that the answer lies in CLIP-like contrastive Vision-Language Models (VLMs), due to their remarkable performance in zero-shot classification. In contrast, Large Multimodal Models (LMM) are more suitable for complex tasks. In this work, we argue that this answer overlooks an important capability of LMMs: in-context learning. We benchmark state-of-the-art LMMs on diverse datasets for closed-world classification and find that, although their zero-shot performance is lower than CLIP's, LMMs with a few in-context examples can match or even surpass contrastive VLMs with cache-based adapters, their "in-context" equivalent. We extend this analysis to the open-world setting, where the generative nature of LMMs makes them more suitable for the task. In this challenging scenario, LMMs struggle whenever provided with imperfect context information. To address this issue, we propose CIRCLE, a simple training-free method that assigns pseudo-labels to in-context examples, iteratively refining them with the available context itself. Through extensive experiments, we show that CIRCLE establishes a robust baseline for open-world classification, surpassing VLM counterparts and highlighting the potential of LMMs to serve as unified classifiers, and a flexible alternative to specialized models.

AI.tldr: LMMs with in-context learning can match or exceed CLIP-like VLMs in closed-world classification, and with a training-free method (CIRCLE) can robustly handle open-world classification, offering a unified, flexible alternative to specialized models.
AI.motivation: To reassess the performance trade-offs between CLIP-like VLMs and Large Multimodal Models for classification, focusing on in-context learning and open-world robustness.
AI.method: Benchmark state-of-the-art LMMs on diverse closed-world datasets to assess zero-shot and few-shot in-context performance; compare with contrastive VLMs (CLIP-like) and cache-based adapters; extend to open-world tasks using LMMs’ generative capabilities; introduce CIRCLE, a training-free pseudo-labeling scheme that iteratively refines in-context labels.
AI.result: In closed-world settings, LMMs with a few in-context examples can match or surpass CLIP with adapters, despite lower zero-shot accuracy; in open-world settings, LMMs outperform CLIP-like models when provided with reliable context, but struggle with imperfect context; CIRCLE provides a robust, training-free baseline for open-world classification, outperforming VLMs.
AI.conclusion: LMMs are viable unified classifiers that can excel with in-context learning and robust pseudo-labeling strategies in open-world scenarios, offering a flexible alternative to specialized VLMs and adapters.

title: Partial recovery of meter-scale surface weather
summary: Near-surface atmospheric conditions can differ sharply over tens to hundreds of meters due to land cover and topography, yet this variability is absent from current weather analyses and forecasts. It is unclear whether such meter-scale variability reflects irreducibly chaotic dynamics or contains a component predictable from surface characteristics and large-scale atmospheric forcing. Here we show that a substantial, physically coherent component of meter-scale near-surface weather is statistically recoverable from existing observations. By conditioning coarse atmospheric state on sparse surface station measurements and high-resolution Earth observation data, we infer spatially continuous fields of near-surface wind, temperature, and humidity at 10 m resolution across the contiguous United States. Relative to ERA5, the inferred fields reduce wind error by 29% and temperature and dewpoint error by 6%, while explaining substantially more spatial variance at fixed time steps. They also exhibit physically interpretable structure, including urban heat islands, evapotranspiration-driven humidity contrasts, and wind speed differences across land cover types.
