Unlike existing semi-supervised methods, SOTAlign effectively leverages unpaired images and text, learning robust joint embeddings across datasets and encoder pairs, and significantly outperforming supervised and semi-supervised baselines.

AI.tldr: Semi-supervised alignment of frozen unimodal encoders using few image-text pairs and abundant unpaired data via SOTAlign; a two-stage method with a linear teacher for coarse geometry and optimal-transport divergence for fine-relational alignment; outperforms baselines.
AI.motivation: Reduce supervision for cross-modal alignment while leveraging unpaired data to learn robust joint embeddings across modalities and encoder pairs.
AI.method: Two-stage: Stage 1—recover coarse shared geometry with a linear teacher from limited paired data; Stage 2—refine alignment on unpaired samples using an optimal-transport-based divergence to transfer relational structure without constraining the target space.
AI.result: Significantly outperforms supervised and semi-supervised baselines, enabling robust joint embeddings across datasets and encoder pairs.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates that meaningful cross-modal alignment can be achieved with limited paired data by exploiting unpaired data via OT-based relational transfer, supporting the Platonic Representation Hypothesis in practice.

title: A Dataset is Worth 1 MB
summary: A dataset server must often distribute the same large payload to many clients, incurring massive communication costs. Since clients frequently operate on diverse hardware and software frameworks, transmitting a pre-trained model is often infeasible; instead, agents require raw data to train their own task-specific models locally. While dataset distillation attempts to compress training signals, current methods struggle to scale to high-resolution data and rarely achieve sufficiently small files. In this paper, we propose Pseudo-Labels as Data (PLADA), a method that completely eliminates pixel transmission. We assume agents are preloaded with a large, generic, unlabeled reference dataset (e.g., ImageNet-1K, ImageNet-21K) and communicate a new task by transmitting only the class labels for specific images. To address the distribution mismatch between the reference and target datasets, we introduce a pruning mechanism that filters the reference dataset to retain only the labels of the most semantically relevant images for the target task. This selection process simultaneously maximizes training efficiency and minimizes transmission payload. Experiments on 10 diverse datasets demonstrate that our approach can transfer task knowledge with a payload of less than 1 MB while retaining high classification accuracy, offering a promising solution for efficient dataset serving.

AI.tldr: PLADA transmits only class labels derived from a large unlabeled reference set to enable local model training, eliminating pixel transfer; a pruning step selects semantically relevant images to address domain shift, achieving sub-1 MB payloads with strong accuracy across 10 datasets.
AI.motivation: Distributing large, high-dimensional datasets to diverse clients incurs huge communication costs; existing dataset distillation struggles with high-resolution data and small payloads.
AI.method: Assume agents have a large unlabeled reference dataset. Server sends only pseudo-labels (class labels) for a carefully pruned subset of reference images; pruning selects images whose labels best align with the target task, mitigating distribution mismatch.
AI.result: Experiments on 10 diverse datasets show task knowledge transfer with payload <1 MB while maintaining high accuracy.
AI.conclusion: PLADA offers a highly communication-efficient dataset serving mechanism by replacing pixel data with semantically informative labels, suitable for heterogeneous client environments.

title: Model Agreement via Anchoring
summary:
Numerous lines of aim to control $\textit{model disagreement}$ -- the extent to which two machine learning models disagree in their predictions. We adopt a simple and standard notion of model disagreement in real-valued prediction problems, namely the expected squared difference in predictions between two models trained on independent samples, without any coordination of the training processes. We would like to be able to drive disagreement to zero with some natural parameter(s) of the training procedure using analyses that can be applied to existing training methodologies.
  We develop a simple general technique for proving bounds on independent model disagreement based on $\textit{anchoring}$ to the average of two models within the analysis. We then apply this technique to prove disagreement bounds for four commonly used machine learning algorithms: (1) stacked aggregation over an arbitrary model class (where disagreement is driven to 0 with the number of models $k$ being stacked) (2) gradient boosting (where disagreement is driven to 0 with the number of iterations $k$) (3) neural network training with architecture search (where disagreement is driven to 0 with the size $n$ of the architecture being optimized over) and (4) regression tree training over all regression trees of fixed depth (where disagreement is driven to 0 with the depth $d$ of the tree architecture). For clarity, we work out our initial bounds in the setting of one-dimensional regression with squared error loss -- but then show that all of our results generalize to multi-dimensional regression with any strongly convex loss.

AI.tldr: An anchoring-based framework bounds independent model disagreement and shows zero disagreement in key learners as model capacity grows; the bounds are first derived for 1D regression with squared loss and then extended to multi-dimensional, strongly convex losses.
AI.motivation: Quantify and control how far two independently trained models may disagree, enabling insights for ensembles, stability, and generalization across standard learning algorithms.
AI.method: Introduce a general anchoring technique that bounds disagreement by relating each model to the average of the two. Apply this to four learners: (1) stacked ensembles, (2) gradient boosting, (3) neural architecture search, and (4) regression trees of fixed depth. Begin with 1D regression with squared loss; extend to multi-dimensional regression with any strongly convex loss.
AI.result: Derived explicit bounds on E[(f1(x)-f2(x))^2] showing disagreement can be driven to zero as the relevant complexity parameter grows: number of models k in stacking, iterations k in boosting, architecture size n in NAS, and depth d in regression trees.
AI.conclusion: The anchoring method provides a unified, broadly applicable mechanism to certify and reduce independent model disagreement across common ML paradigms, with results that extend to multi-dimensional, strongly convex loss settings.
